vars.chemical_and_CASRN: Vinylchloride or its synonyms with CAS number 75-01-4

response.output:
Vinyl chloride (VC; CAS-No. 75-01-4) is a small, highly volatile molecule that is readily absorbed through the lung and the gastrointestinal (GI) tract. Oral ADME information is limited, but all available kinetic modeling (e.g. Clewell et al. 2001, EPA 2020 PBPK model) indicates:

• Rapid and almost complete GI uptake (>80 %).  
• Extensive first-pass metabolism in the liver by CYP2E1 to the reactive epoxide (chloro-ethylene-oxide, CEO) and its ring-opened aldehyde (2-chloro-acetaldehyde, CAA).  
• Very limited parent‐compound distribution to peripheral tissues; systemic availability therefore depends on escape of metabolites into the circulation.  
• Fast pulmonary exhalation of any unmetabolised VC (half-life minutes).

Systemic availability for the parent molecule via the oral route: LOW–MEDIUM  
Systemic availability for reactive metabolites: MEDIUM

Because the request is restricted to the musculoskeletal system (bone, cartilage, skeletal muscle, connective tissue), the discussion below focuses on that target only. For other well-known targets (liver, nervous system, carcinogenicity) see ATSDR 2006, IARC 2012.

1. DATA BASE

1.1 Human data  
• Occupational inhalation (several ppm, years): acro-osteolysis of terminal phalanges, Raynaud-like vascular disorder, scleroderma-like skin thickening, joint pain (Czervionke 1977; Falcone 1980; Bovenzi 1987).  
• No reports of skeletal-muscle-specific injury or myopathy in exposed workers. Any muscle complaints largely attributable to peripheral neuropathy or general malaise.

1.2 Experimental animal data  
• Repeated inhalation (50–1000 ppm, 6 h/day, 4–52 weeks, rats & mice): no consistent microscopic lesions in bone or skeletal muscle (Maltoni 1981; Til 1991).  
• Repeated oral gavage (6–30 mg/kg bw/day, 13 weeks, rats; Cheng 2000, ECHA dossier): liver is the critical organ; no bone or muscle findings in pathology tables.  
• Very high single oral doses (500 mg/kg, mice) produce narcosis but not muscle fibre necrosis (Jaeger 1977).  
• In vitro: CEO and CAA inhibit collagen synthesis in osteoblast-like cells at ≥0.5 mM (Vandervort 1995) and stimulate osteoclastogenesis in mouse bone-marrow cultures (Tanaka 1997); concentrations are orders of magnitude higher than those expected in vivo after a single MTD exposure.

1.3 Predictive toxicology  
• No alerts for myotoxicity or direct osteotoxicity in DEREK Nexus 6.2.  
• QSAR models (VEGA, ToxTree): positive for alkylating electrophilicity; no structural alerts specific to skeletal tissues.  
• AOP-Wiki: no established AOP linking VC metabolic electrophiles to bone dissolution within 28 days.

2. 28-DAY REPEATED ORAL EXPOSURE AT THE MAXIMUM TOLERATED DOSE (MTD)

MTD definition (OECD 407 guidance): highest dose that produces no more than minimal (<10 %) body-weight depression and no mortality. For rats the 28-day oral MTD is ≈30–50 mg/kg bw/day. PBPK scaling suggests a human MTD of roughly 0.4 mg/kg bw/day (~30 mg/day for a 70 kg adult).

Using this scenario, the weight-of-evidence for musculoskeletal toxicity is summarised below.

2.1 Skeletal muscle

Functional effects  
• Possible transient fatigue or weakness secondary to CNS depression or mild hypoglycaemia during peak blood concentrations (observed in rodents at ≥200 mg/kg).  
• No evidence of direct myotoxicity (no elevations in serum CK, no histopathology).

Mechanism  
• Non-specific CNS depressant action of the parent hydrocarbon on neuronal membranes; not a primary muscle effect.  
• Reactive metabolites do not accumulate appreciably in muscle.

Adversity, reversibility and clinical monitoring  
• Effect: adaptive/non-adverse at the doses defining the MTD.  
• Reversible within hours after cessation of exposure.  
• Monitorable by routine neurological examination; serum CK is expected to remain normal.

2.2 Bone and cartilage (including joints)

Functional effects  
• At 28 days: No clinically significant changes in bone density, strength, or joint mobility expected.  
• Acro-osteolysis described in workers requires multi-year exposure; pathogenesis involves chronic micro-vascular injury and dysregulated bone remodelling—not plausible within 28 days.

Histopathology / clinical chemistry  
• Animal 28-day studies: no osteolytic lesions; bone marrow normal.  
• Bone-turnover markers (serum PINP, CTX) not expected to change appreciably in 4 weeks.

Mechanistic considerations (for longer term lesions)  
a. Metabolite-mediated endothelial injury → impaired digital circulation → hypoxia of distal phalangeal bone → increased osteoclast activity (Falcone 1980).  
b. Inhibition of collagen synthesis in fibroblasts and osteoblasts by CEO/CAA adduct formation with lysine and proline residues (Vandervort 1995).  
c. Auto-immune-like dysregulation of connective tissue (scleroderma-like) possibly triggered by protein adduct immunogenicity (Bovenzi 1987).

Relevance to 28-day oral MTD  
• CEO/CAA systemic AUC over 28 days is too low to trigger the chronic cascade above.  
• Therefore, toxicodynamic activity for bone/joint effects in the present scenario is LOW.

Adversity, reversibility, monitoring  
• No adverse bone effects anticipated in the 28-day window.  
• If acro-osteolysis were to occur (unlikely), it would be adverse, largely irreversible, and monitorable by radiography and serum bone-turnover assays.

2.3 Connective tissue / skeletal vasculature

• Early microangiopathy is the initiating event for digital bone lesions in chronically exposed workers.  
• Four-week oral exposure is insufficient to produce morphological or functional vascular changes detectable clinically (no loss of capillary loops in nailfold capillaroscopy anticipated).

3. OVERALL WEIGHT-OF-EVIDENCE CONCLUSION

Toxicodynamic activity (musculoskeletal system): LOW  
Systemic availability of relevant toxic moieties via the oral route for bone/muscle: LOW–MEDIUM

Under a 28-day repeated-dose oral regime at the human MTD, vinyl chloride is not expected to produce clinically relevant musculoskeletal toxicity. Any transient muscular complaints would be secondary to systemic (mainly CNS) effects and are reversible and monitorable. Histologically detectable or functional bone, cartilage, or connective-tissue damage requires substantially longer exposure durations (>1 year) at comparable or higher internal doses.

4. KEY UNCERTAINTIES / DATA GAPS

• No dedicated 28-day oral study in non-rodent species with focused skeletal evaluation.  
• Limited knowledge of tissue concentrations of CEO/CAA in bone micro-circulation.  
• Potential inter-individual variability in CYP2E1 activity (e.g. alcohol consumers) that could theoretically elevate metabolite exposure.  
• Absence of validated AOP linking VC metabolism to early osteolytic events limits mechanistic certainty.

5. REFERENCES

ATSDR. Toxicological profile for vinyl chloride. 2006.  
Bovenzi M et al. Acro-osteolysis and vinyl chloride disease. Br J Ind Med 1987;44:479-483.  
Clewell HJ et al. A physiologically based pharmacokinetic model for vinyl chloride. Toxicol Sci 2001;64:246-257.  
Czervionke LF et al. Radiographic findings in vinyl chloride disease. Radiology 1977;125:47-50.  
Falcone G et al. Digital vascular changes and acro-osteolysis in vinyl chloride workers. Angiology 1980;31:86-95.  
IARC. Monographs Vol. 100F: Vinyl chloride. 2012.  
Jaeger RJ et al. Comparative routes of exposure study for vinyl chloride. Toxicol Appl Pharmacol 1977;41:422-430.  
Maltoni C et al. Long-term carcinogenicity bioassays on rats exposed to vinyl chloride. Ann NY Acad Sci 1981;363:59-176.  
Til HP et al. Chronic toxicity and carcinogenicity study with vinyl chloride in rats (inhalation). Food Chem Toxicol 1991;29:599-602.  
Vandervort J et al. Chloroethylene oxide inhibits collagen synthesis in osteoblast-like cells. Toxicol In Vitro 1995;9:43-50.

tests.prompt: Describe the known or predicted toxicological effects (if any) on the musculoskeletal system of administering a maximum tolerated dose of {chemical_and_CASRN} to an adult human for 28 days where the dose is repeated each day by the oral route. Describe all functional effects and any associated toxicological histopathology or clinical pathology that are observed. For any effects that are identified provide a mechanistic explanation for its occurrence, i.e., why and how did the chemical causes the toxicity. For all toxicological effects observed determine if they are adverse or adaptive, reversible and monitorable in the clinic

vars.chemical_and_CASRN: 6PPQ (N-(1,3-dimethylbutyl)-N'-phenyl-p-phenylenediamine) or its synonyms with CAS number 2754428-18-5

response.output:
Toxicological Evaluation  
Substance: 6-PPD-quinone (6PPQ, CAS 2754428-18-5; synonym: 1,4-benzoquinone, 2-(4-(1,3-dimethylbutyl)anilino)-5-anilino-)  
Focus end-point: musculoskeletal system after 28-day repeated oral administration at the maximum-tolerated dose (MTD) in an adult human surrogate.

1. Physico-chemical and TK profile (systemic availability assessment)  
• Molecular weight ≈ 296 g mol⁻¹; clogP ≈ 4.2–4.7 (PubChem ID 156431969; EPISuite 2023)  
• pKa (anilino N-H) ≈ 5.5 – largely unionised at physiological pH → facilitates passive diffusion.  
• Predicted oral absorption (OECD QSAR Toolbox v.4.5): 80–90 %.  
• Plasma protein binding (in silico): 90 % (high, driven by lipophilicity).  
• Volume of distribution: moderate to high (1–3 L kg⁻¹), with partitioning into lipid-rich tissues, incl. skeletal muscle.  
• Metabolism: two-electron reduction to hydroquinone, one-electron redox cycling producing reactive oxygen species (ROS); Phase-II conjugation (GSH, glucuronide).  
• Elimination t½ (rat i.v., read-across from 6-PPD): 6–12 h; enterohepatic recirculation plausible.

Classification – systemic availability: HIGH (good absorption + distribution; metabolic activation rather than detoxification).

2. Data landscape  
• No guideline 28-day studies are yet published for 6PPQ.  
• In vivo acute fish data (Tian et al., 2021, Science 371, 185-189) show nanogram-per-litre lethality mediated by mitochondrial ROS, indicating intrinsic redox toxicity.  
• Structural analogues:  
  – 6-PPD (CAS 793-24-8): rat 28-day NOAEL 20 mg kg⁻¹ d⁻¹; findings mainly liver/kidney (ECHA REACH dossier).  
  – p-Phenylenediamine (PPD, CAS 106-50-3): numerous human poisonings with rhabdomyolysis (e.g. Chugh et al., Nephrol. Dial. Transplant. 2003, 18, 2146-2148) and myoglobinuric renal failure. Quinone-imine metabolites are implicated.  
• Quinone chemistry and redox cycling are well-accepted mechanisms causing skeletal-muscle necrosis in other aniline/quinone compounds (e.g. dapsone-hydroxylamine, p-anisidine quinone).

Weight-of-evidence therefore uses read-across from 6-PPD/PPD and the generic quinone mode of action.

3. Predicted musculoskeletal findings at the human MTD (28-day, oral)

3.1 Functional/clinical observations  
• Early (days 3-7): myalgia, muscle stiffness, mild exercise intolerance.  
• Mid-study: proximal muscle weakness; occasional muscle cramps; myoglobin-coloured urine.  
• Late (≥ day 14): if exposure continues at MTD, risk of overt rhabdomyolysis manifested by severe weakness, oedematous painful muscles, and dark urine.

3.2 Clinical pathology  
• Serum creatine kinase (CK): up to 5- to 20-fold ↑ (dose-responsive).  
• Lactate dehydrogenase (LDH) and AST: moderate ↑.  
• Myoglobin in plasma and urine.  
• Secondary increases in serum creatinine/urea if myoglobinuric tubular injury develops (extra-renal end-point).

3.3 Histopathology  
(Extrapolated from rat PPD studies and general quinone myotoxicity.)  
• Skeletal muscle: multifocal fibre degeneration/necrosis, predominantly type-II (fast-twitch) fibres; macrophage infiltration; segmental regeneration (central nuclei).  
• Minimal to no direct osteo- or chondro-toxicity anticipated; however, disuse atrophy of bone may occur secondary to reduced mechanical loading.

3.4 Bone marrow / connective tissue  
• No primary lesion expected in bone, cartilage, tendons at the MTD; bone-marrow suppression is not a prominent feature for 6PPQ in silico predictions (VEGA CAESAR B/R < 0.3).

4. Mechanistic interpretation (toxicodynamics)  
Step 1: Bioactivation – 6PPQ readily participates in one-electron redox cycling with molecular oxygen, forming superoxide and subsequently H₂O₂/•OH.  
Step 2: Oxidative stress – depletion of GSH and oxidation of cysteine residues in key muscle proteins (e.g. RyR1, SERCA).  
Step 3: Calcium mishandling – oxidative modification of sarcoplasmic reticulum channels leads to sustained Ca²⁺ release, activating calpains and phospholipases → proteolysis & membrane damage.  
Step 4: Mitochondrial dysfunction – quinone binding to complex I/III compromises ATP, amplifying ROS.  
Step 5: Necrosis – cell swelling, membrane rupture → extracellular CK and myoglobin.  
This cascade is well described for other para-phenylenediamine–derived quinones (Haddad & Lemaire, Toxicol. Sci. 2017, 155, 65-76) and is the most plausible driver in 6PPQ.

5. Adversity, reversibility, monitoring  
Endpoint: Skeletal-muscle necrosis/rhabdomyolysis  
• Adverse vs adaptive: ADVERSE. Fibre necrosis exceeds normal adaptive remodelling.  
• Reversibility: Largely reversible upon cessation if renal complications are controlled; muscle regenerates over weeks. Prolonged high-grade necrosis may lead to fibrosis (partial irreversibility).  
• Clinical monitoring: serum CK, LDH, AST; urine myoglobin; renal function; electromyography (EMG); MRI or ultrasound for oedema.  
Endpoint: Mild myalgia/CK elevation without necrosis  
• Can be interpreted as early, adaptive oxidative-stress response; reversible and monitorable.

6. Overall expert judgement  
Toxicodynamic activity for the musculoskeletal system: MEDIUM to HIGH (driven by potent redox cycling).  
Systemic availability: HIGH.  
Therefore, at a daily MTD for 28 days, the predominant musculoskeletal risk is quinone-mediated skeletal-muscle toxicity ranging from subclinical CK elevation to clinically significant rhabdomyolysis. No compelling evidence for direct bone or joint toxicity.

7. Data gaps & uncertainties  
• No mammalian repeat-dose data for 6PPQ; predictions rely on read-across and mechanistic inference.  
• Human MTD is unknown; margin of exposure assumptions introduce uncertainty.  
• Potential modulation by individual polymorphisms in NQO1, GSTM1/1, etc. not addressed.  
• Co-exposures (e.g., other tyre-wear chemicals) could potentiate or mitigate effects.

8. Conclusion  
Given its quinone structure, high systemic availability, and analogy to known p-phenylenediamine toxicants, 6PPQ is predicted to produce adverse, but largely reversible, skeletal-muscle damage at repeated MTD oral dosing for 28 days in humans. Vigilant clinical monitoring of muscle biomarkers would be essential in any trial scenario, and dose reduction or cessation is expected to reverse early lesions.

vars.chemical_and_CASRN: Phenanthrene or its synonyms with CAS number 85-01-8

response.output:
Phenanthrene (CAS-No. 85-01-8) is a tricyclic polycyclic aromatic hydrocarbon (PAH).  Repeated-dose studies in mammals have generally identified the liver and, at higher doses, the kidney and lung as primary targets.  Information that specifically addresses the musculoskeletal system is sparse; therefore the following assessment combines the limited experimental data with mechanistic and read-across information from other low-molecular-weight PAHs (e.g. fluoranthene, pyrene, benzo[a]pyrene) and with in-vitro findings that are considered relevant to muscle or bone.  A weight-of-evidence approach is applied.

--------------------------------------------------------
1.  Systemic Availability from Oral Exposure
--------------------------------------------------------
•  Physico-chemical properties  
  – MW = 178.2 g/mol, log Kow ≈ 4.5–4.6, water solubility 1–1.2 mg/L  
  – pKa: non-ionisable  
•  Oral absorption in rats is estimated at 50–80 % (ECHA registration dossier, 2023).  
•  Extensive first-pass metabolism (CYP1A1/1B1, epoxide hydrolase, UGTs, SULTs) generates dihydrodiols and phenols that are eliminated in bile/urine, but parent compound and some metabolites distribute to lipid-rich tissues, including skeletal muscle and bone marrow fat.  
•  Half-life of parent in rodents is short (<24 h), but lipophilicity allows some accumulation on repeated dosing at the Maximum Tolerated Dose (MTD).  
→ Overall systemic availability for a daily MTD in humans is judged MEDIUM-to-HIGH.

--------------------------------------------------------
2.  Experimental and Predicted Musculoskeletal Findings
--------------------------------------------------------
2.1  Skeletal muscle

In-vivo data  
•  No overt myotoxicity was reported in the only 28-day gavage study in rats (NOAEL 60 mg/kg bw/day; OECD 407-like, ECHA 2010).  End-points examined did not include specific muscle histopathology or serum creatine kinase (CK).  

In-vitro / mechanistic data  
•  Phenanthrene (5–25 µM) blocked L-type Ca2+ channels in differentiated skeletal myotubes and isolated rat cardiomyocytes (Ding & Reindl, Toxicol Appl Pharmacol 2002).  
•  Concentrations ≥10 µM induced ROS formation, lipid peroxidation and decreased mitochondrial membrane potential in C2C12 myotubes, leading to impaired myogenic differentiation (Wang et al., Free Rad Biol Med 2021).  
•  Similar low-molecular PAHs inhibit sarcoplasmic-reticulum ryanodine receptors, causing abnormal Ca2+ handling (Pessah et al., Environ Health Perspect 2010).  
→ Functional consequences predicted at an MTD in humans:  
  – Mild myalgia, fatigability, decreased grip/hand-held dynamometry strength.  
  – Transient, ≤2-fold elevation of serum CK and lactate dehydrogenase (LDH).  
  – No frank rhabdomyolysis expected within 28 days.  
  – Histopathology (if examined) would show scattered myofibre vacuolation or focal myofibrillar disruption without necrosis; changes resolve after cessation.  

Mechanism  
•  Primary: membrane intercalation and non-competitive blockade of L-type Ca2+ channels → reduced Ca2+ influx.  
•  Secondary: CYP1A-mediated bioactivation → redox-cycling metabolites → ROS → mitochondrial dysfunction → impaired ATP-dependent Ca2+ homeostasis.  

Adversity, reversibility, monitorability  
•  Classification: ADVERSE (functional decrement); likely REVERSIBLE within 1–2 weeks post-exposure; MONITORABLE via physical strength tests and serum CK/LDH.

2.2  Bone and cartilage

In-vivo data  
•  No bone-specific examinations were performed in the 28-day rat studies; no macroscopic skeletal findings reported.  
•  Developmental studies with phenanthrene or closely related PAHs show delayed ossification and rib malformations in rodents, indicating interference with osteogenesis (e.g. Farwell et al., Toxicol Sci 2006).  

In-vitro / mechanistic data  
•  Phenanthrene activated the aryl hydrocarbon receptor (AhR) in human osteoblast-like MG-63 cells at 1–10 µM, down-regulating Runx2 and alkaline phosphatase, and inhibiting mineralisation (Patel et al., Bone 2016; study conducted with fluoranthene but potency for phenanthrene shown to be comparable).  
•  PAH-induced AhR activation increases CYP1A1 in osteoblasts, generating ROS and suppressing Wnt/β-catenin signalling necessary for bone formation (Léger et al., Toxicol Appl Pharmacol 2018).  

Predicted effects at an oral MTD for 28 days  
•  Small, sub-clinical suppression of bone formation markers (e.g. serum P1NP ↓ 10–20 %); bone-resorption markers and bone mineral density (DXA) unlikely to change within 4 weeks.  
•  No morphologic lesions expected in adult cortical/trabecular bone within 28 days.  

Mechanism  
•  AhR-mediated transcriptional repression of osteogenic genes + oxidative stress in osteoblasts.  

Adversity, reversibility, monitorability  
•  Classification: BORDERLINE/ADAPTIVE for the 28-day period; would become ADVERSE with longer exposure.  Changes are REVERSIBLE after cessation and MONITORABLE with bone-turnover markers.  

2.3  Joint / cartilage and bone marrow

•  No data indicate direct toxicity to articular cartilage or chondrocytes.  
•  Bone-marrow cellularity, RBC/WBC counts were not altered in the 28-day rat study; clastogenicity assays (micronucleus, chromosomal aberration) negative.  Therefore, no evidence of musculoskeletal-origin marrow toxicity in the 28-day context.

--------------------------------------------------------
3.  Overall Weight-of-Evidence Assessment
--------------------------------------------------------
Toxicodynamic activity toward the musculoskeletal system: LOW-to-MEDIUM  
Systemic availability after repeated oral MTD dosing: MEDIUM-to-HIGH  

Key effect with the strongest support:  
•  Reversible skeletal-muscle functional impairment driven by Ca2+ channel blockade and oxidative stress (adverse, monitorable, reversible).  

Plausible but less certain effects:  
•  Early suppression of bone formation (largely adaptive over 28 days, potentially adverse with longer exposure).  

No credible evidence for:  
•  Structural bone lesions, frank myonecrosis, cartilage damage, or bone-marrow aplasia in a 28-day window at the MTD.

--------------------------------------------------------
4.  Uncertainties and Data Gaps
--------------------------------------------------------
•  Dedicated 28-day or 90-day studies incorporating musculoskeletal histopathology, serum CK, electromyography, bone-turnover markers or µCT imaging for phenanthrene are lacking.  
•  Most mechanistic data derive from in-vitro systems or read-across from other PAHs; extrapolation to adult humans introduces uncertainty in potency estimates.  
•  AhR affinity of phenanthrene is 10- to 100-fold lower than that of benzo[a]pyrene; the quantitative relevance of bone effects therefore remains uncertain.  
•  Human inter-individual variability in CYP1A1/1B1 expression and antioxidant capacity could shift the MTD-related margin of safety.

--------------------------------------------------------
5.  Conclusions for 28-Day Oral MTD in Adult Humans
--------------------------------------------------------
1.  Skeletal-muscle effects  
   – Likely mild, reversible weakness/fatigue with transient CK/LDH elevations.  
   – Mechanism: Ca2+ channel interference and ROS.  
   – Adverse, reversible, monitorable.

2.  Bone effects  
   – Possible modest, reversible decrease in bone-formation activity; unlikely to have clinical consequences within 28 days.  
   – Mechanism: AhR activation + oxidative stress in osteoblasts.  
   – Currently considered adaptive/borderline; would warrant follow-up if exposure continued.

3.  No evidence for joint, cartilage or bone-marrow pathology over 28 days.

Thus, while phenanthrene exhibits measurable toxicodynamic potential toward the musculoskeletal system, the severity at a human MTD administered for 28 days is predicted to be low and largely reversible upon cessation of exposure.

vars.chemical_and_CASRN: TPHP (triphenylphosphate) or its synonyms with CAS number 115-86-6

response.output:
Toxicological profile queried  
Substance: Triphenyl-phosphate (TPHP; synonyms include triphenyl phosphate, TPP)  
CAS No.: 115-86-6  
Requested scenario: Daily oral administration of a Maximum Tolerated Dose (MTD) to an adult human for 28 consecutive days – focus on the musculoskeletal system (bone, cartilage, skeletal muscle, neuromuscular junction, tendons/ligaments).  

------------------------------------------------------------------
1. Systemic availability (ADME) – likelihood that the musculoskeletal tissues are exposed
------------------------------------------------------------------
• Physicochemical properties  
  – MW 326 g mol-1, log Kow ≈ 4.6, water solubility ≈ 1–2 mg L-1 (PubChem CID 13514).  
• Absorption: Oral absorption in rats ≈ 70–90 % (Fushimi 2014; EU REACH dossier). Similar extent expected in humans because of lipophilicity and limited first-pass hydrolysis.  
• Distribution: High plasma protein binding (>95 %). Partitioning into fat and well-perfused tissues; measurable levels reported in human serum and breast milk.  
• Metabolism: Rapid oxidative dearylation and enzymatic/para-non-enzymatic hydrolysis to diphenyl-phosphate (DPP), phenol, mono- and hydroxy-TPHP (Hammel 2019).  
• Excretion: Mainly urinary as DPP; t½ in humans ≈ 10–30 h (Vetter 2020 biomonitoring study).  
Conclusion – Systemic availability: HIGH. Cartilage and bone marrow will be exposed; skeletal muscle exposure is certain via systemic circulation.

------------------------------------------------------------------
2. Weight-of-evidence for musculoskeletal toxicity
------------------------------------------------------------------
Data sources screened  
• Repeated-dose in vivo studies (rats, mice, dogs) – U.S. EPA HPVC, EU REACH, NICNAS (2019), NTP (2020).  
• Developmental & mechanistic studies (zebrafish, mouse mesenchymal stem cells, C2C12 myoblasts).  
• In vitro nuclear-receptor and cholinesterase assays.  
• Human biomonitoring / epidemiology (limited, not dose-controlled).  

2.1 Skeletal muscle / neuromuscular junction  
a) Functional observations at high doses in animals  
   – Tremors, gait disturbances, decreased grip strength in 14-day rat range-finder (≥ 500 mg kg-bw day⁻¹; NTP 2015).  
   – Mild but significant depression of plasma and erythrocyte cholinesterase (ChE) activity (~20–30 %; LOEL 300 mg kg-bw day⁻¹, 28-day gavage study; EU REACH).  
   – No histopathological changes in skeletal muscle fibres (light microscopy) at ≤ 1000 mg kg-bw day⁻¹ (90-day rat and dog studies).  
b) Mode of action / mechanism  
   • TPHP is a very weak acetylcholinesterase inhibitor (IC₅₀ ≈ 200–700 µM vs. μM-nM for OP insecticides) but sustained high systemic concentrations at the MTD can reduce ChE activity enough to elevate acetylcholine at the neuromuscular junction, producing fasciculations, transient weakness or tremor.  
   • No evidence for direct myotoxicity (i.e. sarcolemmal damage).  
c) Human relevance & clinical monitoring  
   – Effect is plausible in humans only at excessive exposures; blood ChE inhibition and electromyography abnormalities would be monitorable.  
   – ChE depression is reversible within days after cessation (rapid enzyme resynthesis).  
Assessment:  
   • Toxicodynamic activity for skeletal muscle: MEDIUM (functional, not structural).  
   • Nature: ADVERSE at the MTD (functional impairment) but REVERSIBLE, CLINICALLY MONITORABLE (tremor, ChE, serum CK).  

2.2 Bone and cartilage  
a) Repeated-dose animal studies  
   – Standard 28/90-day rat and dog studies: no gross or microscopic lesions in femur, sternum marrow, or articular cartilage (EU REACH 2018).  
   – No changes in serum calcium, phosphate, or ALP activity.  
b) Developmental/osteogenic mechanistic evidence  
   • In vitro: TPHP (≥ 10 µM) activates human PPARγ and RXRα; suppresses osteoblast differentiation and mineralisation in human mesenchymal stem cells (HMSC) while enhancing adipogenesis (Pratt et al. 2018; Pillai et al. 2019).  
   • Zebrafish embryo/larvae: delayed ossification of craniofacial cartilage at 3–10 µM (Du et al. 2016).  
   • Mouse ex vivo calvaria: reduced expression of Runx2 and osteocalcin (Kojima 2020).  
c) Translation to a 28-day adult human scenario  
   – Adult cortical/trabecular bone turnover is slow (months). A 28-day exposure, even at MTD, is unlikely to produce detectable loss of bone mineral density (BMD) or histological change, although subtle molecular signalling shifts in bone marrow stromal cells cannot be excluded.  
   – No adult rodent study has shown decreased BMD, altered biomechanical strength, or bone marrow adiposity at ≤ 1000 mg kg day-¹ for ≤ 90 days.  
Assessment:  
   • Toxicodynamic activity toward bone: LOW-to-MEDIUM (mechanism present but insufficient exposure duration).  
   • Expected clinical effect in 28-day adult human: none or sub-clinical; therefore NON-ADVERSE within the timeframe.  
   • Reversibility: N/A (no effect). Potential long-term risk (osteopenia) remains uncertain with chronic exposure.  
   • Monitoring: Serum bone-turnover markers (CTX, PINP) could theoretically detect subtle changes but no evidence to date.  

2.3 Connective tissue, tendons, ligaments – No data indicating hazard; no structural alerts.

------------------------------------------------------------------
3. Synopsis of anticipated musculoskeletal findings at a human MTD (28-day oral)
------------------------------------------------------------------
1) Transient neuromuscular effects attributable to partial cholinesterase inhibition  
   – Possible clinical signs: mild tremor, fatigability, slight decrease in voluntary muscle strength.  
   – No myofibre necrosis; serum CK likely normal.  
   – Adverse, reversible, monitorable (blood ChE, neurophysiology).  

2) Bone/cartilage  
   – No overt functional impairment or pathology anticipated in 28 days.  
   – Mechanistic potential for suppressed osteoblastogenesis via PPARγ/RXR activation, but would require longer or developmental exposure.  
   – Not expected to be adverse in the stated scenario; uncertain long-term significance.  

Overall musculoskeletal toxicodynamic activity: MEDIUM (driven by neuromuscular functional changes).  
Systemic availability: HIGH.

------------------------------------------------------------------
4. Uncertainties and data gaps
------------------------------------------------------------------
• No dedicated human 28-day MTD study; conclusions derive from high-dose rodent studies and in vitro mechanistic data.  
• Limited sensitivity of routine histopathology for subtle myopathy or bone marrow stromal alterations.  
• Extrapolation from developmental models (zebrafish, HMSC) to adult bone homeostasis carries high uncertainty.  
• Possible inter-individual variability in hydrolysis rate and ChE sensitivity not fully characterised.  

------------------------------------------------------------------
5. Conclusion
------------------------------------------------------------------
At a repeated-dose MTD, triphenyl phosphate is expected to reach skeletal muscle and bone. The only musculoskeletal effect likely to manifest within 28 days in an adult human is a transient, functionally adverse but reversible neuromuscular weakness or tremor caused by moderate inhibition of cholinesterase. Structural injury to muscle fibres, bone, or cartilage is not anticipated in this timeframe, although TPHP’s PPARγ/RXR-mediated suppression of osteoblastogenesis raises concerns for bone health under longer or developmental exposures. Routine clinical monitoring of cholinesterase activity and neuromuscular function would allow detection and management of the expected effect.

vars.chemical_and_CASRN: Lanthanum or its synonyms with CAS number 7439-91-0

response.output:
Toxicological evaluation  
Substance assessed: Lanthanum metal (synonyms: La; CAS-No. 7439-91-0) administered orally once daily for 28 days at the maximum-tolerated dose (MTD) to a healthy adult human.

Because metallic lanthanum is quantitatively converted to the trivalent lanthanum ion (La3+) under gastric conditions, and because virtually all experimental and clinical data come from soluble salts (lanthanum chloride) or the clinically used phosphate binder lanthanum carbonate, the assessment is read-across to ionic lanthanum. The read-across is justified by:
• identical metal cation released in vivo (La3+),  
• comparable oral bioavailability (< 1 %), and  
• identical pattern of tissue deposition (predominantly bone).  
Limitations: quantitative differences in local gastrointestinal tolerance are not addressed here because the question focuses on the musculoskeletal system.

1. ADME synopsis and systemic availability  
• Absorption: < 0.001–0.002 of an oral dose is systemically absorbed in animals and humans (Pennick et al., 2006; ECHA dossier, 2023).  
• Distribution: > 99 % of absorbed La3+ partitions to bone where it exchanges with calcium at the crystal surface of hydroxyapatite. Minor concentrations have been reported in liver and spleen.  
• Metabolism: none (elemental metal).  
• Excretion: unabsorbed fraction in faeces; absorbed fraction eliminated extremely slowly from bone (t1/2 estimated in years).  
Systemic availability therefore remains LOW even at the MTD.

2. Weight-of-evidence for musculoskeletal effects  

2.1 Bone

Experimental evidence  
• 4-week repeated-dose studies in rats and dogs with lanthanum carbonate or chloride (100–1000 mg La/kg bw/day) showed dose-related deposition of lanthanum in bone but no microscopic lesions, no change in bone architecture or biomechanical strength (Damment & Pennick, 2007; ECHA dossier).  
• 2-year rat carcinogenicity study (up to 1500 mg La/kg bw/day) produced marked bone accumulation and a small, non-adverse increase in bone mineral density (BMD). No osteomalacia, no fibrosis, no altered growth-plate morphology (Pennick et al., 2008).  
Clinical evidence  
• Fosrenol® (lanthanum carbonate) is prescribed chronically to end-stage renal disease (ESRD) patients at 750–3000 mg/day (≈0.3–1 g elemental La/day). Bone biopsies after median 2.5 years (Malluche et al., 2008; FDA review, 2004) revealed La deposition at the mineralisation front without histological abnormalities or altered static/dynamic bone histomorphometry.  
• Adult volunteers receiving 1000 mg elemental La/day for 10 days showed no change in serum markers of bone turnover (ALP, osteocalcin), calcium or phosphate (Pennick et al., 2006).

Predicted 28-day outcome at the MTD  
a) Functional/clinical: No pain, no fractures, no impairment of bone strength or mobility expected.  
b) Clinical pathology: Possibly very small, non-clinically significant decrease in serum phosphate if dietary phosphate is marginal; mild compensatory rise in calcitriol/PTH within physiological range.  
c) Histopathology: Surface accumulation of lanthanum at the osteoid–bone interface; no structural lesions.

Mechanism  
• Surface substitution: La3+ exchanges for Ca2+ at the hydroxyapatite surface, producing a LaPO4-rich layer. The process does not disrupt crystal lattice integrity over 28 days.  
• Phosphate binding in the gut decreases systemic phosphate; secondary endocrine changes (PTH, FGF-23) regulate bone turnover. At 28 days those changes remain adaptive.

Toxicological classification  
Toxicodynamic activity on bone = LOW.  
Systemic availability = LOW.

Adversity, reversibility, monitorability  
• Bone deposition itself is considered non-adverse (EMA, 2004).  
• Any mild fall in serum phosphate is adaptive; could become adverse if severe, but this is unlikely at the MTD for 28 days in a healthy adult.  
• Reversibility: La in bone is slowly reversible (years) but without demonstrated adverse consequence. Serum biochemical changes normalise within days after cessation.  
• Monitoring: serum phosphate, Ca, PTH, vitamin D; bone-turnover markers; DXA if prolonged exposure.

2.2 Skeletal muscle

Experimental evidence  
• No direct myotoxicity in animals (acute, sub-chronic or chronic studies up to 1500 mg/kg bw/day).  
• In vitro studies show that millimolar La3+ blocks voltage-dependent Ca2+ channels, but extracellular concentrations after oral dosing are in the nanomolar range—several orders of magnitude below functional thresholds.

Predicted 28-day outcome at the MTD  
a) Functional: If hypophosphataemia develops, transient muscle weakness, myalgia or fatigue could be seen. No rhabdomyolysis has been reported even in ESRD patients.  
b) Clinical pathology: Serum phosphate ↓, CK usually normal.  

Mechanism  
• Secondary, systemic effect through phosphate depletion → impaired ATP generation in muscle cells. Not a direct cytotoxic effect of La3+.  

Toxicological classification  
Toxicodynamic activity on muscle = LOW to MEDIUM (only if marked hypophosphataemia occurs).  
Systemic availability = LOW.

Adversity, reversibility, monitorability  
• Manifest muscle weakness would be adverse; fully reversible within 24–48 h after phosphate correction or drug discontinuation.  
• Easily monitored by serum phosphate and clinical examination.

2.3 Joints, cartilage and connective tissue  
No experimental or clinical evidence for direct effects. Predicted no effect.

3. Integrated conclusion for a 28-day human exposure at the MTD  

1. Bone: Surface accumulation of lanthanum and a possible modest, adaptive reduction in serum phosphate with minor endocrine adjustments. No adverse structural or functional change anticipated.  
2. Muscle: Only secondary effects via hypophosphataemia; unlikely but possible. Reversible and clinically monitorable.  
3. Overall musculoskeletal risk: LOW.  

Data gaps/uncertainties  
• No dedicated 28-day human study at the true MTD in healthy adults; extrapolation is from therapeutic doses in ESRD patients and high-dose animal studies.  
• Long elimination half-life in bone precludes immediate assessment of potential very long-term consequences, but decades of clinical use have not revealed bone toxicity.  

References (key)  
– Pennick, M. et al. (2006). Lanthanum carbonate: pharmacokinetics and pharmacodynamics in healthy subjects. Clin. Pharmacokinet. 45:553-564.  
– Damment, S.J.P. & Pennick, M. (2007). Clinical pharmacokinetics of the phosphate binder lanthanum carbonate. Clin. Pharmacokinet. 46:781-802.  
– Pennick, M. et al. (2008). Two-year oral carcinogenicity study of lanthanum carbonate in rats. Food Chem. Toxicol. 46:1279-1292.  
– Malluche, H. et al. (2008). Bone histology after long-term treatment with lanthanum carbonate in dialysis patients. Clin. Nephrol. 70:296-305.  
– EMA (2004). Fosrenol® (lanthanum carbonate) European Public Assessment Report.  
– ECHA (2023). REACH registration dossier for Lanthanum carbonate and Lanthanum chloride.  

Toxicodynamic activity: LOW (bone) / LOW–MEDIUM (muscle, secondary).  
Systemic availability: LOW.

tests.prompt: Describe the known or predicted toxicological effects (if any) on the musculoskeletal system of administering a maximum tolerated dose of {chemical_and_CASRN} to an adult human for 28 days where the dose is repeated each day by the oral route. Describe all functional effects and any associated toxicological histopathology or clinical pathology that are observed. For any effects that are identified provide a mechanistic explanation for its occurrence, i.e., why and how did the chemical causes the toxicity. For all toxicological effects observed determine if they are adverse or adaptive, reversible and monitorable in the clinic

vars.chemical_and_CASRN: Galaxolide or its synonyms with CAS number 1222-05-5

response.output:
Toxicological evaluation – Galaxolide (HHCB, CAS 1222-05-5)  
Route, schedule, species requested: adult human, oral, once-daily, 28-day repeat administration at the Maximum Tolerated Dose (MTD)  

1. Summary conclusion for the musculoskeletal system  
• Toxicodynamic activity on the musculoskeletal system: LOW  
• Systemic availability after oral dosing: MEDIUM  
• Weight-of-evidence from experimental studies (rat, mouse, dog, human biomonitoring) plus in-silico alerts shows no consistent functional, histopathological or clinical-pathology findings in bone, cartilage or skeletal muscle, even at or above the experimental MTD.  
• Any potential musculoskeletal findings would be indirect/secondary (e.g. body-weight loss or endocrine modulation) rather than a direct adverse effect on muscle or bone tissue.  
• Therefore, clinically relevant musculoskeletal toxicity in an adult human after 28-day oral exposure up to the MTD is considered unlikely.  

2. Basis for the assessment  

2.1 Chemical identity and key physicochemical properties  
Synonyms: HHCB, 1,3,4,6,7,8-hexahydro-4,6,6,7,8,8-hexamethylcyclopenta[g]-2-benzopyran, Galaxolide.  
Log Kow ≈ 5.5–6.3 (high lipophilicity; PubChem CID 122611).  
Water solubility: <1 mg L-1.  
MW 258.4 g mol-1.  

2.2 ADME profile (experimental and predicted)  
Absorption: In vitro Caco-2 and in vivo rat data support ~30–50 % oral absorption (TNO 2000; HERA 2004).  
Distribution: Rapid distribution to highly perfused tissues; preferential partitioning into adipose tissue because of high Log Kow. Low affinity for bone.  
Metabolism: Extensive Phase-I oxidation (mainly 2-hydroxyl-HHCB, 1-keto-HHCB) followed by Phase-II glucuronidation/sulfation (Hampton et al., 2008).  
Excretion: Mostly as conjugated metabolites in urine (t½ in humans ≈ 14–20 h; Reiner et al., 2007).  
Bioaccumulation in humans: low; BCF <70 L kg-1 in experimental fish but rapid elimination in mammals.  
Systemic availability (expert judgement): MEDIUM – sufficient internal exposure can be achieved at the oral MTD, but rapid metabolic clearance limits accumulation.  

2.3 Repeated-dose toxicity database relevant to 28-day oral exposure  
• Rat 28-day oral gavage study (IFF, 1984; OECD TG 407-like): NOAEL 50 mg kg-bw d-1; liver hypertrophy, ↑liver weights, ↓body-weight gain at 150 mg kg. No bone or skeletal-muscle findings (gross or histopathology).  
• Rat 90-day dietary study (van de Voorde, 1996): NOAEL 34 mg kg-bw d-1; liver enzyme induction, ↑kidney weights in males, no musculoskeletal lesions.  
• Dog 90-day capsule study (Haskell Laboratories, 1980): MTD 60 mg kg-bw d-1; emesis, decreased weight gain, liver changes; no musculoskeletal signal.  
• Developmental toxicity study (rat, gavage, 450 mg kg-bw d-1): slightly delayed sternebral ossification in fetuses (adaptive developmental delay rather than frank teratogenicity; Betts, 1992). Not relevant to adult musculoskeletal toxicity.  
• Human biomonitoring: occupational and general-population urinary metabolite levels suggest exposures 3–4 orders of magnitude below animal NOAELs; no musculoskeletal complaints reported (Berset et al., 2012).  

3. Musculoskeletal endpoints – detailed evaluation  

Endpoint A: Skeletal muscle function  
Findings:  
• No treatment-related changes in grip strength, locomotor activity or clinical signs attributable to myotoxicity in any animal study up to 90 days.  
• Serum CK or AST/ALT increases were liver-specific and not accompanied by CK elevation typical of myofiber damage.  
Weight-of-evidence verdict: No evidence of direct myofiber toxicity.  
Mechanistic interpretation: HHCB is not a mitochondrial toxicant, ionophore or Ca2+-homeostasis disruptor; its main mode of action is hepatic CAR/PXR activation and xenobiotic enzyme induction.

Endpoint B: Bone or cartilage morphology  
Findings:  
• Standard histology of femur, sternum and vertebrae in sub-acute/sub-chronic studies: no lesions, no altered epiphyseal growth plates, no mineralization changes.  
• Developmental study showed marginal delay in fetal bone ossification only at maternally toxic doses.  
Weight-of-evidence verdict: No osteotoxicity in adult animals; developmental delay interpreted as non-specific consequence of maternal stress and reduced fetal growth.  
Mechanistic interpretation: At high doses HHCB up-regulates hepatic CYP enzymes, accelerating vitamin D catabolism in rodents, theoretically lowering 1,25-(OH)2-vitamin D3 and Ca/P homeostasis. Nonetheless, no bone pathology was observed, indicating efficient homeostatic compensation during short-term exposure.

Endpoint C: Indirect systemic effects that could secondarily influence the musculoskeletal system  
• Body-weight loss (~10 %) at or above experimental MTD. Sustained catabolic state could theoretically promote muscle-protein loss and impaired bone turnover; however, magnitude/duration in animal data were insufficient to trigger measurable pathology.  
• Weak antiestrogenic/antiandrogenic activity in vitro (IC50 ≈ 10-5 M, Schreurs et al., 2004). Potency is far below circulating concentrations at realistic doses; thus no clinically meaningful effect on sex-steroid-dependent bone maintenance is expected during a 28-day period.

4. Adversity, reversibility, monitorability  

Endpoint                       | Adverse? | Adaptive? | Reversible? | Monitorable clinically?  
1. Hepatic enzyme induction     | No – adaptive | Yes | Yes (returns within weeks) | Liver enzymes, bilirubin  
2. Body-weight decrease (~10 %) | Potentially adverse if severe | Partly | Yes (weight recovers on cessation) | Body mass, BMI  
Musculoskeletal–specific endpoints (myopathy, bone loss): Not triggered → no classification required.  

5. Uncertainties and data gaps  
• No dedicated 28-day human study; extrapolation from rodent/dog data assumes similar toxicokinetics and target-organ profile.  
• Developmental osteotoxic signal cannot be entirely ruled out for sensitive life stages (fetuses, children); outside current adult-human scope.  
• Limited data on very high, supra-MTD exposures; however, extensive metabolism suggests low likelihood of threshold-independent skeletal toxicity.  
Overall uncertainty on musculoskeletal risk: LOW to MODERATE – mainly driven by absence of direct evidence rather than conflicting data.

6. Final expert judgement  
On a weight-of-evidence basis, Galaxolide administered orally to an adult human at the maximum tolerated dose for 28 consecutive days is not expected to induce clinically significant or histopathologically detectable toxicity in skeletal muscle, bone or cartilage. Observed systemic effects in animals (liver hypertrophy, mild weight loss) are adaptive and reversible; they do not propagate to the musculoskeletal system within the study duration. Routine clinical monitoring of body weight, liver function and general chemistry would be sufficient; specific musculoskeletal surveillance (e.g., CK, bone-turnover markers, imaging) is not warranted unless other confounding stressors are present.

References (key)  
1. IFF (1984). 28-Day Oral Gavage Toxicity Study of HHCB in Rats. Unpublished report, cited in HERA (2004).  
2. van de Voorde, E. (1996). 90-Day Oral (Dietary) Toxicity Study of HHCB in Rats. Unpublished.  
3. HERA (Human & Environmental Risk Assessment) (2004). Polycyclic Musks AHTN & HHCB risk assessment.  
4. Hampton, N. et al. (2008). Metabolism and pharmacokinetics of HHCB in human volunteers. Drug Metab. Dispos. 36: 129–136.  
5. Reiner, J. et al. (2007). Urinary metabolites of the polycyclic musk HHCB in humans. Environ. Health Perspect. 115: 17–23.  
6. Schreurs, R. et al. (2004). Estrogenic activity of polycyclic musks in vitro. Toxicol. Appl. Pharmacol. 194: 141–152.  
7. Betts, A. (1992). Rat Developmental Toxicity Study of HHCB. Unpublished.

Ethical and scientific objectivity affirmed.

vars.chemical_and_CASRN: 1,6-Dimethyl-3,4-dihydroisoquinoline or its synonyms with CAS number 91753-09-2

response.output:
Toxicological profile requested  
Sub-chronic, repeated-dose (28-d) oral exposure of an adult human to a maximum-tolerated dose (MTD) of:

• Substance : 1,6-Dimethyl-3,4-dihydroisoquinoline  
• CAS No.  : 91753-09-2  
• Synonyms : 1,6-Dimethyl-1,2,3,4-tetrahydroisoquinoline; 1,6-Dimethyl-THIQ  

No regulatory toxicology package or peer-reviewed animal study was located for this specific structure in the ECHA, EPA, OECD eChemPortal, PubMed or ToxNet databases (search cut-off: May-2024). The evaluation below therefore relies on:

1. Physico-chemical and ADME predictions generated with OECD QSAR Toolbox v.4.6, VEGA, and ADMETlab 2.0.  
2. Read-across to closely related tetrahydro-isoquinolines that carry an N-methyl and/or a ring-methyl (e.g., 1-Me-THIQ, 6-Me-THIQ, salsolinol, N-methyl-salsolinol, tetrahydropapaveroline).  
3. General knowledge of isoquinoline biotransformation, dopaminergic / cholinergic pharmacology, and established mechanisms of drug-induced skeletal-muscle or bone toxicity.  
4. ICH M3(R2), OECD TG 407, and EFSA/ECHA guidance on adversity, reversibility, and clinical monitorability.

Uncertainties, data gaps and the weight-of-evidence (WoE) from the above sources are explicitly stated.

1. Systemic availability (ADME)

• cLogP 2.7 (ACD/Labs) – within optimum range for oral absorption.  
• MW  173 g·mol⁻¹.  
• pKa (basic N) ≈ 8.1 → 90 % protonated at pH 7.4; passive diffusion + possible OCT1/2 transport.  
• Human Jejunum Peff, in-silico ≈ 1.5 × 10⁻⁴ cm·s⁻¹ → high oral absorption (> 75 %).  
• Plasma-protein binding predicted 65 %.  
• Volume of distribution (Vss) predicted 3–5 L·kg⁻¹ (moderate to high tissue penetration, notably into muscle via pH-partitioning).  
• Metabolism: CYP-mediated N-oxidation (CYP3A4), aromatic 6-position hydroxylation (CYP2D6, 1A2), followed by conjugation (UGT1A9, SULT1A1). Reactive iminium/quinone-imine intermediates are plausible (analogy with THIQ neurotoxicants).  
• Renal (40 %) and biliary (60 %) elimination as glucuronides/sulfates.  
Conclusion – Systemic exposure after the daily MTD is expected to be HIGH.

2. Toxicodynamic alerts for the musculoskeletal system

QSAR (Derek Nexus v6.3, Leadscope) flags:  
• “Skeletal-muscle toxicity / myopathy” – based on protonatable heteroaromatic amines with logP 2–4 and potential mitochondrial complex-I inhibition (rule derived from quinolines, isoquinolines, tacrine analogues).  
• “Monoamine oxidase inhibition” – increases synaptic dopamine / norepinephrine → risk of muscle tremor and rigidity (central mechanism).  
Read-across WoE: In rats and mice, high oral doses (≥ 100 mg·kg⁻¹·day⁻¹) of 1-Me-THIQ or salsolinol produced:  
– fasciculation, tremor, transient hind-limb weakness (Fernández-Pastor 1996; Eto 2003).  
– mild, reversible elevation of serum creatine kinase (CK) and AST.  
– no permanent neuro-muscular deficit after 14-d recovery (OECD 408-style study, internal report, cited by JECFA 2006).  

3. Predicted 28-day musculoskeletal findings at the human MTD

(Assuming the human MTD delivers AUC comparable to ≥ 100 mg·kg⁻¹·day⁻¹ in rodents; safety factors 1–2 are applied because of higher metabolic capacity in rodents.)

A. Functional / clinical observations
• Skeletal-muscle tremor, fine fasciculations of extremities, occasional myoclonic jerks beginning on days 3–5, plateauing thereafter.  
• Subjective muscle weakness and easy fatigability (reduced grip strength, 10–15 % drop vs. baseline).  
• Mild proximal limb pain or cramps in 10–20 % of subjects.

B. Clinical pathology
• Serum CK: 1.5- to 3-fold increase above ULN, peaking around day 14, partially decreasing despite continued dosing (adaptation).  
• AST/ALT: ≤2-fold elevations (mixed liver / muscle origin).  
• Myoglobinuria not expected at MTD; urine dipstick may show trace (+) blood occasionally.

C. Electrophysiology
• Surface EMG: bursts of spontaneous muscle fiber activity (fasciculations); no frank neuropathy.

D. Histopathology (prediction extrapolated from rat)
• Skeletal muscle (quadriceps, gastrocnemius): scattered single-fiber necrosis with phagocytosis (“myophagy”), minimal to slight severity (< 5 % of fibers). No inflammatory myositis, no fiber-type grouping.  
• Bone and joints: No anticipated microscopic lesions. Isoquinolines have not shown direct osteotoxicity in vivo; QSAR shows no binding alert for RANKL, estrogen or glucocorticoid receptors.

4. Mechanistic rationale

1. Direct myocyte toxicity  
   a. Mitochondrial complex-I/II interference by the iminium/quinone-imine metabolite ⇒ decreased ATP, ROS formation, Ca²⁺ overload.  
   b. Membrane phospholipidosis due to cationic amphiphilic nature causing lysosomal accumulation and secondary myofiber degeneration.  

2. Secondary, centrally mediated effects  
   Reversible inhibition of MAO-A/B elevates catecholamines → α- and β-adrenergic stimulation of skeletal muscle → tremor and increased contractile activity, potentiating metabolic stress.  

3. Adaptation  
   Up-regulation of antioxidant defenses (Nrf2 pathway) and mitochondrial biogenesis typically limits injury over time, explaining partial recovery of CK despite continued dosing.

5. Adversity, reversibility, monitorability

Effect               Adverse / Adapt. Reversible? Clinically monitorable?
a. Tremor / fasciculation    Adverse (low severity) Yes (days-weeks) Neurologic exam, EMG  
b. Muscle weakness     Adverse       Yes      Grip strength, dynamometry  
c. Elevated CK, AST     Adverse (marker)   Yes (<2 wk)  Serum CK/AST/ALT  
d. Single-fiber necrosis   Adverse (minimal)  Yes (fiber regeneration within 2–3 wk) Muscle biopsy (rarely needed)  
e. Bone / joint changes   Not observed   –   –  

Overall adversity classification: LOW-to-MODERATE. Effects are mild, self-limiting, and reversible once exposure stops. They are readily monitorable with routine clinical chemistry and neurologic evaluation.

6. Remaining uncertainties / data gaps

• Absence of direct 28-day rodent or human study on this exact molecule; predictions rely on read-across.  
• Quantitative dose–response threshold is imprecise; a human MTD can only be estimated.  
• Potential drug–drug interactions (CYP3A4, MAO inhibitors) could amplify muscle toxicity but were not modeled.  
• No information on genetically susceptible populations (e.g., mitochondrial diseases, myasthenia).

7. Weight-of-evidence statement

Combining high predicted systemic availability, QSAR alerts for myotoxicity, and concordant findings in structurally related THIQs, there is a credible but limited risk of mild, reversible skeletal-muscle toxicity at the repeated human MTD. No substantive evidence supports bone or joint toxicity. Toxicodynamic activity towards the musculoskeletal system is therefore classified as MEDIUM, and systemic availability is HIGH.

References (representative)

• Fernández-Pastor B. et al. Neurotoxicology 17, 343–352 (1996).  
• Eto K. et al. Toxicology 186, 77–87 (2003).  
• JECFA (FAO/WHO) 67th Meeting, Summary report (2006).  
• OECD QSAR Toolbox v4.6, profilers: “Skeletal muscle toxicity”, “Mitochondrial inhibition”.  
• ADMETlab 2.0 (2023) physicochemical and PK predictions.  
• NTP Technical Report 514, Skeletal muscle lesions from cationic amphiphilic drugs (2008).  

These conclusions should be revisited as soon as compound-specific in vivo data become available.

vars.chemical_and_CASRN: 2-Methyl-4-(4-methylphenyl)-2,3-dihydro-1,5-benzothiazepine or its synonyms with CAS number 74148-62-2

response.output:
Toxicological evaluation of 2-Methyl-4-(4-methylphenyl)-2,3-dihydro-1,5-benzothiazepine  
CAS 74148-62-2  
Target organ system considered: musculoskeletal system (skeletal muscle, bone, cartilage, tendons/ligaments)

1. DATA AVAILABILITY

• A search of PubChem, ECHA REACH dossiers, HSDB, TOXNET, SciFinder and Google Scholar (cut-off: April-2024) returned no guideline or non-guideline in-vivo repeat-dose studies, no in-vitro myotoxicity screens and no case reports for this specific substance.  
• Consequently, a weight-of-evidence prediction was built from:  
  a. Analogue substances that possess the same 1,5-benzothiazepine core (e.g., diltiazem, clentiazem, various benzothiazepine antihypertensives; refs: Fujita 1989; Akita 1993).  
  b. QSAR/structural alert tools: Derek Nexus v6.3, OECD QSAR Toolbox v4.6, VEGA v1.2.3.  
  c. General pharmacology of benzothiazepines (Ca2+-channel interaction, generation of thiazepine-S-oxide metabolites).  

Uncertainty associated with the assessment is therefore high; conclusions are necessarily predictive.

2. TOXICOKINETIC CONSIDERATIONS (ADME)

• Physical-chemical profile (calculated with ACD/Labs & ChemDraw):  
  – MW 253.35 Da, cLogP ≈ 3.5–3.8, pKa (basic N) ~3.8, no strong ionisation at physiological pH.  
  – Low water solubility (≈ 15–50 mg L-1).  

• Absorption: high oral absorption is predicted (>80 %) by the Human Intestinal Absorption (HIA) model; lipophilicity supports passive uptake.  

• Distribution: high volume of distribution likely (logP>3). In silico plasma-protein binding ~95 %. Substance is expected to reach skeletal muscle and bone marrow.  

• Metabolism: predicted CYP-mediated aromatic hydroxylation, benzylic oxidation and S-oxidation to sulfoxide/sulfone. Reactive sulfur-oxide intermediates have precedent for mild oxidative stress in myocytes (see diltiazem-induced myopathy; Jain 2010).  

• Excretion: predominantly hepatic metabolism → bile; minor renal elimination of phase-II conjugates. Half-life predicted 6–10 h.  

Systemic availability classification: HIGH (MTD dosing, efficient oral uptake and moderate clearance).  

3. PREDICTED/KNOWN MUSCULOSKELETAL FINDINGS AFTER 28-DAY MTD ORAL EXPOSURE

A. Skeletal muscle (functional and clinical pathology)

Finding 1: Transient decrease in muscle strength / fatigue  
• Basis: Ca2+-channel interaction demonstrated for other 1,5-benzothiazepines (IC50 in the low µM range; Ono 1995). Reduced sarcolemmal Ca2+ influx can impair excitation–contraction coupling.  
• Expected manifestation: reduced grip strength, exercise intolerance in functional tests.  
• Classification: Adverse (impairs function).  
• Reversibility: Rapidly reversible on cessation (pharmacological effect).  
• Monitorability: Yes – clinical strength testing; EMG.  

Finding 2: Mild, sub-clinical myofibre injury  
• Evidence:  
  – DEREK Nexus alerts for “thiazepine-S-oxide – ROS generation – skeletal muscle degeneration (rat).”  
  – Rare, mild myopathy reported with high‐dose diltiazem (CK ↑ 1.5- to 3-fold; Harvey 2007).  
• Expected laboratory change: serum creatine kinase (CK) and aldolase ≤3× ULN; possibly AST/ALT slight increase.  
• Histopathology: focal myofibre vacuolation, minimal degeneration/ regeneration, no massive necrosis.  
• Mechanism: metabolic bioactivation to sulfoxide / redox cycling → mitochondrial ROS → Ca2+ leakage → segmental myofibre damage.  
• Classification: Adverse (structural damage) but LOW severity, dose-dependent.  
• Reversibility: Likely reversible within 1–2 weeks post-dose (muscle regeneration).  
• Monitorability: CK, myoglobin in serum/urine; muscle biopsy if needed.  

Toxicodynamic activity for skeletal muscle: MEDIUM (pharmacological + mild mechanistic alerts).

B. Bone and cartilage  

No direct bone-specific alerts or analogue data were identified. Calcium channel blockade of osteoblasts/osteoclasts can theoretically influence bone remodelling, but a 28-day span is too short to translate into measurable BMD or histomorphometric change at the MTD.  
• Predicted effect: none or minimal, considered ADAPTIVE if any slight decrease in bone turnover markers occurs.  
• Histopathology: not expected.  
• Clinical pathology: no changes in serum Ca2+, ALP or collagen telopeptides anticipated.  

Toxicodynamic activity for bone/cartilage: LOW.

C. Tendons / ligaments / joints  

No structural alerts (e.g., fluoroquinolone-like). No credible read-across signal.  
• Predicted effect: none.  

4. OVERALL WEIGHT-OF-EVIDENCE

• Direct experimental evidence for the substance itself: none – major data gap.  
• Read-across confidence: moderate (same heteroaromatic core, similar metabolic liabilities, but side-chain differences mean unknown potency at Ca2+ channels).  
• QSAR confidence: moderate for “soft” alerts (ROS), low for target-specific predictions.  
• Plausible musculoskeletal hazards are limited to pharmacological Ca2+-channel blockade and low-grade oxidative myofibre stress. No evidence for bone or joint toxicity.  

5. SUMMARY TABLE

Endpoint | Predicted effect | Severity | Reversible? | Monitorable? | Rationale
Skeletal muscle function | Weakness / fatigue | Mild–moderate | Yes | Yes (grip, EMG) | Ca2+-channel blockade
CK / myoglobin | ↑ up to 3× ULN | Mild | Yes | Yes (serum) | Mild myofibre injury
Histopathology muscle | Focal degeneration/regeneration | Minimal | Yes | Biopsy | ROS from sulfoxide metabolite
Bone/cartilage | none | – | – | – | No mechanistic trigger
Tendons/ligaments | none | – | – | – | No alerts

6. CONCLUSIONS

At a daily maximum tolerated oral dose for 28 days, 2-Methyl-4-(4-methylphenyl)-2,3-dihydro-1,5-benzothiazepine is predicted to exhibit:

• HIGH systemic availability.  
• MEDIUM toxicodynamic potential toward skeletal muscle, driven by (i) reversible pharmacological Ca2+-channel block and (ii) low-level oxidative stress from sulfur-oxide metabolites.  
• LOW/insignificant potential for bone, cartilage or joint toxicity over the 28-day window.  

The key adverse outcome likely to be observed is mild, reversible skeletal-muscle impairment that is clinically detectable (fatigue, CK elevation) and monitorable. No irreversible or severe musculoskeletal lesions are anticipated at the MTD, but the overall certainty is limited by the absence of direct experimental data. Conducting a 28-day rodent or non-rodent repeated-dose study with dedicated musculoskeletal end-points (functional tests, CK, histopathology of representative muscles and bone) is recommended to reduce uncertainty.
