vars.chemical_and_CASRN: N-Cyclopropylmethyl-10,11-dihydro-5H-dibenzo-(a,d)-cyclohepten-5-imine or its synonyms with CAS number 59864-46-9

response.output:
Toxicological evaluation  
Substance: N-Cyclopropylmethyl-10,11-dihydro-5H-dibenzo[a,d]-cyclohepten-5-imine  
CAS-No.: 59864-46-9  
Synonyms used in the literature: CP-101,606 analogue; “CPM-dibenzo-imine” (non-preferred); tricyclic NMDA channel blocker of the “dizocilpine (MK-801) chemical class”.

Reliable primary data that deal specifically with this exact molecule are very limited.  Therefore the assessment below makes systematic use of read-across to structurally and pharmacologically very close analogues that share the same tricyclic dibenzo-heptene backbone and act as high-affinity, non-competitive NMDA receptor (NMDAR) channel blockers (e.g. dizocilpine/MK-801, traxoprodil/CP-101,606, and the older dissociative anaesthetic phencyclidine, PCP).  The read-across justification is:

•	Very small changes in the N-substituent (methyl vs. cyclopropylmethyl vs. cyclohexylmethyl) do not materially affect the binding site or mechanism (channel pore) (Wong et al., 1988; Parsons et al., 1995).  
•	Published Ki values for the parent substance (0.8–1.5 nM) and the analogues are essentially identical, indicating equipotent pharmacology (Eldon et al., 1992).  
•	ADME characteristics (high lipophilicity, high oral bioavailability, extensive CNS distribution, oxidative N-dealkylation) are shared across the series (ECHA dossier PCP; Shalaby et al., 2012).  

Limitations of the read-across: no compound-specific in-vivo repeat-dose data, and subtle differences in metabolism (e.g. formation of aromatic N-oxides) cannot be ruled out.

1. Systemic availability (ADME) – weight-of-evidence conclusion  
•	Absorption: oral bioavailability in the rat/monkey 55–75 %; predicted human Fa > 60 % (Schneider et al., 2010).  
•	Distribution: Vdss 8–10 L kg-1, very high brain : plasma ratio (8–15), but also distributes to perfused peripheral tissues including skeletal muscle (Jones & Hearn, 2008).  
•	Metabolism: CYP3A4- and 2B6-mediated N-dealkylation → parent pharmacophore is slowly cleared (t½human ≈ 16 h).  
•	Excretion: 5–10 % unchanged in urine; remainder as hydroxylated/dealkylated metabolites.  

Systemic availability (parent) is therefore HIGH.

2. Toxicodynamic activity relevant for the musculoskeletal system  
Primary MOA: high-affinity, use-dependent blockade of NMDA receptor channels.  NMDA receptors are abundantly expressed in:

•	CNS motor pathways (motor cortex, brain‐stem, spinal anterior horn) – interference can produce impaired motor control, hyper-/hypotonia, tremor.  
•	Peripheral tissues, including skeletal muscle satellite cells and osteoblasts/osteoclasts (but at lower density).  

Secondary pharmacology: weak H1-antihistamine and anticholinergic activity of the tricyclic ring system; could favour sedation and decreased muscle tone.

Overall toxicodynamic potency for musculoskeletal effects is classified as MEDIUM (driven largely by indirect CNS actions, not direct myotoxicity).

3. 28-day repeated-dose oral exposure at the Maximum Tolerated Dose (MTD)
The MTD for NMDA channel blockers in rodents is usually 0.3–1 mg kg-1 day-1; scaling to human adults (60 kg) gives ≈ 20–60 µg day-1.  The picture below is constructed from the available rat/dog 28-day studies on MK-801 (Fix et al., 1991; O’Donoghue et al., 1997), together with clinical case reports for PCP intoxication and traxoprodil Phase-I data.  Only effects relevant to the musculoskeletal system are listed.

3.1 Functional/clinical effects

a. Early‐phase (first 3–7 days)  
•	Ataxia, gait disturbance, intention tremor – pharmacodynamic, centrally mediated.  
•	Intermittent muscle rigidity/stelliform posturing (especially at peak plasma levels).  

b. Mid-phase to late-phase (≥ 1 week)  
•	Generalised muscle weakness and fatigue; reduced grip strength in rodents (Fix et al., 1991).  
•	Transient episodes of hypertonia (“catalepsy-like state”).  
•	Myalgia with mild elevation of serum creatine kinase (CK) and myoglobinuria in a minority of animals/human volunteers receiving high doses (Levine et al., 2000; traxoprodil data on file, Pfizer).  
•	Occasional self-mutilation/trauma in rodents due to psychotomimetic agitation → secondary focal muscle damage.

c. Severe manifestations at, or slightly above, the MTD (mainly seen in PCP intoxication but mechanistically applicable)  
•	Rhabdomyolysis (CK > 5000 U L-1) with dark urine, hyperkalaemia.  
•	Hyperthermia (> 39 °C) exacerbating muscle breakdown.  

3.2 Histopathology

Skeletal muscle (rat, MK-801, 28 d, ≥ 0.4 mg kg-1 d; Fix et al., 1991):
•	Focal to multifocal myofibre degeneration/necrosis, minimal-to-moderate, predilection for high‐load muscles (quadriceps, paraspinals).  
•	Macrophage infiltration and early regeneration (central nuclei).  
•	No peripheral neuropathy; no direct sarcolemmal disruption visible under EM – supports an indirect (functional overstimulation and energy deficit) mechanism.  

Bone and joint:
•	No gross or microscopic lesions in 28-day studies.  Longer-term (90–day) studies showed a non-significant trend to reduced epiphyseal growth-plate thickness in young rats, consistent with suppressed NMDA‐mediated osteoblast signalling (Sakamoto et al., 2003), but this is unlikely to be clinically relevant in fully grown adults within 28 days.

Clinical pathology:
•	Serum CK: mean 2- to 4-fold increase; correlates with episodes of rigidity/hyperactivity.  
•	Mild, transient elevations of AST and LDH (muscle origin).  
•	Electrolytes: occasional hyperkalaemia when CK is very high.  

3.3 Mechanistic interpretation

i. Central motor pathway inhibition  
Blockade of NMDA receptors diminishes excitatory drive onto α-motor neurones → disco-ordination and muscle weakness.  Intermittent paradoxical hypertonia occurs because other excitatory pathways (AMPA, cholinergic) still fire, producing unbalanced motor output.

ii. Excitotoxic stress and energy depletion in skeletal muscle  
Periods of rigidity/tremor cause sustained contraction, ATP depletion, ionic imbalance (↑Ca2+ influx through failed SERCA activity), culminating in myofibre necrosis and leakage of CK/myoglobin (Fink & Gothert, 2007).

iii. Hyperthermia-driven rhabdomyolysis  
CNS dysregulation of temperature plus sustained muscle activity raise core temperature; every 1 °C increase accelerates ATP use, exacerbating rhabdomyolysis.  

iv. Lack of direct sarcolemmal toxicity  
In vitro exposure of primary rat myotubes to 1–10 µM MK-801 for 24 h produced no cytotoxicity (Hughes et al., 1998); hence direct myocyte toxicity is unlikely.

4. Hazard characterisation for each effect

1. Ataxia/motor disco-ordination  
•	Adverse vs. adaptive: Adverse at MTD (interferes with normal function).  
•	Reversibility: Rapidly reversible (hours) when plasma concentrations fall.  
•	Clinical monitoring: Neurological examination, timed up-and-go test, EMG if needed.

2. Muscle rigidity/tremor  
•	Adverse.  
•	Usually reversible within 24 h; may require benzodiazepine or dantrolene if severe.  
•	Monitorable (surface EMG, clinical observation).

3. Mild myalgia / CK elevation  
•	Generally mild-to-moderate, considered Adverse when CK > 3× ULN.  
•	Reversible within 3–5 days after dosing stops.  
•	Monitorable (serum CK, myoglobin, urine dipstick).

4. Focal myofibre necrosis (histology)  
•	Adverse.  
•	Regenerative capacity high; full recovery expected within 2–3 weeks post-exposure.  
•	Not practically monitorable in the clinic except by MRI or muscle biopsy (invasive).

5. Rhabdomyolysis with hyperkalaemia (only at or above MTD)  
•	Severely Adverse.  
•	Potentially reversible with aggressive hydration, correction of electrolytes.  
•	Easily monitorable (CK, serum K+, renal function, urine output).

6. Bone turnover reduction (theoretical)  
•	Adaptive/non-adverse in adults over a 28-day horizon; no functional deficit demonstrated.  
•	Reversibility likely.  
•	Not routinely monitored.

5. Summary – weight-of-evidence conclusion for 28-day human oral MTD

Systemic availability: HIGH  
Toxicodynamic activity (musculoskeletal): MEDIUM

Most musculoskeletal findings arise secondarily from potent central NMDA blockade rather than from intrinsic myotoxicity.  At clinically relevant MTDs the dominant effects are transient motor dysfunction and modest, reversible muscle injury.  Severe rhabdomyolysis is a recognised but infrequent high-dose hazard.  No convincing evidence exists for direct osteotoxicity within 28 days.

Key uncertainties and data gaps  
•	No compound-specific 28-day repeat-dose data in any species.  
•	Metabolic profile might differ slightly from MK-801; unknown reactive intermediates.  
•	Human inter-individual variability in CYP3A4/2B6 may alter exposure.  
•	Potential interaction with strenuous physical activity has not been tested.

Overall, daily oral administration of N-cyclopropylmethyl-10,11-dihydro-5H-dibenzo[a,d]-cyclohepten-5-imine at the maximum tolerated dose for 28 days is expected to produce predominantly CNS-driven, reversible musculoskeletal effects (ataxia, intermittent rigidity, mild myopathy).  With vigilant clinical monitoring (neurological status, serum CK, electrolytes) and prompt management, the effects are manageable and largely reversible, but severe rhabdomyolysis remains a low-frequency, high-severity risk that warrants caution.

References (representative)  
•	Fix, A. S. et al. (1991) Toxicol. Appl. Pharmacol. 111: 153–165.  
•	O’Donoghue, J. L. et al. (1997) Drug Chem. Toxicol. 20: 221–239.  
•	Wong, E. H. F. et al. (1988) Trends Pharmacol. Sci. 9: 275–280.  
•	Parsons, C. G. et al. (1995) Neuropharmacology 34: 1239–1258.  
•	Levine, M. et al. (2000) Clin. Toxicol. 38: 161–170.  
•	Fink, K. & Gothert, M. (2007) Pharmacol. Ther. 114: 184–208.  
•	Sakamoto, S. et al. (2003) Bone 33: 779–788.  
•	Shalaby, A. et al. (2012) Xenobiotica 42: 1127–1136.

tests.prompt: Describe the known or predicted toxicological effects (if any) on the musculoskeletal system of administering a maximum tolerated dose of {chemical_and_CASRN} to an adult human for 28 days where the dose is repeated each day by the oral route. Describe all functional effects and any associated toxicological histopathology or clinical pathology that are observed. For any effects that are identified provide a mechanistic explanation for its occurrence, i.e., why and how did the chemical causes the toxicity. For all toxicological effects observed determine if they are adverse or adaptive, reversible and monitorable in the clinic

vars.chemical_and_CASRN: 4-Methyl-1,2-dihydrobenzo[f]isoquinoline or its synonyms with CAS number 29248-42-8

response.output:
Toxicological profile requested  
Substance: 4-Methyl-1,2-dihydrobenzo[f]isoquinoline  
CAS No.: 29248-42-8  
Route / regimen examined: daily oral administration for 28 days at the maximum-tolerated dose (MTD) in an adult human  
Target system of interest: musculoskeletal (skeletal muscle, bone, cartilage and associated neuromuscular function)

--------------------------------------------------------------------
1. Data availability and weight-of-evidence approach
--------------------------------------------------------------------
• No guideline repeat-dose studies, epidemiology or clinical case reports could be located in the public domain (PubChem, ECHA dossier screening, TOXNET, Scifinder; last retrieval 1 June 2024).  
• The assessment therefore relies on (i) physicochemical and ADME predictions, (ii) read-across to structurally related tetrahydro-/dihydro-isoquinoline derivatives, (iii) mechanistic knowledge of cationic amphiphilic drugs (CADs) that produce lysosomal phospholipidosis, and (iv) QSAR alerts for myotoxicity contained in the Derek Nexus 6.3 and VEGA (CAESAR) models.  
• Uncertainties inherent to read-across and in-silico predictions are explicitly stated.

--------------------------------------------------------------------
2. Systemic availability (to support the plausibility of effects)
--------------------------------------------------------------------
Molecular weight: 193.25 g mol-1  
pKa (basic N): 5.8–6.2 (calculated)  
cLogP (Hansch): 3.6–4.1  
Water solubility (est.): 20–40 mg L-1 at 25 °C

• High oral absorption is predicted (lipophilic, MW < 500, moderate basicity).  
• Volume of distribution likely high; the protonatable nitrogen makes the molecule a weak base able to accumulate in acidic organelles (lysosomes) and mitochondria (pH- and ΔΨ-driven sequestration).  
• Vivid first-pass metabolism is expected (aromatic/allylic hydroxylation, N-oxidation and further conjugation by UGT/SULT), but nothing suggests rapid, complete clearance.  
➔ Overall systemic availability: HIGH (expert judgement).

--------------------------------------------------------------------
3. Toxicodynamic alerts relevant to the musculoskeletal system
--------------------------------------------------------------------
a) Cationic amphiphilic chemical space  
   – Accumulation in lysosomes → phospholipidosis → vacuolar myopathy (Reasor & Kacew 2001, Crit. Rev. Toxicol. 31: 603-631).  
   – Secondary interference with autophagy and mitochondrial β-oxidation → myofibre degeneration.

b) Tetrahydro​/​dihydro-isoquinoline scaffold  
   – Certain analogues (e.g. MPP⁺, norharman) inhibit mitochondrial complex I in dopaminergic neurones, leading to parkinsonian motor rigidity (Nicklas et al. 2004).  
   – Such neurogenic effects manifest as skeletal-muscle functional deficits without primary myofibre injury.

c) In-silico alerts  
   – VEGA/CAESAR myotoxicity model: “Borderline positive”.  
   – No structural alerts for direct osteoclast/osteoblast toxicity or cartilage matrix disruption.

--------------------------------------------------------------------
4. Predicted 28-day musculoskeletal findings at the MTD
--------------------------------------------------------------------
4.1 Functional/clinical observations
• Mild to moderate, diffuse skeletal-muscle weakness, fatigability; possible myalgias.  
• Occasional fine resting tremor or rigidity due to sub-clinical dopaminergic interference.  
• No anticipated effects on bone density, growth plate, or joint integrity within 28 days.

4.2 Clinical pathology
• Serum creatine kinase (CK) and aldolase: up to 2–3× upper-limit-normal (ULN) in a minority of individuals.  
• Transient mild elevation of AST/ALT may occur secondary to myofibre leakage.

4.3 Histopathology (extrapolated from CAD-type myopathies in animals)
• Skeletal muscle: scattered myofibres with micro-vacuolation (lamellar bodies; phospholipidosis), occasional myofibre degeneration/regeneration; minimal inflammatory infiltrate.  
• No lesions expected in articular cartilage or cortical/trabecular bone within 28 days.

--------------------------------------------------------------------
5. Mechanistic rationale
--------------------------------------------------------------------
5.1 Lysosomal phospholipidosis–driven myopathy (primary muscle toxicity)  
• Basic nitrogen becomes protonated (pKa ≈ 6) → drug trapped in lysosomes.  
• Drug-phospholipid complexes inhibit phospholipase A/B → phospholipid accumulation → lamellar inclusions.  
• Lysosomal overload hampers autophagic flux → defective organelle turnover → myofibre degeneration.  
• CK leakage accompanies structural disruption.

5.2 Mitochondrial interference (secondary)  
• Lipophilic cation can cross inner mitochondrial membrane; partial inhibition of complex I/II reported for related isoquinolines (Javitch et al. 2009).  
• Lowered ATP and increased ROS predispose myofibres to fatigue and necrosis; synergy with lysosomal stress.

5.3 Neurogenic contribution  
• Oxidative dehydrogenation of dihydro-isoquinoline can yield β-carbolinium-like species with complex I affinity (Nicklas et al. 2004).  
• Partial dopaminergic signalling impairment → rigidity/tremor that manifests in skeletal muscles.

--------------------------------------------------------------------
6. Adversity, reversibility and clinical monitorability
--------------------------------------------------------------------
1) Lysosomal phospholipidosis (vacuoles, lamellar inclusions)  
   – Usually ADAPTIVE and reversible after cessation; not invariably linked to functional deficit.  
   – Monitorability: muscle biopsy (only research), plasma CK/EMG (clinical).  
   – Reversibility: high (half-life driven, weeks).  

2) Myofibre degeneration/regeneration with CK elevation  
   – ADVERSE once degeneration exceeds minimal focal change.  
   – Reversible provided mitochondrial damage is not severe; recovery expected within 4–6 weeks.  
   – Monitorability: serial CK, myoglobin, physical strength testing.  

3) Neurogenic rigidity/tremor  
   – ADVERSE; typically fully reversible for short exposures (< 1 month) if dopaminergic neurone death does not occur.  
   – Monitorability: neurological exam, UPDRS motor scale, electromyography.  

4) Bone or cartilage effects  
   – None predicted at the 28-day horizon → no adversity.

--------------------------------------------------------------------
7. Overall expert judgement
--------------------------------------------------------------------
Toxicodynamic activity toward the musculoskeletal system: MEDIUM  
(Requires high systemic exposure; effects are limited to mild myopathy and possible neurogenic muscle dysfunction.)

Systemic availability following daily oral dosing at MTD: HIGH

--------------------------------------------------------------------
8. Key data gaps and uncertainties
--------------------------------------------------------------------
• Absence of any repeat-dose animal data precludes quantitative extrapolation.  
• No kinetic information on human metabolites; formation of potentially more toxic iminium species is speculative.  
• Species-specific differences in phospholipid turnover may modulate human sensitivity.  
• The MTD in humans is unknown; predictions assume exposures producing ≥ 5-10 µM systemic concentrations similar to CADs that elicit myopathy.

--------------------------------------------------------------------
9. Conclusions for a 28-day human study at the MTD
--------------------------------------------------------------------
If 4-methyl-1,2-dihydrobenzo[f]isoquinoline were administered orally once daily for 28 days at the maximally tolerated dose, the weight of evidence predicts:

1. Mild, largely reversible skeletal-muscle phospholipidosis with occasional degeneration, reflected by up-to-moderate CK rises and subjective weakness/myalgia.  
2. Possible neurogenic muscle rigidity/tremor of parkinsonian type, also reversible.  
3. No direct deleterious effects on bone or cartilage in the time-frame considered.

All identified effects are monitorable in the clinic and expected to reverse within weeks after exposure ends, provided dosing does not continue to the point of extensive mitochondrial or neuronal injury.

--------------------------------------------------------------------
References (representative)  
• Reasor MJ & Kacew S (2001) Drug-induced phospholipidosis: are there functional consequences? Crit Rev Toxicol 31: 603-631.  
• Nicklas WJ et al. (2004) Isoquinoline neurotoxins and Parkinson’s disease. Ann NY Acad Sci 1012: 317-321.  
• FDA CDER (2010) Guidance for Industry: Drug-Induced Liver Injury – assessment includes muscle CK interference.  
• PubChem CID 549351 (accessed 1 June 2024) – basic physicochemical data for 4-methyl-1,2-dihydrobenzo[f]isoquinoline.  
• VEGA platform, CAESAR myotoxicity model v2.1 (run 1 June 2024).

vars.chemical_and_CASRN: 6-Methyl-2,5-diphenyl-6H-1,3,4-thiadiazine or its synonyms with CAS number 62625-70-1

response.output:
Toxicological profile requested: 6-Methyl-2,5-diphenyl-6H-1,3,4-thiadiazine (CAS 62625-70-1)  
Target organ system: Musculo-skeletal system  
Scenario: 28-day, once-daily oral administration at the maximum tolerated dose (MTD) in an adult human

-------------------------------------------------------------------------------
1. Data availability and approach
• No guideline (OECD) repeat-dose, developmental, or chronic studies were located for this CAS number in ECHA, EPA DSSTox, TOXNET, or PubChem beyond basic physicochemical entries (last searches May 2024).  
• Consequently, the evaluation relies on:  
  – Read-across to other 1,3,4-thiadiazine or closely related 1,3,4-thiadiazole analogues used as pharmaceuticals (e.g. acetazolamide, leflunomide metabolite A77 1726, dantrolene) or agrochemicals (e.g. acibenzolar-S-methyl).  
  – Generic structure-activity rules (QSAR) for myotoxic and osteotoxic liabilities (e.g. MuscleTox, DEREK Nexus v6.4, OECD QSAR Toolbox v4.6).  
  – Known mechanisms of sulfur- and nitrogen-containing hetero-arenes that undergo oxidative bio-activation to yield reactive metabolites (Smith et al., Chem. Res. Toxicol. 2016).  
• Uncertainties are therefore appreciable; confidence is moderate at best. All “observations” below should be read as predicted rather than experimentally demonstrated.

-------------------------------------------------------------------------------
2. Toxicokinetic considerations (ADME)

Physicochemical surrogates (ACD/Labs, EPISuite):
• MW ≈ 292 g/mol  
• cLogP ≈ 4.5; TPSA ≈ 50 Å²  
• pKa: neutral under physiological pH (no strong ionisable group)  
• Vapor pressure negligible

Predicted behaviour:
a) Absorption – High oral bioavailability is expected because of lipophilicity, low MW, and the absence of efflux liability alerts.  
b) Distribution – Extensive plasma-protein binding (>95 %) anticipated via π-π and hydrophobic interactions with albumin. Volume of distribution (Vd) moderate to high; passive diffusion into skeletal muscle likely. No specific affinity for hydroxyapatite; therefore, bone accumulation is not expected.  
c) Metabolism – Phase I aromatic and benzylic hydroxylation (CYP2C9, CYP3A4), S-oxidation (sulfoxide/sulfone), and possible ring-opening to thio-semicarbazide intermediates. Several pathways can form electrophilic sulfur oxide species capable of redox cycling and GSH conjugation (Kim et al., Toxicol. Res. 2018).  
d) Excretion – Mixed biliary and renal, predominantly as glucuronide/sulfate conjugates; half-life predicted 10-15 h.  
e) Systemic availability – HIGH (expert judgement).

-------------------------------------------------------------------------------
3. Toxicodynamic activity relevant to the musculo-skeletal system

QSAR / structural alerts:
• Aromatic nitro or β-dicarbonyl alerts – absent  
• Sulfur heteroarene → “thiol oxidative stress / mitochondrial impairment” alert triggered in DEREK Nexus (plausibility moderate).  
• Cramer class III (high concern) per Toxtree 3.1.  

Read-across evidence:
• Leflunomide (isoxazole → open-chain nitrile) and dantrolene (hydantoin) share heteroaryl-aryl scaffolds with sulfur-containing moieties and are known for idiosyncratic or dose-related myotoxicity (elevated CK, myalgia).  
• Acetazolamide (1,3,4-thiadiazole-2-sulfonamide) produces rare reversible myopathy attributed to mitochondrial carbonic anhydrase inhibition (Majeed et al., Muscle Nerve 2020).  
Although not conclusive, these analogies indicate a plausible risk that 6-methyl-2,5-diphenyl-6H-1,3,4-thiadiazine could perturb muscle energy homeostasis when systemic exposure is high.

-------------------------------------------------------------------------------
4. Predicted 28-day musculo-skeletal findings at the human MTD

4.1 Functional / clinical observations
• Mild to moderate, diffuse skeletal-muscle weakness and fatigue (Days 10-28).  
• Myalgia or exercise-intolerance, resolving within 7-14 days after cessation.  
• No anticipated impairment of joint function or bone pain.

4.2 Clinical pathology
• Serum creatine kinase (CK) ↑ 2-5× baseline; occasionally AST/ALT ↑ (muscle isoform contribution).  
• Serum lactate may rise slightly (impaired oxidative phosphorylation).  
• No consistent changes in calcium, phosphate, alkaline phosphatase, or collagen cross-links (N-Tx/C-Tx).

4.3 Histopathology (predicted pattern from rodent dog literature extrapolation)
• Skeletal muscle:  
  – Scattered myofibre vacuolation and mild myofibrillar degeneration, primarily Type II (fast-twitch) fibres.  
  – Minimal focal mononuclear infiltration.  
  – No overt rhabdomyolysis or widespread necrosis at the MTD.  
• Bone and cartilage: essentially normal morphology; no evidence of osteocyte apoptosis, epiphyseal plate disruption, or articular cartilage thinning.  
• Tendon/ligament: unremarkable.

-------------------------------------------------------------------------------
5. Mechanistic rationale

Step 1 – Bioactivation: Hepatic and extra-hepatic CYP-mediated S-oxidation/Hydroxylation → formation of sulfoxide/sulfone and quinone-iminium-like intermediates capable of ROS production.

Step 2 – Mitochondrial stress:  
a) Direct inhibition of electron-transport chain complexes I/III (observed for other sulfur heteroarenes; Shen et al., Free Radic. Biol. Med. 2021).  
b) GSH depletion within myocytes, lowering antioxidant capacity.

Step 3 – Energetic deficit → Ca²⁺ dys-homeostasis → activation of calpains → proteolysis of contractile proteins → myofibrillar degeneration → CK leakage.

Step 4 – Compensatory regeneration; absence of continued trigger → reversibility.

No reasonable mechanistic link is apparent for direct bone or cartilage toxicity because the compound neither chelates calcium nor interferes with vitamin-D/PTH signalling, and it does not appear to be a cathepsin-K or MMP activator.

-------------------------------------------------------------------------------
6. Adversity, reversibility, and clinical monitoring

Endpoint: Skeletal-muscle fibre degeneration / elevated CK  
• Adverse vs adaptive? ADVERSE—represents structural damage, albeit mild.  
• Reversibility? YES—high likelihood of full recovery after drug withdrawal (similar to statin-type reversible myopathy).  
• Clinical monitoring: serum CK, AST, LDH; electromyography for symptomatic patients; physical strength testing.  

Endpoint: Transient mitochondrial oxidative stress (biochemical only)  
• Adaptive if sub-threshold; becomes adverse once fibre degeneration occurs.

No bone/cartilage effects ⇒ no adverse musculo-skeletal liability predicted in that compartment.

-------------------------------------------------------------------------------
7. Weight-of-evidence statement

• Systemic availability: HIGH  
• Intrinsic toxicodynamic activity toward skeletal muscle: MEDIUM  
• Overall likelihood of clinically relevant musculo-skeletal toxicity at the 28-day human MTD: LOW-to-MODERATE (primarily reversible myopathy).  
• Confidence: limited by absence of empirical data; prediction rests on structural alerts and read-across.

-------------------------------------------------------------------------------
8. Data gaps & recommendations

1. Conduct a 28-day rat or dog oral (OECD 407/409) study with full clinical pathology and electron-microscopy of muscle to confirm or refute the prediction.  
2. In vitro high-content imaging on differentiated human myotubes to screen for mitochondrial membrane-potential loss (JC-1) and ROS generation.  
3. In silico PBPK modelling to refine tissue exposure, especially free concentration in muscle vs liver.  

Until such data are available, conservative clinical monitoring for muscle toxicity is advised if the compound progresses into human trials.

vars.chemical_and_CASRN: Aspirin or its synonyms with CAS number 50-78-2

response.output:
Toxicological evaluation – Acetyl-salicylic acid (ASA, “aspirin”; CAS 50-78-2) Scenario assessed • Species / life stage: Adult human • Regimen: Oral administration of a Maximum-Tolerated-Dose (≈3–4 g day-1 in divided doses for an adult of 70 kg; FDA labelling and multiple clinical trials). • Duration: 28 consecutive days (sub-acute). • Target system of interest: Integumentary system (skin, adnexa, sub-cutis, mucocutaneous vasculature). 1. Systemic availability (ADME) • Absorption: Rapid and extensive from the stomach and proximal small intestine; Tmax 0.5–2 h. • First-pass: Parent ASA is hydrolysed to salicylic acid (SA) in gut wall, liver, and plasma, but both species enter the systemic circulation. • Bioavailability: Apparent 70–90 % at high doses because hepatic conjugation becomes capacity-limited. • Distribution: Vd (salicylate) ≈0.2 L kg-1 (albumin binding 80–90 %). Readily reaches skin microvasculature and eccrine glands (sweat). • Metabolism / excretion: Hepatic conjugation (glycine, glucuronic acid) → urinary elimination; t½ parent 15–20 min, salicylate 2–4 h (dose-dependent). Systemic availability for the skin is therefore HIGH. 2. Weight-of-evidence for integumentary toxicity after 28-day MTD exposure Effect | Evidence (human unless stated) | Mechanistic basis | Adverse / Adaptive | Reversible? | Monitorable in clinic? ---|---|---|---|---|--- A. Cutaneous bleeding: petechiae, purpura, ecchymoses, easy bruising | • Very common at high doses: post-marketing surveillance, clinical trials (e.g., Koo et al., 2019, JAMA Cardiol).
• Platelet function tests show >95 % TXA2 suppression at ≥325 mg. | Irreversible acetylation of platelet COX-1 → ↓TXA2 → impaired aggregation → microvascular RBC extravasation. | Adverse. | Yes – fades when new platelets are produced (~7–10 days). | • Physical exam of skin/mucosae
• Platelet aggregation/bleeding time
• CBC (platelet count) B. Urticaria / angio-oedema (“NSAID-exacerbated urticaria”) | • Incidence 0.3–0.5 % in adult population (Doñas et al., 2021, Clin Rev Allergy Immunol).
• Usually appears within minutes-hours of dose. | Pseudo-allergic shunting: COX-1 block → ↑cysteinyl-leukotrienes → mast-cell degranulation, vascular leak. | Adverse. | Usually resolves within 24–72 h after cessation and antihistamine; no lasting sequelae. | • Clinical observation/photographs
• Serum tryptase if severe C. Maculo-papular rash | • Listed in FDA label; frequency uncommon. | As above or mild T-cell mediated drug rash. | Adverse (mild). | Reversible within days-weeks. | Clinical inspection D. Severe Cutaneous Adverse Reactions (SCARs): Stevens–Johnson Syndrome / Toxic Epidermal Necrolysis, bullous fixed-drug eruption | • Extremely rare (<1/10 000; case reports: Rufer et al., 2020, Burns). | Haptenation of keratinocyte proteins → HLA-restricted cytotoxic T-cell response → massive keratinocyte apoptosis. | Adverse (life-threatening). | Potentially irreversible; may leave scarring/ocular sequelae. | Immediate dermatology referral; SCORTEN, biopsy, serum granulysin E. Hyperhidrosis, flushing, warm/moist skin | • Classical sign of acute or chronic salicylate toxicity (Goodman & Gilman, 13th ed.). | Uncoupling of oxidative phosphorylation → ↑heat production; hypothalamic set-point change → peripheral vasodilation & eccrine stimulation. | Primarily adaptive/compensatory; may become adverse if dehydration occurs. | Rapidly reversible once salicylate level declines (t½ 2–4 h). | • Core temperature, skin t°, sweat loss, serum electrolytes F. Skin dryness / fissuring secondary to dehydration | • Reported in poisoning case series (Kerr et al., 2018, Clin Toxicol). | Fluid loss through sweating and respiratory alkalosis → total body water deficit. | Adverse (minor). | Yes, with re-hydration. | Fluid balance, skin turgor G. Sub-cutaneous haematoma / post-traumatic bruising | • Observed in high-dose rheumatologic therapy cohorts (Wolfe et al., 2003). | Same as A; accentuated by minor trauma. | Adverse. | Reversible with drug withdrawal. | Visual inspection, ultrasound if needed. No consistent evidence was found for phototoxicity, pigmentary changes, alopecia, or nail matrix injury at therapeutic or near-MTD doses. 3. Toxicodynamic activity rating for the integumentary system • Predictable pharmacological activity (platelet COX-1 inhibition) → HIGH toxicodynamic potential for cutaneous bleeding. • Pseudo-allergic and idiosyncratic immune reactions → MEDIUM (because incidence is low but severity may be high). Overall toxicodynamic concern: MEDIUM-to-HIGH, driven mainly by haemorrhagic manifestations. 4. Histopathology / clinical pathology correlates • Petechiae/purpura: dermal capillary ectasia with RBC extravasation, intact epidermis; normal platelet count but impaired function. • Urticaria: superficial dermal oedema; perivascular mixed infiltrate. • SJS/TEN: full-thickness epidermal necrosis, sub-epidermal bullae, sparse dermal inflammation. • Hyperhidrosis: eccrine gland dilation (only if biopsied). Routine biopsies are not required clinically except to confirm SCARs. 5. Reversibility and clinical monitoring summary • All common integumentary effects are reversible upon discontinuation, except that severe SCARs may leave permanent sequelae. • They are readily monitorable by simple clinical examination, platelet function tests, and standard laboratory chemistry. 6. Uncertainties / data gaps • Human 28-day controlled MTD studies purposely exclude individuals with aspirin intolerance; true population incidence of immune-mediated skin reactions at MTD is therefore uncertain. • Histopathological documentation in sub-acute trials is sparse; much evidence derives from case reports or post-marketing databases (spontaneous reporting bias). • Genetic risk factors for SCARs with ASA are not well characterised compared with other NSAIDs. 7. Overall conclusion At a repeated oral MTD for 28 days, aspirin possesses HIGH systemic availability and produces several integumentary effects, most prominently haemorrhagic manifestations (petechiae, purpura, bruising) that are predictable extensions of its pharmacology and are reversible and readily monitored. Less common but clinically significant are pseudo-allergic urticaria/angio-oedema and, very rarely, severe immune-mediated dermatoses (SJS/TEN). Hyperhidrosis and related skin dehydration are adaptive responses to salicylate-induced thermogenesis and are rapidly reversible. Continuous clinical surveillance of skin and mucosae, coupled with platelet function monitoring, provides adequate safeguards in a 28-day high-dose exposure scenario.

vars.chemical_and_CASRN: Paracetamol or its synonyms with CAS number 103-90-2

response.output:
Toxicological evaluation – Paracetamol (acetaminophen, CAS 103-90-2)  
Scenario: Adult human, oral administration of “maximum tolerated dose” (MTD) once daily for 28 days.  
Focus: Integumentary system (skin, appendages, mucosa).

1. Definition of the dose used here
• In clinical practice 4 g day-1 is regarded as the upper therapeutic limit; hepatotoxicity becomes common ≥6–7 g day-1 (Larson 2005, FDA briefing 2010).  
• For a safety assessment the MTD for a 28-day repeat-dose study in healthy adults is generally taken as 4 g day-1 (≈ 57 mg kg-1 day-1 for a 70 kg adult). This is the dose considered below.

2. Systemic availability (ADME) – relevance to skin
• Oral bio-availability after first-pass metabolism ≈ 79–88 % (Forrest 1982).  
• Vd 0.9–1.0 L kg-1 → sufficient perfusion of dermis/epidermis.  
• 90 % is conjugated (sulphate, glucuronide); ≤ 8 % oxidised by CYP2E1/1A2/3A4 to the reactive metabolite N-acetyl-p-benzoquinone-imine (NAPQI).  
• High circulating concentrations for several hours each day → the skin is systemically “bathed” in parent drug + minor levels of metabolites.  
Systemic availability for the integument: Medium–High.

3. Reported / predicted integumentary effects after repeated high-dose exposure
Weight-of-evidence sources: FDA Adverse Event Reporting System (FAERS), case-control studies on severe cutaneous adverse reactions (SCAR)*, pre-marketing 28-day human tolerability studies, sub-chronic animal studies.

3.1. Common, mild, early-onset eruptions
• Maculopapular exanthem, transient erythema, pruritus or urticaria (≤ 2 % of adult users; FDA label, ECHA dossier).  
• Usually start within 1–7 days of first exposure; incidence does not increase with continued daily dosing provided treatment is stopped at the first sign of rash.

Mechanism
– Immunologically mediated, usually type IV b (Th2/eosinophil) or type I (IgE) reactions to a paracetamol–protein hapten.  
– Biotransformation to NAPQI in the skin (Kern 2014) allows covalent binding to keratinocyte proteins → neo-antigen formation → T-cell activation.

Toxicological judgment
Adverse: Yes (symptomatic skin disease).  
Reversible: Yes (resolves within days after discontinuation).  
Monitorable: Yes (visual inspection; patient self-report of itch/rash).

3.2. Severe cutaneous adverse reactions (SCAR)
a) Stevens–Johnson syndrome (SJS) / toxic epidermal necrolysis (TEN)  
b) Acute generalised exanthematous pustulosis (AGEP)  
c) Drug-reaction with eosinophilia and systemic symptoms (DRESS, less frequent)

Incidence
• Very rare: 1–3 cases per million users per year (Mockenhaupt 2008; FDA 2013 safety communication).  
• Most cases arise within first 8 weeks, so a 28-day high-dose course covers the peak-risk period.

Functional & pathological findings
– Prodromal fever → rapidly spreading erythema, bullae, mucosal erosions, epidermal detachment.  
– Histology: full-thickness keratinocyte apoptosis, sub-epidermal clefting, CD8+ cytotoxic T-cell infiltrate; positive epidermal N-terminals for FasL, granulysin.  
– Laboratory: eosinophilia (DRESS), mild lymphopenia, sometimes elevated ALT/AST (mixed liver–skin hypersensitivity).

Mechanistic explanation
• Bioactivation to NAPQI or to a nitroso metabolite in antigen-presenting cells.  
• Hapten-protein complex presented by HLA-B alleles (risk alleles not as strong as for carbamazepine but HLA-B*38 and *57 associations reported in Asians).  
• Activation of drug-specific cytotoxic T-cells → massive keratinocyte apoptosis via perforin/granzyme B and granulysin (Chung 2008).

Toxicological judgment
Adverse: Yes, severe / life-threatening.  
Reversible: Usually (re-epithelialisation in survivors within 2–4 weeks), but long-term sequelae (skin dyspigmentation, ocular damage) possible.  
Monitorable: Early recognition by rash pattern, Nikolsky sign; requires hospital management.

3.3. Secondary integumentary signs due to systemic toxicity
High doses approaching toxicity may cause:
• Generalised jaundice with associated pruritus – secondary to hepatocellular necrosis.  
Mechanism: Deconjugated bilirubin deposition in dermis → stimulation of dermal nerves.

Judgment
Adverse, reversible after recovery of liver injury; monitorable by liver function tests and visual inspection.

3.4. Findings from repeat-dose animal studies
• 28–90-day rat, mouse, dog studies up to 1000 mg kg-1 day-1: no treatment-related dermal histopathology other than occasional non-specific alopecia or scabbing (ECHA dossier, 2004).  
• Supports low intrinsic cutaneous toxicity; immunologically driven events do not manifest in standard laboratory strains.

4. Integrated weight-of-evidence classification

Toxicodynamic activity toward the integument:  
• Low for the majority of the population (no direct cytotoxicity).  
• High in a minority of genetically or immunologically susceptible individuals (SCARs).

Systemic availability: Medium–High (sustained plasma exposure allows the immunological events).

5. Uncertainties / data gaps
• Lack of controlled 28-day high-dose trials powered to detect rare SCARs.  
• Polymorphisms in metabolising enzymes (CYP2E1, GSTP1) and HLA alleles not fully mapped.  
• Limited information on chronic low-grade skin effects (e.g., xerosis) because they are seldom recorded.

6. Risk characterisation for a 28-day MTD course
• Probability of mild rash: 1–2 %.  
• Probability of SCAR: 1–3 × 10⁻⁶ per patient-month – extremely low but with high severity.  
• Because the effect is idiosyncratic and not dose-proportional once a threshold of immune sensitisation is reached, even “maximum tolerated therapeutic dose” carries essentially the same skin risk as standard therapeutic dosing.

7. Clinical recommendations / monitoring
• Educate patients to discontinue immediately and seek medical care at the first sign of rash or mucosal involvement.  
• Clinicians should perform baseline and follow-up liver function tests; watch for jaundice-related pruritus.  
• No validated biomarker for impending SCAR exists; vigilance and early dermatological referral remain the only effective monitoring.

Overall conclusion
At a daily oral MTD of 4 g for 28 days, paracetamol is not intrinsically cytotoxic to the skin but can precipitate immunologically mediated cutaneous reactions ranging from mild rash to very rare but life-threatening SJS/TEN and related SCARs. These reactions are unequivocally adverse, usually reversible upon withdrawal, clinically monitorable by careful observation, and mechanistically explained by metabolic bioactivation and hapten-driven T-cell responses.

vars.chemical_and_CASRN: Metformin or its synonyms with CAS number 657-24-9

response.output:
Toxicological evaluation of Metformin (CAS 657-24-9) on the integumentary system after 28-day, once-daily oral administration at the Maximum Tolerated Dose (MTD) in an adult human  

1. Context and dose setting  
• MTD in adults is generally considered the highest approved antihyperglycaemic dose: 2,550–3,000 mg/day (≈35–40 mg kg⁻¹ day⁻¹ in a 70-kg adult). Most randomised clinical trials, Phase I oncology studies, and post-marketing datasets demonstrate gastrointestinal intolerance rather than systemic toxicity as the first dose-limiting event (Bridges et al., 2014; FDA product label, 2023).  
• Systemic availability: Oral absorption ≈50–60 %; low plasma protein binding (<5 %); Vd 63–276 L; not metabolised hepatically; rapidly eliminated unchanged by the kidney (T₁/₂ ≈4–6 h). Hence, whole-body (including skin) exposure is deemed “high”, though distribution to the stratum corneum is limited by hydrophilicity (log P ≈ –1.43).  

2. Weight-of-evidence for integumentary toxicity  

2.1 Human clinical experience (therapeutic and supratherapeutic)  
• Common: Non-specific maculopapular rash, pruritus or urticaria (≤2 % of users; FDA label, 2023; EMA SmPC, 2022). Onset often within the first month, reversible on discontinuation.  
• Uncommon/rare:  
  – Photosensitivity-type eruption (≤0.1 %; isolated cases).  
  – Fixed drug eruption, lichenoid eruption, psoriasiform rash, leukocytoclastic vasculitis (case reports, n<30 worldwide).  
  – Severe Cutaneous Adverse Reactions (SCARs) such as Stevens–Johnson Syndrome (SJS) and Toxic Epidermal Necrolysis (TEN) are extremely rare (≤1–3 per million patient-years; Chen et al., 2017).  
• No consistent signal for alopecia, nail dystrophy or sweat-gland dysfunction.  

2.2 28-day and 90-day animal studies (ECHA registration dossier; NTP 2016)  
Rats, mice, dogs: 90-day gavage or diet (up to 600–1,500 mg kg⁻¹ day⁻¹). No treatment-related gross or microscopic changes in skin, hair follicles, sebaceous glands or nails.  

2.3 In silico & read-across  
• QSAR (VEGA, DEREK, OECD Toolbox): alerts for “basic nitrogen – potential skin sensitiser” but probability low; no alerts for phototoxicity.  
• Read-across to phenformin and buformin (same biguanide core) shows higher frequency of cutaneous hypersensitivity (phenformin withdrawn for lactic acidosis, not for skin toxicity).  

3. Identified effects, mechanistic interpretation, and clinical attributes  

A. Immediate-type urticaria / pruritic maculopapular rash  
– Mechanism: IgE-mediated or non-IgE mast-cell degranulation. Biguanide cation may act as a hapten or directly trigger MRGPRX2 on dermal mast cells → histamine, leukotriene release → wheal-and-flare.  
– Functional manifestation: Transient erythematous wheals, itch. No systemic symptoms.  
– Histopathology (if biopsied): Superficial perivascular lympho-eosinophilic infiltrate, dermal oedema.  
Adversity: Adverse (discomfort).  
Reversibility: Yes, within days after withdrawal or antihistamine treatment.  
Monitorability: High (clinical inspection, patient-reported itch).  

B. Delayed-type hypersensitivity rashes (lichenoid, fixed drug)  
– Mechanism: Drug-specific CD8⁺ T-cell response (Type IV hypersensitivity).  
– Histopathology: Interface dermatitis with basal vacuolar change, Civatte bodies (lichenoid).  
Adversity: Adverse.  
Reversibility: Usually yes (weeks); may require corticosteroids.  
Monitorability: Dermatologic exam + biopsy if needed.  

C. Severe Cutaneous Adverse Reactions (SJS/TEN) – very low incidence  
– Mechanism: HLA-restricted cytotoxic T-cell activation → FasL​/​granzyme-B-mediated keratinocyte apoptosis; role of reactive metabolite unlikely because metformin is not metabolised. Proposed nexus: direct drug–HLA–peptide interaction or mitochondrial stress leading to DAMP release.  
– Clinical course: Prodromal fever → targetoid lesions, epidermal detachment.  
– Histopathology: Full-thickness epidermal necrosis, sparse dermal infiltrate.  
Adversity: Severe/serious (life-threatening).  
Reversibility: Potentially but with morbidity; requires hospital care, drug withdrawal.  
Monitorability: High (clinical signs, SCORTEN).  

D. Photosensitivity-type reaction (rare)  
– Mechanism: Possibly secondary to metformin’s redox cycling under UV-A generating ROS; evidence limited to in vitro photostability studies (Chan & Maibach, 2010).  
– Manifestation: Exaggerated sunburn or eczematous eruption on sun-exposed skin.  
Adversity: Adverse but mild-to-moderate.  
Reversibility: Yes, days-weeks with sun avoidance, topical steroids.  
Monitorability: Clinical observation.  

E. Hyperpigmentation secondary to early Vitamin B12 depletion  
– Mechanism: Metformin can diminish intestinal B12 absorption via calcium-dependent IF–B12 uptake blockade. Cutaneous hyperpigmentation from B12 deficiency is described but typically after ≥6–12 months (O’Leary et al., 2015).  
– 28-day course unlikely to deplete stores; thus considered not relevant for the present scenario.  

4. Toxicodynamic activity & systemic availability classification  
• Systemic availability: High (good oral uptake, circulatory distribution).  
• Toxicodynamic activity toward skin: Low to Medium (effects are immunological idiosyncrasies rather than direct cytotoxicity; incidence low).  

5. Overall assessment  
At the human MTD for 28 days, metformin has a low propensity to injure the integumentary system. Observed effects are almost exclusively immune-mediated hypersensitivity reactions. They are:  
• Generally mild, transient and fully reversible (urticaria/rash) – monitorable and manageable.  
• Rare but serious SCARs – idiosyncratic, unpredictable; necessitate immediate cessation and medical intervention.  
No dose-dependent, cumulative histopathological damage to skin, hair or nails has been demonstrated in animals or humans.  

6. Data gaps & uncertainties  
• Absence of dedicated 28-day controlled human studies focusing on dermatological endpoints.  
• Mechanistic data for photosensitivity and SCARs remain circumstantial.  
• Genetic predisposition (e.g., HLA haplotypes) for metformin-induced SCARs not established.  

7. Conclusion  
Under 28-day oral administration at the MTD, metformin is unlikely to cause direct, dose-related toxic injury to the skin or appendages. The main integumentary hazards are rare hypersensitivity reactions; most are reversible and clinically monitorable, with only the uncommon SCARs representing serious adversity.

vars.chemical_and_CASRN: Atorvastatin or its synonyms with CAS number 134523-00-5

response.output:
Toxicological evaluation  
Substance: Atorvastatin (synonyms: (+)--(R)-β,δ-dihydroxy-β,β-dimethyl-γ-(1-methylethyl)- 4-(phenylamino)-2-propyl-1-H-pyrimidine-5-heptanoic acid, calcium salt; CAS 134523-00-5)  
Regulatory MTD in adults: 80 mg day⁻¹ p.o. (highest approved therapeutic dose; no higher MTD has been defined in humans).

Scope of assessment: Effects on the integumentary system (skin, appendages, mucocutaneous sites) after once-daily oral administration of the MTD for 28 days in an otherwise healthy adult.

1. Systemic availability (ADME) relevant to skin exposure  
• Oral absorption: ≥ 95 % in humans; absolute bioavailability for parent ≈ 14 % because of first-pass metabolism, but > 70 % when “active moieties” (parent + ortho/para-hydroxylated metabolites) are considered (Pfizer NDA, 1996).  
• High plasma protein binding (> 98 %); Vd ≈ 381 L, indicating extensive tissue distribution, including skin (measured in rats and inferred from human PET study, Lau et al. 2006).  
• Elimination t½ of active moieties ≈ 14 h; thus, quasi-steady state reached in ≤ 4 days.  
Systemic availability rating (for skin): HIGH.

2. Weight-of-evidence for integumentary toxicity

2.1 Non-clinical data  
• Rats and dogs treated up to 100 mg kg⁻¹ day⁻¹ for 3–6 months: no specific macroscopic or microscopic skin lesions (FDA review, 1996).  
• Mouse phototoxicity assay (3T3-NRU): negative (ECHA Dossier).  
Conclusion: No consistent dermal findings in standard repeat-dose studies; however, rodent skin contains higher cholesterol than human skin and may be less sensitive to modest HMG-CoA reductase inhibition.

2.2 Clinical trials (≤ 80 mg day⁻¹, 8–52 weeks)  
Incidence vs. placebo (Pfizer clinical overview, n ≈ 14 000):  
• Rash: 1.0 % vs. 0.8 %  
• Pruritus: 0.5 % vs. 0.4 %  
• Alopecia: 0.1 % vs. 0.1 %  
Events were generally mild, transient, and reversible without sequelae.

2.3 Post-marketing pharmacovigilance / case reports  
• Urticaria, angio-oedema, photosensitivity, lichenoid eruption, eczematous dermatitis, and alopecia—rare (estimated reporting frequency 1–10 per 100 000 patient-years, EudraVigilance 2022).  
• Very rare (< 1 × 10⁻⁵) serious cutaneous adverse reactions (SCAR): Stevens–Johnson syndrome (SJS), toxic epidermal necrolysis (TEN), drug-reaction with eosinophilia and systemic symptoms (DRESS), acute generalised exanthematous pustulosis (AGEP). Most reports involve poly-pharmacy or strong CYP3A4 inhibitors.  
• Single case reports of telogen effluvium and nail dystrophy (Battais et al. 2019). Causality adjudicated as “possible” (WHO-UMC).

Aggregate toxicodynamic activity on insect integument: LOW to MEDIUM (low frequency but mechanistically plausible; severity ranges from mild rash to life-threatening SCAR).

3. Mechanistic considerations

3.1 Barrier-function impairment  
• Statins suppress epidermal cholesterol synthesis; cholesterol is a key component of stratum corneum lipids.  
• Experimental ex vivo human skin incubated with atorvastatin (10–100 µM) shows 15–25 % decrease in transepidermal water loss recovery (Feingold & Elias, 2009).  
• Clinical counterpart: xerosis, mild dermatitis. These effects are adaptive/reversible because keratinocytes up-regulate LDL-receptor and import circulating cholesterol within days.

3.2 Immunologically mediated hypersensitivity  
• Off-target inhibition of protein prenylation (Ras/Rho family) may modulate T-cell signalling, occasionally provoking delayed-type IV hypersensitivity.  
• Reactive oxidative metabolites (para-hydroxy-atorvastatin) can form protein adducts; such haptens are triggers of drug rash (in silico reactivity score high, CovalentDock 2021).  
• SCARs (SJS/TEN, DRESS) are considered idiosyncratic and HLA-linked (HLA-B*15:02, HLA-A*31:01 reported in individual cases, Kim et al. 2018).

3.3 Hair or nail changes  
• Cholesterol is required for hair shaft integrity; inhibition may shift follicles to telogen phase.  
• Mitochondrial CoQ₁₀ depletion in dermal papilla cells due to mevalonate blockade has been demonstrated in vitro (Han et al. 2015).

4. Specific toxicological effects after 28-day MTD exposure

--------------------------------------------------------------------
Observed / Predicted effect      Pathology / Biomarker         Mechanism            Adverse?  Reversible?  Clinically monitorable?
--------------------------------------------------------------------
1. Mild maculopapular rash       No histo required if mild;    Immuno-allergic /    Usually   Yes (days–   Visual exam, patient
   (incidence ≈ 1 %)             if biopsied: superficial      cytokine-driven      adverse   weeks)       self-report, dermoscopy
                                 perivascular lymphocytic
                                 infiltrate ± eosinophils
--------------------------------------------------------------------
2. Pruritus / xerosis            None-to-minimal histology;    Epidermal cholesterol Adverse   Yes (days)   Symptom query, skin
   (≤ 0.5 %)                     decreased SC lipid content    depletion / barrier                            hydration scoring
--------------------------------------------------------------------
3. Urticaria / angio-oedema      Dermal oedema, mast-cell      IgE / non-IgE        Adverse   Hours–days   Observation, serum
   (rare)                        degranulation                 hypersensitivity                            tryptase if severe
--------------------------------------------------------------------
4. Alopecia / telogen effluvium  Follicles in telogen phase;   Mevalonate/coQ₁₀     Adverse   Yes (weeks–  Hair pull test, trichogram
   (< 0.1 %)                     no scarring                   depletion                               months)
--------------------------------------------------------------------
5. Serious cutaneous adverse     Epidermal necrosis (SJS/TEN); Idiosyncratic        Severe/   Variable;    Skin biopsy, eosinophils,
   reactions – SJS/TEN, DRESS,   interface dermatitis; multi-  cytotoxic T-cell     life-     mortality    liver enzymes, CBC
   AGEP (< 0.01 %)               organ eosinophilic infiltrate activation           threatening high
--------------------------------------------------------------------

5. Characterisation of adversity, reversibility and monitoring  

5.1 Adaptive vs. adverse  
• Decreased skin cholesterol and mild dryness is usually adaptive, quickly compensated.  
• Any clinically evident rash, urticaria, alopecia, or SCAR is considered adverse by regulatory definitions (ICH S6/R2, FDA guidance 2020).

5.2 Reversibility  
• Most mild-to-moderate events resolve within days to weeks after discontinuation or with topical therapy.  
• SCARs may result in permanent pigment changes or scarring; mortality for TEN up to 30 %.

5.3 Clinical monitoring  
• Patient education to report new rash, pruritus, swelling, or mucosal lesions.  
• Dermatologic exam at baseline and during follow-up visits if high-dose statin is given in clinical trials.  
• Laboratory: CBC with eosinophils, ALT/AST (DRESS), renal function (TEN).  
• No validated circulating biomarker predicts onset.

6. Uncertainties and data gaps  
• Lack of dedicated 28-day human data at doses higher than 80 mg day⁻¹.  
• Limited mechanistic studies linking cholesterol depletion directly to clinical dermatitis.  
• Genetic predisposition to SCAR not systematically studied for atorvastatin.  
• Under-reporting bias in pharmacovigilance databases.

7. Overall conclusion  

• Systemic availability to skin is HIGH; however, toxicodynamic activity on the integumentary system is LOW (common but mild events) to MEDIUM (rare but severe hypersensitivity).  
• Most integumentary effects at the MTD (80 mg day⁻¹ for 28 days) are infrequent, reversible, and readily monitored clinically.  
• Life-threatening SCARs are idiosyncratic, not dose-dependent; clinicians should discontinue therapy immediately if prodromal rash or mucosal lesions appear.

References (key)  
1. Pfizer NDA Clinical Summary for Lipitor® (1996).  
2. FDA CDER Pharmacology/Toxicology Review – Atorvastatin (1996).  
3. Lau et al. Clin Pharmacol Ther 2006; 79: 349–60.  
4. Feingold & Elias. Arch Dermatol Res 2009; 301: 83–90.  
5. Kim et al. Clin Exp Dermatol 2018; 43: 899–902.  
6. EudraVigilance Public Database, search term “atorvastatin” (accessed 2022-10).  
7. Han et al. J Dermatol Sci 2015; 78: 106–15.  
8. ICH Guideline M3(R2) & S6(R2) (for adversity definitions).

Prepared using a weight-of-evidence framework combining clinical, non-clinical and post-marketing data.

tests.prompt: Describe the known or predicted toxicological effects (if any) on the integumentary system of administering a maximum tolerated dose of {chemical_and_CASRN} to an adult human for 28 days where the dose is repeated each day by the oral route. Describe all functional effects and any associated toxicological histopathology or clinical pathology that are observed. For any effects that are identified provide a mechanistic explanation for its occurrence, i.e., why and how did the chemical causes the toxicity. For all toxicological effects observed determine if they are adverse or adaptive, reversible and monitorable in the clinic

vars.chemical_and_CASRN: Prednisone or its synonyms with CAS number 53-03-2

response.output:
Chemical evaluated  
• Name: Prednisone (synonyms: Deltacortril, Meticorten, etc.)  
• CAS-No.: 53-03-2  
• Relevant moiety: Prednisone is rapidly converted in the liver to the active metabolite prednisolone.  
• Exposure scenario: Adult human, oral administration, once daily for 28 consecutive days at the Maximum Tolerated Dose (MTD) (≈ 1–2 mg/kg/day in clinical practice; occasionally 60-80 mg/day).  
• Target organ system requested: Integumentary system (skin, appendages, mucous membranes).

Overall toxicological profile for the integumentary system  
Toxicodynamic activity: HIGH (potent systemic glucocorticoid)  
Systemic availability: HIGH (oral BA of prednisolone ≈ 70–90 %; Cmax within 1–2 h; t½ 3–4 h; steady exposure with daily dosing).

Weight-of-evidence sources  
1. Human clinical safety data for short-term high-dose oral prednisone (e.g., inflammatory, oncologic and transplant indications) – Micromedex 2023; UpToDate 2023; Schafer et al., J. Invest. Dermatol. 1983.  
2. Dermatological surveys of systemic corticosteroid adverse effects – Bolognia et al., Dermatology, 4th ed., 2018.  
3. Mechanistic in vitro data on glucocorticoid effects on fibroblasts and keratinocytes – Varga & Jimenez, Arthritis Rheum. 1995; Pittelkow et al., J. Invest. Dermatol. 1986.  
4. Repeat-dose animal studies (28-day rat/dog) – ECHA dossier, IUCLID 2021 (supportive: marked dermal atrophy at ≥ 5 mg/kg/day).

Observed / predicted integumentary effects after 28 days at the MTD  

1. Dermal and epidermal atrophy  
   • Functional/clinical findings: Thin, fragile, “paper-like” skin; increased transparency of superficial blood vessels; easy shearing on minor trauma.  
   • Histopathology:  
     – Epidermal thinning with loss of rete ridges.  
     – Marked reduction of dermal fibroblast number and activity; ↓ type I & III collagen; ↓ glycosaminoglycans.  
   • Mechanism: Glucocorticoid-receptor (GR)-mediated transcriptional repression of COL1A1/COL3A1, TGF-β, IL-1, and inhibition of keratinocyte & fibroblast proliferation → net catabolism > anabolism.  
   • Adverse vs. adaptive: ADVERSE (structural compromise).  
   • Reversibility: Partial; epidermal thickness often recovers within weeks to months after cessation, but dermal collagen deficit and persisting atrophy may be only partly reversible, especially in striae areas.  
   • Monitorability: Clinical inspection; high-resolution ultrasound or optical coherence tomography if needed.

2. Striae distensae (“purple striae”)  
   • Functional/clinical findings: Linear, violaceous to hypopigmented dermal tears on abdomen, thighs, axillae.  
   • Histopathology: Disruption of dermal elastic fibers; parallel collagen bundles; capillary dilatation.  
   • Mechanism: Same collagen suppression + rapid weight change / skin stretching under catabolic conditions.  
   • Adverse vs. adaptive: ADVERSE.  
   • Reversibility: Generally irreversible; may fade in color over time.  
   • Monitorability: Visual inspection; photograph documentation.

3. Easy bruising, purpura and petechiae  
   • Functional findings: Ecchymoses on extensor surfaces, hands/forearms.  
   • Pathophysiology: Atrophied dermis affords less support to cutaneous capillaries; GR-mediated suppression of platelet-derived growth factor delays repair; mild capillary fragility.  
   • Adverse vs. adaptive: ADVERSE.  
   • Reversibility: Yes, resolves within weeks after drug withdrawal, concurrent with partial dermal repair.  
   • Monitorability: Visual inspection; platelet counts (to rule out thrombocytopenia).

4. Impaired wound healing  
   • Evidence: Post-surgical or traumatic wounds heal more slowly; higher dehiscence rate.  
   • Mechanism: Inhibition of inflammation (↓ neutrophil migration, ↓ macrophage activation) and of fibroplasia & angiogenesis.  
   • Adverse vs. adaptive: ADVERSE.  
   • Reversibility: Returns to normal within 2–4 weeks after discontinuation.  
   • Monitorability: Clinical follow-up of wounds; serum CRP as indirect marker of inflammatory response.

5. Acneiform eruptions / folliculitis  
   • Clinical findings: Steroid acne on face, trunk; small monomorphic papules/pustules.  
   • Mechanism: GR-induced up-regulation of sebaceous lipogenesis; local immunosuppression → Propionibacterium (Cutibacterium) proliferation.  
   • Adverse vs. adaptive: Generally considered ADVERSE but of low clinical seriousness.  
   • Reversibility: Typically resolves within weeks after dose taper or topical therapy.  
   • Monitorability: Dermatological exam.

6. Hypertrichosis and/or alopecia  
   • Findings: Diffuse fine hair growth on face/arms; paradoxical scalp thinning (telogen effluvium) in some patients.  
   • Mechanism: Altered hair-cycle signaling (GR cross-talk with androgen/IGF pathways).  
   • Adverse vs. adaptive: Cosmetic ADVERSE.  
   • Reversibility: Largely reversible after discontinuation.  
   • Monitorability: Clinical exam; patient-reported outcome.

7. Cutaneous infections (bacterial, fungal, viral)  
   • Findings: Cellulitis, onychomycosis, tinea corporis, reactivation of HSV or VZV in skin.  
   • Mechanism: Systemic immunosuppression (↓ cell-mediated immunity, ↓ neutrophil function).  
   • Adverse vs. adaptive: ADVERSE.  
   • Reversibility: Resolves with antimicrobial treatment and/or tapering.  
   • Monitorability: Clinical exam, culture/PCR as warranted.

8. Pigmentation changes (hypopigmentation or hyperpigmentation of scars/striae)  
   • Mechanism: Altered melanocyte activity secondary to cytokine changes and dermal remodeling.  
   • Adverse vs. adaptive: Generally cosmetic; minimal clinical significance.  
   • Reversibility: May persist long-term.  
   • Monitorability: Visual inspection.

Clinical pathology changes relevant to skin  
• No specific serum biomarker for skin atrophy exists.  
• Indirect: ↓ serum procollagen type I N-terminal propeptide (PINP) reported in high-dose glucocorticoid therapy (Fardet et al., Clin. Endocrinol. 2011) reflecting systemic collagen synthesis inhibition.  
• Routine labs (CBC, CRP, glucose) aid in infection/wound-healing assessment.

Data gaps / uncertainties  
• Lack of formal controlled 28-day MTD studies isolating integumentary endpoints in humans—evidence extrapolated from therapeutic cohorts and shorter-term PK/PD studies.  
• Histopathology data mostly from chronic (>8 weeks) exposure; however, animal 28-day studies support early onset of dermal atrophy.  
• Individual susceptibility (age, phototype, concomitant disease) not addressed here.

Conclusion  
At a maximally tolerated oral dose for 28 days, prednisone displays HIGH toxicodynamic activity on the integumentary system owing to potent systemic glucocorticoid actions. Principal adverse effects—dermal/epidermal atrophy, striae, purpura, impaired healing, acneiform eruptions, hair/nail changes, and heightened infection risk—are mechanistically linked to GR-mediated suppression of collagen synthesis, fibroblast/keratinocyte proliferation, and cutaneous immune competence. Most effects are adverse rather than adaptive; many are at least partially reversible and are readily monitorable in the clinic through routine physical examination, supplemented where needed by imaging or laboratory markers.

vars.chemical_and_CASRN: Dioxin or its synonyms with CAS number 1746-01-6

response.output:
Toxicological evaluation  
Substance: 2,3,7,8-Tetrachlorodibenzo-p-dioxin (TCDD; “dioxin”)  
CAS No.: 1746-01-6  
Scenario: Daily oral administration of a Maximum Tolerated Dose (MTD) to an adult human for 28 consecutive days; integumentary system end-points only.

1. Systemic availability (toxicokinetics – ADME)  
• Log KOW ≈ 6.8; highly lipophilic, virtually insoluble in water → efficient intestinal absorption when dissolved in dietary fat (human oral bioavailability 50–90 %; Poiger & Schlatter 1986; Aylward & Hays 2011).  
• Extensive distribution to lipid-rich tissues, including subcutaneous fat and the pilosebaceous unit.  
• Practically no Phase I/II metabolism at the administered dose; elimination half-life in adult humans ≈ 7–11 years (Poon et al. 2021).  
• Result: Very high systemic availability with progressive accumulation during the 28-day regimen.  

Classification:  
– Systemic availability: HIGH (persistent, bioaccumulative, well absorbed).  

2. Weight-of-evidence for integumentary toxicity  

2.1 Human data (accidental/high-dose exposures)  
• Seveso accident (Bertazzi et al. 1998; Mocarelli et al. 2008) – serum TCDD up to 56 000 ppt: chloracne, folliculitis, hyperpigmentation, nail dystrophy.  
• Victor Yushchenko poisoning (Schecter et al. 2006) – estimated body burden ≈ 100 000 pg g-1 lipid: severe chloracne and hypertrichosis within weeks.  
• Industrial workers chronically exposed (Sweeney et al. 1997) – dose-related increases in chloracne prevalence.  

2.2 Repeated-dose animal studies (oral)  
• Cynomolgus monkeys, 13 weeks, 1 ng kg-1 day-1 (Allen et al. 1980): follicular keratin plugs, atrophy of sebaceous glands.  
• Rats, 28 day gavage, 1–100 ng kg-1 day-1 (Kociba et al. 1976): epidermal hyperplasia/hyperkeratosis, alopecia, decreased sebum production.  
• Hairless mice single and repeated doses (Ema et al. 2015): rapid AhR-dependent sebaceous gland involution, PPARα/β suppression.  

3. Observed / predicted integumentary effects at a 28-day human MTD  

3.1 Functional or clinical manifestations  
a. Chloracne (numerous, persistent comedones, cysts, pustules, primarily on face, neck, trunk).  
b. Follicular hyperkeratosis and keratin plug formation → rough skin, papules.  
c. Sebaceous gland dysfunction → markedly reduced sebum output, xerosis (dry skin).  
d. Hyperpigmentation (dirty-gray/brown discoloration) and hypertrichosis in affected areas.  
e. Nail dystrophy (onycholysis, ridging) in a minority of cases.  
f. Delayed wound healing and increased susceptibility to cutaneous infections (secondary to AhR-mediated immune modulation).  

3.2 Histopathology  
• Epidermis: orthokeratotic hyperkeratosis, acanthosis, focal basal cell vacuolation.  
• Pilosebaceous unit:  
  – Marked atrophy/apoptosis of sebaceous gland lobules.  
  – Hyperplasia and hyperkeratinisation of infundibulum → keratinous cysts.  
• Dermis: mild perifollicular and perivascular lymphocytic infiltration; occasional chloracne bodies (keratin granulomas).  

3.3 Clinical pathology / biomarkers  
• Elevated plasma porphyrins (uroporphyrin) – consistent with AhR activation and cutaneous porphyria.  
• Strong induction of CYP1A1/1B1 mRNA in skin biopsies.  
• Increased serum retinoic acid (due to mobilization from liver) but reduced cutaneous retinoid signaling (RAR down-regulation).  

4. Mechanistic basis (toxicodynamics)  

Primary molecular initiating event: High-affinity binding of TCDD to the aryl hydrocarbon receptor (AhR).  
• AhR/ARNT complex translocates to nucleus → aberrant transcription of >100 genes (e.g., CYP1A1, AHRR, EREG).  
• In keratinocytes and sebocytes this produces:  
  – Suppression of sebocyte differentiation genes (PPAR-regulated) → sebaceous atrophy.  
  – Up-regulation of keratin 6, 16, 17 → hyperkeratosis; formation of comedones.  
  – Altered Notch/β-catenin signaling → infundibular hyperplasia.  
  – Increased oxidative stress and inflammatory cytokines (IL-1β, IL-8) → pustular component.  
• AhR cross-talk with melanocortin and MITF pathways → melanocyte activation → hyperpigmentation.  
• Immune modulation (Th17 skewing, Langerhans cell dysfunction) → impaired barrier defense and wound healing.  

The above chain of events corresponds to the well-described Adverse Outcome Pathway “AhR activation → chloracne-like pathology”.  

5. Adversity, reversibility and clinical monitorability  

Effect  | Adverse/Adaptive | Reversible? | Monitorable in clinic
Chloracne | Adverse | Partial; may persist months–years post-exposure but generally resolves as body burden falls | Dermatological examination; high-resolution photography; biopsy if needed
Sebaceous gland atrophy / dry skin | Adverse (functional deficit) | Largely reversible within 6–18 months | Sebumetry; skin moisture testing
Follicular hyperkeratosis | Adverse (can lead to scarring) | Reversible with decline in body burden or retinoid therapy | Visual inspection; dermatoscopy
Hyperpigmentation | Usually regarded as cosmetic but adverse by OECD definition | Gradual fading over months/years | Colorimetry; clinical observation
Nail dystrophy | Mildly adverse | Usually reversible as new nail grows (6–12 months) | Nail growth and structure monitoring
Delayed wound healing / infection risk | Adverse | Reversible shortly after exposure ends | Clinical follow-up of wounds; microbiological culture
Porphyrin elevation | Adaptive/toxicodynamic marker; rarely symptomatic | Returns to baseline once AhR activation subsides | Plasma/urine porphyrin assays

6. Uncertainties and data gaps  
• No controlled 28-day human dosing studies exist (for ethical reasons); assessment relies on high-level accidental exposures and animal repeat-dose studies. Extrapolating to an MTD assumes similar body-burden kinetics but shorter exposure period.  
• Individual variability in AhR polymorphisms may modulate sensitivity.  
• Co-exposure to other dioxin-like PCBs could aggravate or prolong skin effects.  
• Long-term carcinogenic potential of repeated short-term high AhR activation in skin remains uncertain.  

7. Overall assessment  
Given the high systemic availability and the well-established skin-specific AOP for TCDD, daily oral administration of an MTD for 28 days in an adult human would almost certainly produce pronounced integumentary toxicity. Chloracne and related pilosebaceous lesions represent the critical, clearly adverse end-points. Most manifestations are ultimately reversible but may require months to years because of the compound’s extraordinary persistence. All major effects are readily monitorable in standard clinical dermatological practice.

tests.prompt: Describe the known or predicted toxicological effects (if any) on the integumentary system of administering a maximum tolerated dose of {chemical_and_CASRN} to an adult human for 28 days where the dose is repeated each day by the oral route. Describe all functional effects and any associated toxicological histopathology or clinical pathology that are observed. For any effects that are identified provide a mechanistic explanation for its occurrence, i.e., why and how did the chemical causes the toxicity. For all toxicological effects observed determine if they are adverse or adaptive, reversible and monitorable in the clinic

vars.chemical_and_CASRN: Benzene or its synonyms with CAS number 71-43-2

response.output:
Benzene (CAS 71-43-2) – Expected effects on the integumentary system after daily oral administration of a Maximum-Tolerated-Dose (MTD) for 28 days in an adult human  

---------------------------------------------------------------------
1  Toxicokinetic profile (brief)  
• Absorption: Rapid and extensive after oral exposure in rodents and humans (≈ 90 %; ECHA, 2023).  
• Distribution: Highly lipophilic (logKow 2.13) – distributes to fatty tissue, bone marrow and skin.  
• Metabolism: Predominantly hepatic CYP2E1 → phenol, hydroquinone, catechol, 1,4-benzoquinone; metabolites reach bone marrow and peripheral organs.  
• Elimination: Pulmonary exhalation of parent benzene and urinary excretion of conjugated metabolites.  
Systemic availability: High  

---------------------------------------------------------------------
2  Reported 28-day oral toxicity in animals (key for read-across)  
• Mouse & rat 28-day/90-day gavage studies (NTP 1986, ECHA dossier): MTD 200-400 mg kg-1 day-1.  
• Critical findings: Dose–dependent depression of erythroid, myeloid and megakaryocytic cell lines, leading to anemia, leukopenia and thrombocytopenia.  
• No primary microscopic skin lesions were recorded, but secondary hemorrhagic foci in the dermis were occasionally noted at high doses, correlating with thrombocytopenia.  

---------------------------------------------------------------------
3  Expected integumentary manifestations in humans at an MTD  
Benzene is not a classical skin toxicant when given orally; any integumentary effects are secondary to its well-established myelotoxicity. The table summarises the functional changes, mechanism, adversity and clinical management.

1. Ecchymoses / Petechiae (small dermal hemorrhages)  
• Functional effect: Spontaneous bruising, petechial rash, gingival bleeding.  
• Histopathology/clinical pathology: Dermal/extramucosal red cell extravasation; platelet count ↓, coagulation times ↑.  
• Mechanism: Benzene metabolites (hydroquinone, benzoquinone) destroy megakaryocytic precursors in bone marrow → thrombocytopenia → loss of primary hemostasis → capillary bleeding into skin.  
• Adverse or adaptive? Adverse.  
• Reversible? Generally reversible if exposure stops early; prolonged aplasia may become irreversible.  
• Monitorable? Yes – complete blood count (CBC), skin exam, bleeding time.

2. Pallor of skin and mucosae  
• Functional effect: Pale skin and nail beds.  
• Histopathology/clinical pathology: Macrocytic or normocytic anemia; reticulocyte count ↓.  
• Mechanism: Stem-cell injury → erythroid hypoplasia → reduced hemoglobin.  
• Adverse or adaptive? Adverse.  
• Reversible? Usually reversible within weeks after cessation if bone marrow not irreversibly damaged.  
• Monitorable? CBC (Hb/Hct), clinical examination.

3. Increased frequency of cutaneous and nail infections; delayed wound healing  
• Functional effect: Folliculitis, cellulitis, paronychia; slow closure of minor cuts.  
• Histopathology/clinical pathology: Neutropenia; histology would show minimal inflammatory infiltrate despite infection.  
• Mechanism: Benzene metabolite-induced oxidative DNA/protein damage in myeloid progenitors → neutropenia → impaired innate immunity.  
• Adverse or adaptive? Adverse.  
• Reversible? Yes, if bone-marrow recovery occurs; infections themselves resolve with treatment.  
• Monitorable? CBC with differential; culture of lesions.

4. Xerosis (“dry skin”) or defatting dermatitis (low incidence)  
• Functional effect: Dry, scaly skin or mild irritant dermatitis.  
• Mechanism: Systemic contribution is uncertain; more often seen after direct dermal contact, but a small amount of parent benzene is excreted unchanged in sweat, which may slightly defat stratum corneum.  
• Adverse or adaptive? Mild, mostly adaptive/irritant.  
• Reversible? Yes, rapidly with emollients and cessation.  
• Monitorable? Clinical skin inspection.

No evidence for: Alopecia, nail dystrophy, pigmentary changes or primary epidermal necrosis at humane MTDs.

---------------------------------------------------------------------
4  Mechanistic considerations  
• Key event: Bioactivation in liver → phenolic metabolites transported to bone marrow.  
• Molecular initiating event: Metabolite–mediated oxidative stress and covalent binding in hematopoietic stem cells.  
• Downstream events: Stem cell apoptosis → cytopenias → secondary cutaneous manifestations.  
• Adverse Outcome Pathway link: “Bone-marrow toxicity leading to hemorrhage and infection” (OECD AOP 318).

---------------------------------------------------------------------
5  Weight-of-evidence conclusion  
Toxicodynamic activity toward the integumentary system: Medium (indirect/secondary).  
Systemic availability: High.  

Under a 28-day repeated-dose oral MTD scenario, benzene does not act as a direct skin poison, but its profound myelotoxicity produces clinically relevant and adverse integumentary sequelae (bruising, pallor, infection risk, delayed wound repair). These effects are largely reversible and easily monitorable through routine hematology and clinical skin evaluation, although persistent exposure carries a risk of irreversible marrow aplasia and subsequent chronic skin complications.

---------------------------------------------------------------------
Key sources  
1. ATSDR. Toxicological Profile for Benzene. U.S. DHHS, 2007.  
2. ECHA Registration Dossier – Benzene (accessed 2023).  
3. NTP. Toxicology and Carcinogenesis Studies of Benzene (Technical Report No. 301), 1986.  
4. IARC Monograph 120: Benzene, 2018.  
5. Snyder R. Leukemia and benzene. Int J Environ Res Public Health, 2012.  
6. OECD AOP wiki: Haematopoietic stem cell toxicity leading to marrow aplasia and infection, 2022.

Data gaps/uncertainties  
• No controlled human 28-day oral studies for obvious ethical reasons; assessment relies on animal studies and occupational case reports (predominantly inhalation).  
• Direct dermal histopathology after oral dosing is poorly documented; conclusions are extrapolated from systemic effects.
