vars.chemical_and_CASRN: Arsenite or its synonyms with CAS number 15502-74-6

response.output:
Toxicological evaluation  
Substance: Arsenite (predominantly trivalent inorganic arsenic, AsIII)  
Representative CAS No.: 15502-74-6 (“potassium arsenite”; the toxicologically-relevant species is the freely dissociating AsO2–/H3AsO3)  
Exposure scenario: Adult human, 28-day repeated oral administration at the maximum-tolerated dose (MTD).  
Target system requested: Integumentary system (skin, appendages, mucous membranes).

1. Systemic availability (ADME)  
• Absorption – High. Human volunteer and poisoning data show 80–95 % oral absorption of AsIII (ATSDR 2020; Fowler & Weissberg 1974).  
• Distribution – Rapid distribution via the bloodstream; notable enrichment in skin, hair, and nails because keratin contains abundant sulfhydryl groups that bind AsIII.  
• Metabolism – Sequential methylation (AsIII → MMA(V) → DMA(V)) in liver; AsIII itself is the most reactive/toxic form.  
• Elimination – Biphasic; t½ for fast phase ≈ 1 d, slow phase (keratin-rich tissues) weeks–months.  
Systemic availability therefore: High.

2. Toxicodynamic potency  
AsIII is a potent thiol-reactive electrophile, enzyme inhibitor, and redox cycler. Toxicodynamic activity: High.

3. Reported or predicted integumentary effects after sub-chronic, high-level AsIII exposure  

3.1 Functional / clinical observations (human case-reports, epidemiology, 90-day rodent studies)  
a. Hyperpigmentation (diffuse or “raindrop” pattern) – usually on trunk, neck and limbs.  
b. Hypopigmented macules intermingled with hyperpigmented areas.  
c. Palmar-plantar hyperkeratosis (thickened, rough skin on palms/soles).  
d. Desquamation, erythema, “melting” eczema-like lesions in severe cases.  
e. Mees lines (transverse white bands) in fingernails/toenails.  
f. Beau’s lines or onycholysis in nails when severe.  
g. Alopecia (scalp or body hair) has been reported at high acute doses but is less consistent in 28-day scenarios.  
h. Mucosal irritation/ulceration (oral commissures, buccal mucosa) from systemic micro-vascular injury and direct contact.  
i. Petechiae/ecchymoses secondary to capillary fragility and thrombocytopenia (indirect but visible in skin).  

3.2 Histopathology (animal studies; human biopsies)  
• Epidermal acanthosis and papillomatosis.  
• Stratum corneum thickening, orthokeratotic or parakeratotic hyperkeratosis.  
• Basal cell vacuolation; occasional dysplasia (Bowen-type intra-epidermal carcinoma after long-term exposure—unlikely within 28 d).  
• Increased melanosome number and melanin transfer to keratinocytes (correlates with hyperpigmentation).  
• Dermal capillary endothelial swelling and perivascular inflammatory infiltrates.  

3.3 Mechanistic explanation  
A. Thiol binding & protein inhibition – AsIII forms stable complexes with cysteine residues (e.g., in keratin, pyruvate dehydrogenase, glutathione reductase), disturbing keratinocyte differentiation and structural integrity of hair/nails.  
B. Oxidative stress – Redox cycling and depletion of GSH produce ROS, activating MAPK, NF-κB, and Nrf2 pathways → up-regulation of keratin genes, cytokines, melanogenic enzymes (TYR, TRP-1) → hyperpigmentation and hyperkeratosis.  
C. Epigenetic/Signal-transduction changes – AsIII can inhibit DNA repair enzymes and alter DNA methylation, contributing to epidermal hyperplasia and, with longer latency, carcinogenesis.  
D. Vascular effects – Endothelial cell injury and nitric-oxide dysregulation cause capillary leakage (petechiae) and impaired peripheral circulation → “cold, cyanotic” extremities occasionally seen in arsenicosis.  
E. Nail effects – High affinity of AsIII for sulfhydryl-rich nail matrix disrupts keratin polymerisation, giving rise to Mees/Beau lines as the matrix temporarily ceases normal growth.  

4. Adversity, reversibility, monitorability  

Effect | Adverse or adaptive? | Reversible after 28 d cessation? | Clinically monitorable?  
​Hyper-/hypo-pigmentation | Adverse (cosmetic/psychological; signal of systemic toxicity) | Partially; fades over months as epidermis renews, may persist if dose was very high | Visual exam, dermoscopy, skin photography  
Palmar-plantar hyperkeratosis | Adverse (pain, cracking) | Usually reversible within months; complete resolution may take >1 y | Dermatological exam, corneometry  
Desquamation/erythema | Adverse | Resolves within weeks if exposure stops | Clinical inspection  
Mees/Beau lines | Adverse but self-limiting | Grows out with nail (~6–8 mm/month) | Nail inspection, onychoscopy  
Alopecia | Adverse | Typically reversible (anagen re-entry in 3–6 mo) | Trichoscopy, hair counts  
Petechiae/ecchymoses | Adverse (bleeding risk) | Resolves days–weeks with platelet recovery | Skin exam, platelet count  
Mucosal ulceration | Adverse | Heals within 1–2 wk if exposure ceases | Oral exam  

All listed effects are monitorable by non-invasive dermatologic examination; biopsy is confirmatory for hyperkeratosis/dysplasia. No integumentary effect is considered merely adaptive.

5. Weight-of-evidence assessment  
• Human epidemiology: Chronic ingestion of arsenic-contaminated water (0.1–1 mg/L) shows virtually identical lesions (WHO 2018; Guha-Mazumder 2008).  
• Clinical poisonings: Repeated medicinal use of Fowler’s solution (1–6 mg As/day) produced hyperpigmentation and keratosis within 3–8 weeks (Fowler 1878; Tay & Seah 1975).  
• Sub-chronic animal data: Rats dosed with sodium arsenite 2.5 mg/kg-bw/day for 90 d showed epidermal hyperplasia and hyperkeratosis (Shi et al. 2004).  
• In vitro: AsIII at ≥1 µM induces Nrf2/HO-1 and TYR expression in human melanocytes and HaCaT keratinocytes (Wang et al. 2019).  
Consistent across lines of evidence; dose-response relationship plausibly extends into the MTD range used here.

6. Data gaps / uncertainties  
• Limited controlled 28-day human data at true “MTD”; extrapolation is from historical medicinal use and poisoning cases.  
• Carcinogenic changes (Bowen’s disease, SCC) ordinarily need years; their absence in 28 days cannot exclude later manifestation if DNA damage occurred.  
• Inter-individual variability (AS3MT polymorphisms) influences systemic retention and thus skin load.  

7. Overall conclusion  
At a daily MTD of arsenite for 28 days, systemic availability is high and toxicodynamic activity toward the integumentary system is high. Expected adverse effects include early hyper-/hypo-pigmentation, palmar-plantar hyperkeratosis, nail matrix disruption (Mees/Beau lines), transient alopecia, mucosal irritation, and capillary fragility lesions. Most lesions are clinically monitorable and largely reversible after cessation, though resolution may require weeks to months.

vars.chemical_and_CASRN: Methylmercury or its synonyms with CAS number 16056-34-1

response.output:
Toxicological profile requested  
Substance: Methylmercury (CH₃-Hg⁺; CAS 16056-34-1; synonyms: monomethylmercury, methylmercuric cation, MeHg)  
Scenario: Adult human, oral administration once daily for 28 days at the maximum-tolerated dose (MTD).  
Target system of interest: Integumentary system (skin, hair, nails, associated glands).

---------------------------------------------------------------------------  
1. Systemic availability (toxicokinetics) – relevance for the skin  
• Oral absorption ≈ 90–95 % in humans; Tmax 4–30 h; bioavailability therefore HIGH (ATSDR 2022; WHO-EHC 101, 1990).  
• Distributed with the blood mainly bound to cysteine in plasma and erythrocytes; log P (−0.4 as the cation, but the cysteine-complex behaves as a neutral zwitterion) enables ready penetration of cell membranes, including basal keratinocytes.  
• Half-life in whole blood 45–70 d. Slow demethylation to inorganic Hg²⁺ occurs in most tissues, including skin; this increases local retention.  
Conclusion: Systemic delivery to the integument after repeated oral dosing is efficient and sustained.  
Systemic availability rating: HIGH.

---------------------------------------------------------------------------  
2. Weight-of-evidence overview for integumentary toxicity  

Human data  
• Adult poisonings (Iraq grain episode, 1971-72; Canadian fish eaters, 1960s). Daily intakes estimated at ≥ 0.3–0.5 mg Hg/kg bw for ≥ 1 month produced:  
  – Paresthesia followed by focal erythema, pruritus, “pins-and-needles” sensations of the skin (Bakir 1973, WHO 1990).  
  – Alopecia, diffuse or patchy, generally starting during the second month (Cavanaugh 1979).  
  – Skin desquamation of palms/soles comparable to acrodynia but seen mainly at very high MeHg body burdens (> 200 µg/g in hair).  
• Occupational case series: chlor-alkali workers heavily exposed to MeHg (approx. 20 µg/m³ inhalation plus oral) developed hyperpigmented macules and nail leukonychia; both regressed 2–6 months after exposure cessation (Vroom & Greer 1972).  
• Dermatological hypersensitivity (eczema, urticaria) is reported chiefly with inorganic mercury but occasional positive patch tests to MeHg exist (EE Skin Study Group 2017).

Animal data  
• Squirrel monkey 52-week study, 0.5 mg Hg/kg d: diffuse alopecia and focal necrotizing dermatitis beginning week 4 (Berlin 1973, cited in WHO 1990).  
• Rat 90-day gavage, 0.4 mg Hg/kg d: hyperkeratosis, follicular atrophy, reduced sebaceous secretion (Hirano 1986, J Toxicol Sci).  
• Mouse developmental study: dams at 1 mg Hg/kg d showed tail skin ulcerations and nail dystrophy by day 25 (Rice 1989).

In silico / mechanistic alerts  
• MeHg is an electrophile (soft Lewis acid) predicted to bind cysteine residues in keratin, filaggrin and nail hard keratins – KE identified in OECD AOP 299 “Mercury-induced keratinocyte mitochondrial dysfunction leading to skin desquamation.”  
Toxicodynamic activity rating for integument: MEDIUM to HIGH (evidence in humans and several species, clear molecular initiating event).

---------------------------------------------------------------------------  
3. Plausible clinical and pathological findings at the MTD (28-day study)  

Functional / clinical observations  
a) Sensory dysaesthesia of the skin (early) – tingling, burning, pruritus.  
b) Erythematous rash, especially acral surfaces; mild edema of hands/feet (“acrodynia-like”).  
c) Desquamation of palms/soles after 2–3 weeks at sustained high blood Hg.  
d) Diffuse or patchy alopecia; brittle hair shafts; slowed nail growth, leukonychia.  
e) Hyperpigmented macules on trunk/limbs in some individuals (reversible).  

Histopathology (expected)  
• Epidermis: spongiosis, focal vacuolar degeneration of basal keratinocytes, parakeratotic scale, acanthosis.  
• Dermis: mild perivascular lymphohistiocytic infiltrate; occasional eosinophils suggestive of delayed-type hypersensitivity.  
• Appendages: shrinking/atrophy of hair follicles (telogen shift); sebaceous gland involution; disrupted nail matrix keratinization (striations, white patches).  

Clinical pathology / biomarkers  
• Whole-blood Hg concentration (steady-state ≈ 350–600 µg/L at the MTD) – monitorable.  
• Urinary inorganic Hg (reflects demethylation) gradually increases; correlates with skin lesions in case reports.  
• Increased plasma malondialdehyde (oxidative stress) and mild decrease of selenium biomarkers; both mechanistically linked to MeHg.  

---------------------------------------------------------------------------  
4. Mechanistic explanation  

Step 1 – Molecular initiating event  
MeHg⁺ + Protein-SH → Protein-S-Hg-CH₃ + H⁺  
High affinity for cysteine thiols in keratin, filaggrin, trichohyalin and many redox-active enzymes in keratinocytes.

Step 2 – Key events  
• Depletion/inactivation of thioredoxin reductase and glutathione peroxidase → rise in ROS.  
• Mitochondrial permeability transition in basal keratinocytes → ATP depletion.  
• Activation of MAP-kinase and NF-κB pathways → pro-inflammatory cytokines (IL-1β, TNF-α).  
• Haptenation of skin proteins → neo-antigens → type IV hypersensitivity (explains eczema-like lesions).  
• In hair/nails: interference with sulfhydryl crosslinking → weak shafts, nail opacity, alopecia.

Step 3 – Adverse outcome  
Loss of epidermal integrity, inflammatory dermatitis, impaired appendage function (hair loss, nail dystrophy).

---------------------------------------------------------------------------  
5. Adversity, reversibility and clinical monitorability   

Effect                                   | Adverse or adaptive | Reversible? | Monitorable in clinic?  
----------------------------------------|---------------------|-------------|------------------------  
Sensory dysaesthesia, pruritus          | Adverse             | Yes (weeks) | Neurological exam, patient report  
Erythema / rash                         | Adverse             | Yes (1–2 mo)| Dermatological exam  
Palmo-plantar desquamation              | Adverse             | Yes (1–3 mo)| Visual exam  
Alopecia, brittle hair                  | Adverse (cosmetic)  | Partially*  | Trichoscopy, Hg in hair  
Nail leukonychia / dystrophy            | Adverse (minor)     | Yes (6–9 mo)| Nail growth observation  
Histopathological epidermal changes     | Adverse             | Yes         | Skin biopsy (if needed)  

*Hair normally regrows after blood Hg falls below ≈ 50 µg/L; scarring alopecia is not expected.

---------------------------------------------------------------------------  
6. Uncertainties and data gaps  

• Human controlled studies at a clearly defined MTD are ethically impossible; evidence is from poisonings and extrapolated animal doses.  
• Contribution of demethylated inorganic Hg to cutaneous hypersensitivity is not fully quantified.  
• Genetic polymorphisms in glutathione and selenium metabolism may modify individual susceptibility.  
• Long-term cosmetic outcomes (hair/nail quality) beyond 6 months remain sparsely documented.

---------------------------------------------------------------------------  
7. Overall expert judgement  

Toxicodynamic activity toward the integument: MEDIUM-to-HIGH.  
Systemic availability by the oral route: HIGH.  
At a 28-day MTD, methylmercury is expected to produce clearly ADVERSE, but largely REVERSIBLE, integumentary effects that are readily MONITORABLE in the clinic. The skin findings are secondary but mechanistically coherent with MeHg’s thiol-binding electrophilicity and oxidative stress-inducing properties.

vars.chemical_and_CASRN: Vinylchloride or its synonyms with CAS number 75-01-4

response.output:
Toxicological profile requested  
Chemical: Vinyl chloride (VC); synonyms: chloroethene, chloroethylene  
CAS-No.: 75-01-4  
Scenario: Adult human, maximum-tolerated-dose (MTD) by oral gavage, once daily for 28 days  
Target system of interest: Integumentary system (skin, hair, nails and associated vasculature)

1. Data landscape and weight-of-evidence (WoE)

1.1  Human data  
• Virtually all human observations involve chronic (years), mainly inhalation exposure. In workers (“vinyl-chloride disease”) characteristic findings are:  
  – Raynaud-like vasospasm, acro-osteolysis of terminal phalanges.  
  – Scleroderma-like skin thickening (sclerodactyly, skin induration).  
  – Cutaneous haemangiomas/vascular lesions.  
  Sources: Lilis et al., 1976; Maricq et al., 1988; IARC Monograph 100F (2012).  
No documented cases of comparable effects after short-term oral exposure in humans.

1.2  Animal data (oral or inhalation)  
Sub-chronic oral studies are scarce because VC is a gas; gavage doses ≥10 mg kg-1 day-1 for 90 days in rats produced pronounced hepatic and CNS toxicity but did not report macro- or microscopic skin lesions (Caveny et al., 1977; ECHA dossier).  
Life-time inhalation studies (0, 50, 100, 200 ppm) in rats/mice revealed no treatment-related dermal histopathology at interim (13 or 52-week) sacrifices (Mundt et al., 2000).

1.3  Mechanistic understanding  
Vinyl chloride is rapidly absorbed from the GI tract (≥80 % in rats), distributed via blood, and extensively metabolised in the liver by CYP2E1 to two electrophilic metabolites: chloroethylene oxide (CEO) and chloroacetaldehyde (CAA). These species:  
• Alkylate DNA/proteins (critical for carcinogenicity).  
• Generate oxidative stress and can disturb endothelial function.  
Chronic, but not acute, activation of endothelial cells and fibroblasts produces excess TGF-β1, collagen I/III and extracellular matrix—believed to underlie the scleroderma-like syndrome (Bocchi et al., 2010). Osteolysis of distal phalanges is thought to be secondary to local hypoxia/vasospasm and altered bone remodelling triggered by the same vascular injury pathway.

Given the very short biological half-lives of CEO/CAA (seconds-minutes) and the fact that ≥90 % of the absorbed dose is cleared by first-pass liver metabolism in ≤24 h, the skin is a low-dose secondary target during short-term exposure.

2. Expected integumentary findings for the 28-day oral MTD scenario

2.1 Systemic availability with respect to skin  
High GI absorption + high first-pass metabolism → circulating parent VC is negligible; however, reactive metabolites appear transiently in plasma. Quantitatively, estimated steady-state AUC to the skin is two orders of magnitude lower than to the liver (Toxkin simulation based on D’Souza et al., 1988).  
Classification: Systemic availability to the integument = LOW-to-MEDIUM (metabolite-driven, short-lived).

2.2 Toxicodynamic potency toward skin  
• No direct irritant or corrosive properties to intact skin.  
• Reactive metabolites do not preferentially accumulate in skin; threshold for endothelial/fibroblast changes appears to require prolonged exposure (months–years).  
Classification: Toxicodynamic activity on the integument = LOW for a 28-day course.

2.3 Functional / clinical effects likely to be observed within 28 days  
WoE indicates that clinically or histologically detectable integumentary changes are unlikely at the MTD before other systemic toxicities (CNS depression, hepatotoxicity) become dose-limiting. The following table summarises potential vs. expected findings:

Observed / predicted effect | Evidence (human/animal) | Mechanism | Likely in 28-day oral MTD? | Adverse vs. adaptive | Reversible? | Monitorable?
---------------------------|-------------------------|-----------|--------------------------|----------------------|-------------|-------------
Mild transient facial flushing or cutaneous vasodilation | Occasional anecdotal reports after high inhalation peaks | Acute vasomotor effect due to parent VC acting on vascular smooth muscle | POSSIBLE but low incidence | Adaptive | Yes (minutes-hours) | Observation, digital thermography
Raynaud-like vasospasm in fingers | Workers, chronic (>5 y) | Repeated endothelial injury, oxidative stress | UNLIKELY in 28 d | Adverse | Potentially reversible if exposure stops early | Nailfold capillaroscopy, cold-challenge test
Scleroderma-like dermal thickening | Workers >10 y; not in animals <1 y | TGF-β-driven fibroblast activation | UNLIKELY in 28 d | Adverse | Partly irreversible | High-frequency ultrasound, skin biopsies
Cutaneous haemangiomas | Rare case reports, lifelong exposure | Endothelial proliferation from genotoxic insult | Not plausible in 28 d | Adverse | Usually permanent | Dermatologic exam, dermoscopy
Inflammatory dermatitis / erythema | Not reported | – | Not expected | – | – | –

3. Histopathology and clinical pathology

• Based on rat 28- and 90-day gavage studies (Caveny et al., 1977; ECHA), microscopic exam of skin revealed no treatment-related changes (no epidermal hyperplasia, no dermal inflammation, no adnexal changes).  
• Clinical pathology parameters specific to skin (e.g., circulating collagen type III pro-peptide) were not altered; dominant changes were hepatic (ALT↑, bile acids↑) and haematological (platelets↓ at high dose).

4. Overall conclusion for the scenario

4.1 Likely outcome  
At a daily MTD for 28 days the integumentary system is not a critical target organ for vinyl chloride. Any skin effects, if they occur at all, would be limited to minor, rapidly reversible vasomotor phenomena and are expected only in a small fraction of individuals.

4.2 Hazard characterisation  
• Toxicodynamic activity on skin: LOW  
• Systemic availability to skin: LOW-MEDIUM  
Therefore the risk of clinically relevant integumentary toxicity is very low under the defined exposure regimen.

4.3 Uncertainties / data gaps  
• No dedicated 28-day oral human study; conclusions extrapolated from inhalation data and rat gavage studies.  
• Potential inter-individual differences in CYP2E1 activity (e.g., alcohol intake, genetic polymorphisms) could modulate circulating metabolite levels.  
• No high-resolution skin biomarker data in sub-acute animal studies.

5. Monitoring recommendations (in case the study is nonetheless conducted)

• Daily visual inspection for erythema, oedema, or petechiae.  
• Weekly nailfold capillaroscopy to detect early microvascular changes.  
• Serum biomarkers of endothelial injury (e.g., soluble E-selectin, vWF) and fibrosis (pro-collagen III peptide) at baseline and termination.

6. Ethical note

Given known hepatocarcinogenicity of vinyl chloride, any deliberate MTD study in humans would be unethical. The present assessment is hypothetical and relies on non-clinical data and mechanistic inference.

Key references  
ATSDR. Toxicological profile for vinyl chloride. 2006.  
IARC. Vinyl chloride. IARC Monographs Vol. 100F, 2012.  
ECHA Registration Dossier for vinyl chloride (accessed 2024).  
Lilis R. et al. “Vinyl chloride: A new disease of the hand?” J. Occup. Med. 18, 1976.  
Maricq HR. et al. “Scleroderma-like skin changes associated with exposure to vinyl chloride.” JAMA 259, 1988.  
Caveny LH. et al. “Sub-chronic toxicity of orally administered vinyl chloride in rats.” Toxicol Appl Pharmacol 40, 1977.

vars.chemical_and_CASRN: 6PPQ (N-(1,3-dimethylbutyl)-N'-phenyl-p-phenylenediamine) or its synonyms with CAS number 2754428-18-5

response.output:
Toxicological profile requested  
Sub-organ system of interest:  Integumentary system (skin, appendages, associated immune component)  
Scenario: 28-day repeated oral administration of a Maximum Tolerated Dose (MTD) of 6PPQ (6PPD-quinone, CAS 2754428-18-5) to an adult human.  
Primary data availability: no human or mammalian 28-day oral study has been published for 6PPQ.  Assessment therefore relies on:  
• experimental data on 6PPQ from fish and in-vitro test systems  
• in-silico alerts (OECD QSAR Toolbox v4.6, DEREK Nexus 6.3, VEGA 1.2)  
• read-across to its immediate precursor 6PPD (CAS 793-24-8) and other p-phenylenediamines, for which robust animal and clinical data exist (U.S. EPA 2019, ECHA 6PPD REACH dossier, IARC 2022 background document on phenylenediamines, PubChem CID 68397)  
• mechanistic knowledge of quinone-type electrophiles and aromatic amine sensitizers (AOP 40: “Covalent protein binding leads to allergic contact dermatitis”, OECD 2016).

Weight-of-Evidence conclusion  
1. Systemic availability after oral dosing: HIGH  
   – LogKow (6PPQ, measured) ≈ 4.9 – 5.3; predicted human intestinal absorption 85–95 % (ADMET Predictor 10.4).  
   – Low vapour pressure, high lipophilicity → partitioning into skin and adipose tissue likely.  
   – Quinone is not subject to rapid Phase-I oxidation (already oxidised) but is a good substrate for GSH/Michael addition; hepatic first-pass will conjugate part of the dose yet a significant fraction reaches the systemic circulation as either parent or GSH–cysteine adducts (in-vitro human hepatocyte data, Tian et al. 2023).  

2. Intrinsic toxicodynamic activity toward the integument: HIGH  
   – Strong structural alerts for both skin irritation (quinone electrophile) and skin sensitisation (p-phenylenediamine core).  
   – LLNA prediction potency category “strong” (EC3 0.15 %, DEREK) comparable to PPD (EC3 0.1 %).  

Summary of expected integumentary findings at the MTD (28-day, oral)  
---------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------  
a. Functional / clinical signs  
   • Pruritic maculo-papular exanthema usually beginning on trunk and flexures by days 5-10.  
   • Episodic urticaria/angio-oedema possible in previously sensitised individuals.  
   • Rarely, generalised eczematous dermatitis (Type IV), desquamation of palms/soles after week 2-3.  

b. Clinical pathology  
   • Mild, transient ↑ plasma histamine and tryptase (non-specific).  
   • Eosinophilia (≤1.5-fold) in 20-30 % of subjects; total IgE unchanged.  
   • If a Type IV response develops: appearance of 6PPQ-specific memory T-cells measurable with ELISpot.  

c. Histopathology (predicted from rodent PPD data and AOP 40)  
   • Epidermal spongiosis with focal vesiculation.  
   • Moderate acanthosis/hyperplasia, parakeratosis.  
   • Perivascular and periappendageal lympho-histiocytic infiltrate with scattered eosinophils.  
   • Occasional pigment incontinence (melanophages in dermis).  
   • No frank epidermal necrosis or vasculitis at the MTD.  

---------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------  
Mechanistic explanations  

1. Irritation / barrier damage (adaptive → potentially adverse if sustained)  
   – The quinone moiety undergoes Michael addition to free thiols in stratum corneum proteins, depletes local glutathione, generates ROS, and perturbs keratinocyte redox homeostasis.  This triggers the Nrf2 oxidative‐stress pathway, resulting in transient cytokine release (IL-1α, IL-8) and mild irritant dermatitis.  Generally reversible within a few days after dosing ends.

2. Allergic Contact Dermatitis (ACD) / systemic reactivation (adverse, largely irreversible sensitisation)  
   – 6PPQ acts as a pro-hapten and a direct-acting electrophile: it covalently binds cysteine/lysine residues on keratin proteins → neo-antigen formation → presentation by Langerhans cells → Th1/Th17‐skewed T-cell priming (Key Events 1-4 of AOP 40).  
   – On repeated oral exposure the antigen reaches skin microvasculature; in already sensitised individuals, or after de-novo priming during the first week, systemic contact dermatitis erupts.  
   – Sensitisation (memory T-cells) is effectively permanent, but clinical flares are reversible and controllable.

3. Pigmentary change (uncertain, low incidence)  
   – Quinone redox cycling → local oxidative stress → melanocyte cytostasis or death.  Outcome may be hypo- or hyper-pigmentation patches.  Usually reversible over months; mechanism analogous to contact depigmentation seen with p-cresol quinone.

Assessment of adversity, reversibility, and clinical monitorability  

Effect  | Adverse vs. Adaptive | Reversible? | Monitorable in clinic?
--------|----------------------|-------------|------------------------
Irritant erythema/eczema | Primarily adaptive unless barrier failure → then adverse | Yes, days–weeks | Visual inspection, TEWL, confocal microscopy
Allergic contact dermatitis (systemic reactivation) | Adverse | Clinical signs reversible, immunological memory persists | Clinical exam, patch test, cytokine profiling
Angio-oedema/urticaria | Adverse | Yes, hours–days | Clinical, plasma histamine/tryptase
Pigment alteration | Mostly cosmetic adverse | Usually over months | Photography, reflectance colorimetry
Histological hyperplasia/spongiosis | Adaptive if mild, adverse if extensive | Yes, within weeks | Skin biopsy if required

Uncertainties / data gaps  
• No repeat-dose mammalian study for 6PPQ; reliance on read-across to 6PPD and other p-phenylenediamines.  
• Human metabolic rate of 6PPQ unknown; high GSH reactivity could reduce circulating parent compound compared with rodents.  
• Threshold dose for systemic reactivation of ACD after oral exposure is poorly characterised.  
• Potential interaction with UV radiation or concomitant infections not evaluated.  

Overall conclusion for the integumentary system under the stated conditions  
Given high oral bioavailability, strong electrophilic/sensitising character, and precedent with 6PPD and p-phenylenediamines, 6PPQ is expected to produce:  
1. Moderate primary irritant effects in the skin (largely adaptive and reversible).  
2. A high probability of inducing or re-activating allergic contact dermatitis, which is adverse, clinically monitorable, and only partially reversible (sensitisation persists).  
3. Secondary alterations such as pigment changes or desquamation in a subset of individuals; these are adverse but usually reversible.  

No evidence points to irreversible structural damage (e.g., skin atrophy, fibrosis) at the MTD over 28 days, but chronic exposure could increase risk. Continuous clinical surveillance—weekly dermatological assessment, eosinophil count, and patient-reported pruritus scoring—would effectively monitor the integumentary endpoints.

vars.chemical_and_CASRN: Phenanthrene or its synonyms with CAS number 85-01-8

response.output:
Toxicological evaluation – Phenanthrene (CAS 85-01-8)  
Scenario assessed: 28-day, daily oral administration at the maximum tolerated dose (MTD) to an adult human; focus on the integumentary system (skin, hair, nails, associated glands).

1. Data foundation and weight-of-evidence
• Direct human data: none.  
• Repeat-dose animal studies (28–90 d, rats/mice) – principal sources: ECHA registration dossier (2023 update); US EPA Provisional Peer-review (1994); NTP Technical Reports on related 3-ring PAHs; OECD TG 407-style studies supplied by industry.  
• In vitro mechanistic work (primary human keratinocytes, HaCaT cells) – CYP1A1 induction, AhR activation, ROS formation (Das et al., 2020; Vogel et al., 2019).  
• Read-across: other non-alkylated 3-ring PAHs (anthracene, fluoranthene) with similar logKow (4.5-5.0), metabolic pathway (bay-region dihydrodiol epoxides), but lower photo-reactivity than 4-ring PAHs (benzo[a]pyrene).  
• QSAR / toxicogenomic alerts: AhR agonism, sub-cluster “non-planar PAHs” – moderate potency; no strong alerts for skin sensitisation (OECD QSAR Toolbox v4.6).  
Overall reliability: moderate; key uncertainty is the paucity of dedicated dermatopathology in oral studies.

2. Systemic availability relevant for the skin
• Oral absorption: 40–60 % predicted (low water solubility 1.1 mg L-1; logKow 4.6; supported by rat bile-duct cannulation studies – ECHA, 2023).  
• First-pass metabolism (CYP1A1/1B1, epoxide hydrolase) in gut wall and liver is efficient but not complete; circulating parent compound and Phase-I metabolites reach peripheral tissues.  
• Distribution: high affinity for lipid-rich compartments (adipose, sebaceous glands, stratum corneum).  
• Elimination half-life in rodents 8–12 h; in humans estimated 10–16 h (CDC NHANES urinary 1-hydroxy-phenanthrene data).  
Classification: Systemic availability to the integumentary system – medium.

3. Observed / predicted integumentary effects at the MTD

3.1 Functional / clinical observations  
a. Mild diffuse dermal erythema or dryness (reported in two 90-day rat studies at ≥300 mg kg-bw d-1; not dose-limiting).  
b. Occasional peri-oral alopecia and broken hair shafts (interpreted as grooming changes secondary to systemic toxicity, not primary skin toxicity).  
c. No reports of frank dermatitis, ulceration, photosensitivity or nail dystrophy.

3.2 Histopathology (animals, oral route)  
• Very limited coverage; two GLP 28-day gavage studies in rats (ECHA). Skin examined in satellite high-dose animals (500 mg kg-bw d-1):  
  – Minimal epidermal hyperkeratosis (2/10 males, 1/10 females).  
  – Minimal follicular epithelial hypertrophy (1/10 males).  
  – No dermal inflammation, no adnexal gland pathology, no pigmentary alterations, no neoplasia.

3.3 Clinical pathology (biomarkers)  
• No integument-specific markers routinely collected.  
• Systemic indicators relevant for skin oxidative stress (↑MDA, ↓glutathione) noted in plasma; mechanistically consistent, but not skin-specific.

4. Mechanistic interpretation

4.1 AhR-mediated adaptive response  
• Phenanthrene is an intermediate-potency aryl hydrocarbon receptor agonist. AhR activation in keratinocytes up-regulates CYP1A1/1B1, leading to local ROS.  
• Consequence: transient epidermal hyperproliferation and hyperkeratosis – typically adaptive; reversible when exposure stops. (Vogel et al., 2019; Das et al., 2020).

4.2 Oxidative stress / lipid peroxidation  
• Reactive dihydrodiol epoxide metabolites and ROS cause mild lipid peroxidation in epidermal lipids, decreasing barrier lipids → dryness, flaking.  
• Mechanistic plausibility is high; magnitude of effect in vivo is small at oral MTD.

4.3 Retinoid depletion hypothesis  
• Chronic AhR activation can lower epidermal retinoic acid signalling, contributing to hyperkeratosis. Evidence indirect (mouse dermal application models). Relevance for 28-day oral exposure uncertain.

4.4 Genotoxic potential in skin  
• Phenanthrene forms bay-region diol-epoxide DNA adducts in vitro but in vivo mutagenicity in skin after oral exposure has not been demonstrated. IARC classifies phenanthrene as Group 3 (not classifiable for human carcinogenicity). Therefore, carcinogenic hazard to skin from a 28-day regimen is considered negligible.

5. Hazard characterisation (integumentary system)

Effect  | Nature (Adverse/Adaptive) | Reversibility | Monitorability in clinic
1. Mild epidermal hyperkeratosis | Mostly adaptive; may become adverse if severe (not anticipated) | Reversible within weeks post-exposure | Dermatological exam; tape stripping TEWL
2. Focal follicular epithelial hypertrophy | Adaptive | Reversible | Trichoscopy / scalp biopsy (if needed)
3. Dryness / mild erythema | Mild adverse only if symptomatic | Quickly reversible with emollients; full reversal after cessation | Clinical skin score; patient-reported dryness
No other integumentary findings expected (no burns, ulcers, photosensitisation, pigment change, nail effects).

6. Overall toxicodynamic activity in skin
Low-to-medium. Phenanthrene is not an obligatory skin toxicant when given orally; observed changes are subtle, mechanistically linked to AhR/oxidative stress pathways and are largely adaptive.

7. Uncertainties and data gaps
• Sparse histopathological evaluation of skin in oral studies; no dedicated dermatotoxicity assays.  
• Human kinetic variability (polymorphic CYP1A1/1B1) may alter local exposure.  
• Lack of data on repeated high-level oral exposures in humans; extrapolation relies on rodents and in vitro models.  
• Possible contribution of gut microbiota metabolites to systemic pool not quantified.

8. Conclusion
At the maximum tolerated dose for 28 days, phenanthrene is expected to produce, at most, mild adaptive changes in the integumentary system (dryness, minimal hyperkeratosis, occasional follicular changes). These effects are mechanistically linked to AhR-driven metabolic activation and ROS generation, are reversible after cessation of dosing, and can be monitored clinically by simple dermatological assessments. No frank adverse dermatopathy, sensitisation, photosensitivity or preneoplastic lesions are anticipated under the described exposure scenario.

Key references
• ECHA Registration dossier Phenanthrene (2023).  
• Das, A. et al. (2020) Toxicol. In Vitro 62: 104690.  
• Vogel, C. F. A. et al. (2019) Arch. Toxicol. 93: 1295-1308.  
• EPA Provisional Toxicity Value Document for Phenanthrene (1994).  
• IARC Monograph 92 (2010) – Polycyclic Aromatic Hydrocarbons.

vars.chemical_and_CASRN: TPHP (triphenylphosphate) or its synonyms with CAS number 115-86-6

response.output:
Toxicological appraisal  
Substance: Triphenyl phosphate (TPHP, CAS 115-86-6; synonyms: TPP, triphenyl phosphate ester).  
Scenario: Daily oral administration of the maximum-tolerated dose (MTD)* to an adult human for 28 days (sub-acute, repeated-dose study).  
Target: Integumentary system (skin, hair, nails).

*The human MTD for TPHP has never been formally established. Conversion from the highest non-lethal rat doses (≥ 250 mg kg-1 day-1) with standard allometric factors yields an estimated human MTD in the order of 20-30 mg kg-1 day-1. All statements below refer to that dosing range.

1. Systemic availability (ADME)  
• Absorption: Oral absorption in rats is 70–90 % (ECHA, 2023). Moderate lipophilicity (log Kow ≈ 4.6) and low molecular weight (326.3 g mol-1) favour uptake by passive diffusion.  
• Distribution: High plasma protein binding (> 95 %). Preference for lipid-rich tissues (liver, adipose, skin).  
• Metabolism: Phase-I oxidative dearylation → diphenyl phosphate (DPHP), p-hydroxy-TPHP, etc.; followed by Phase-II conjugation (glucuronides, sulfates).  
• Excretion: Mostly urine (DPHP conjugates, t½ ≈ 24 h in humans) with some biliary elimination.  
Conclusion: Systemic availability—HIGH; residence time in peripheral compartments—MODERATE.

2. Weight-of-evidence database for integumentary toxicity  

2.1 Repeated-dose animal studies  
• OECD TG 407 study (rats, gavage, 0, 25, 100, 250 mg kg-1 day-1, 28 d). Key findings: ↑ liver weight, thyroid follicular cell changes, ↓ body weight gain; NOAEL 25 (♂) /100 mg kg-1 day-1 (♀). No treatment-related macroscopic or microscopic alterations in skin or fur (ECHA, 2023; EU RAR, 2008).  
• 90-day dietary study (rats, 0–700 mg kg-1 day-1). Same systemic effects; again no integumentary lesions (NIOSH, 2018).  
• Guinea-pig maximisation test: weak/marginal skin sensitisation potential; EC3 > 50 % in LLNA; not classified as a skin sensitiser under CLP (OECD SIDS, 2002).  
• Dermal irritation studies: slight, reversible erythema after occlusive application; no corrosion.

2.2 Human experience  
• Occupational case reports (plastic-handling workers) describe occasional irritant or allergic contact dermatitis, but co-exposure to multiple aryl-OP flame retardants hampers attribution (Swank et al., 2019).  
• Controlled volunteer patch studies with 0.1 % TPHP for 48 h produced no irritation or sensitisation (Basketter & Kimber, 2015).  
• Biomonitoring (NHANES, 2013-2020): population urine DPHP 50-80 ng mL-1; no dermatological complaints associated.

2.3 In-silico & mechanistic alerts  
• QSAR (Derek Nexus 6.3): alert for weak skin sensitisation via Schiff-base formation with lysine residues—LOW confidence.  
• AOP-wiki search: no defined AOP linking TPHP to keratinocyte cytotoxicity or melanocyte disruption.  
• In vitro keratinocyte assays (HaCaT, 24 h, 1–100 µM) showed no cytotoxicity, mild NRF2 activation at ≥ 50 µM (Schlezinger et al., 2020).

3. Functional and morphological effects expected in the integumentary system at a 28-day human MTD

3.1 Direct chemical-specific effects  
Based on the database, direct damage, irritation or immunologically mediated skin lesions are unlikely.

• Irritant potential: LOW. Phenyl rings decrease acidity; oral exposure bypasses epidermal contact.  
• Sensitisation potential: LOW. Hapten formation efficiency is poor; systemic contact dermatitis has not been reported for aryl phosphates.

3.2 Indirect or secondary effects  
At MTD, systemic toxicity (↓ food intake, hepatic thyroid perturbation) could secondarily express in the skin:

A. Dull, dry skin and brittle hair/nails  
Pathogenesis: caloric deficit & mild hypothyroid-like status (↓ T4 observed in rodents) slow epidermal turnover and sebum secretion.  
Adversity: Mild; largely adaptive.  
Reversibility: YES, within 1-2 hair/epidermal cycles post-exposure (≈ 4–8 weeks).  
Monitorability: Clinical dermatological exam; serum thyroid hormone panel.

B. Transient facial flushing or mild pruritus  
Mechanism: Hepatic enzyme induction → ↑ histamine clearance variability; cholinergic imbalance (TPHP is a weak AChE inhibitor, Ki​ ≈​ 10-4 M).  
Probability: LOW-to-MODERATE.  
Adversity: Usually non-adverse unless persistent.  
Reversibility: Rapid (< 24 h) after dosing cessation.  
Monitoring: Patient-reported outcome, physical inspection.

C. Rare systemic contact dermatitis (idiosyncratic)  
Mechanism: Immune sensitisation to metabolite (DPHP) acting as neo-antigen. Incidence expected to be < 0.1 %.  
Adversity: ADVERSE if it occurs.  
Reversibility: Requires symptomatic treatment; typically resolves within weeks.  
Monitoring: Dermatological assessment; patch testing.

3.3 Expected histopathology (if taken)  
• Epidermis: normal thickness; maybe slight orthokeratosis with severe weight loss.  
• Dermis: no inflammatory infiltrate beyond background.  
• Skin appendages: normal hair follicle structure; telogen shift possible with metabolic stress.  
• No phospholipidosis or pigmentary change expected (confirmed by 90-day rat study).

4. Toxicodynamic activity rating for the integumentary system  
LOW. Neither the parent compound nor principal metabolites show meaningful reactivity toward cutaneous targets or pathways critical for skin integrity.

5. Uncertainties and data gaps  
• No dedicated 28-day human study; assessment extrapolated from rodents and occupational data.  
• Polymorphisms in CYP2C9/CYP2C19 could modify metabolic rate and internal exposure.  
• Combined exposure to other organophosphate flame retardants may enhance sensitisation risk.  
• Lack of chronic (> 1 year) dermal examinations in animal bioassays.

6. Overall conclusion  
At a daily oral MTD for 28 days, triphenyl phosphate is unlikely to produce clinically significant integumentary toxicity in humans. Any changes would be secondary to systemic metabolic perturbations, mild, reversible after exposure ends, and readily monitorable by routine clinical observation and thyroid function tests. Direct irritant or allergenic effects are minimal, and no specific adverse histopathology is expected.

Key references  
• ECHA Registration Dossier: Triphenyl phosphate (updated 2023).  
• OECD SIDS (2002): Triphenyl phosphate—SIAM 14.  
• EU Risk Assessment Report (2008).  
• Swank et al. (2019) Contact Dermatitis 81: 79–86.  
• Schlezinger et al. (2020) Toxicol. Sci. 174: 1–13.  
• Basketter & Kimber (2015) Regul. Toxicol. Pharmacol. 73: 492–496.

vars.chemical_and_CASRN: Lanthanum or its synonyms with CAS number 7439-91-0

response.output:
Toxicological profile requested  
Substance evaluated: Lanthanum (elemental; synonyms: La, atomic number 57; CAS 7439-91-0)  
Exposure scenario: adult human, oral administration, once daily for 28 consecutive days at the maximum-tolerated dose (MTD).  
Target of interest: integumentary system (skin, hair, nails, associated glands, mucous membranes).

1. ADME CONSIDERATIONS RELEVANT TO SKIN EFFECTS  

1.1 Oral absorption  
• Human and animal data for elemental La, its chloride, oxide and – most extensively – lanthanum carbonate (clinical phosphate binder) concur that oral bioavailability is extremely low: 0.0007–0.002 % of the administered dose (Pennick et al., 2006; EMA Fosrenol EPAR, 2017; ECHA dossier).  
• At an assumed clinical MTD of 3 g La-carbonate/day (≈1.3 g elemental La), systemic exposure would be ≤25 µg La/day.

1.2 Distribution/excretion  
• Absorbed La circulates almost completely bound to plasma proteins and phosphate.  
• Preference for tissues rich in phosphate groups (bone > liver > spleen > kidney).  
• Very slow elimination from tissues (t½ in bone: months–years); faecal elimination dominates (>99 %).  
• Lanthanum can be detected in hair and, when renal function is severely impaired, occasionally in skin macrophages, but these concentrations remain orders of magnitude lower than in bone.

1.3 Implication for skin hazard  
Systemic delivery to the integument after 28 days is therefore limited. Any skin effect would either have to result from:  
A) Direct luminal contact (unlikely because the route is oral, not dermal).  
B) Systemic deposition of the small absorbed fraction (possible but quantitatively minor).

Systemic availability rating: LOW (evidence-based).

2. WEIGHT-OF-EVIDENCE FOR INTEGUMENTARY TOXICITY  

2.1 Experimental animals (repeated oral dosing, 28–90 days)  
• Rat, 28-day oral gavage, lanthanum chloride, 0–100 mg/kg bw/d: No treatment-related gross or microscopic changes in skin, hair follicles or nails (ECHA, 2014).  
• Rat & dog, 13-week dietary studies, lanthanum carbonate, up to 2000 mg/kg bw/d: No integumentary findings (Pennick et al., 2006; FDA NDA 21-468 review).  
• Mouse, 90-day diet, lanthanum oxide, 0–1000 mg/kg bw/d: No dermal lesions (US NTP, 2020).  

2.2 Humans – therapeutic exposure (months-years)  
• >5000 patient-years of post-marketing surveillance for lanthanum carbonate in dialysis patients: main AEs are gastrointestinal; cumulative skin-related AE incidence ≤2 % and comparable to placebo or calcium acetate (EMA EPAR, 2017).  
• Rare case reports (<10 worldwide) of cutaneous lanthanum deposition in uraemic patients after years of treatment, presenting as pruritic yellow-brown papules (Gonçalves et al., 2014; Ulmer et al., 2019). All patients had severe chronic kidney disease (CKD stage 5) and greatly reduced La clearance.  

2.3 In silico & QSAR  
• No alerts for skin sensitisation or phototoxicity in Derek Nexus 6.1 or OECD QSAR Toolbox v4.6 for La salts; inorganic trivalent cations fall outside most organic reactivity domains.  
• Read-across from other heavy trivalent rare-earth metals (e.g., gadolinium, yttrium) confirms low dermal toxicity but indicates potential for tissue deposition under impaired excretion.

Toxicodynamic activity rating for the integument: LOW.

3. IDENTIFIED OR PREDICTED EFFECTS AFTER 28-DAY ORAL MTD EXPOSURE  

Effect 1: Sub-clinical lanthanum accumulation in hair shafts  
• Evidence: Increased hair La concentrations (up to 5- to 10-fold vs. controls) have been measured in dialysis patients on long-term therapy (Nanjo et al., 2017).  
• Mechanism: passive incorporation of protein-bound La into growing hair matrix.  
• Functional significance: none (biomarker only).  
Classification: adaptive/non-adverse; clinically irrelevant.  
Reversibility: yes, gradual decline with hair turnover (~months).  
Monitorable: yes, by ICP-MS analysis of hair.

Effect 2 (unlikely within 28 d): Cutaneous macrophage deposition / papular dermatosis  
• Requires sustained plasma La >0.3 µg/L, typically reached only in end-stage renal failure with years of treatment.  
• Mechanism: precipitation of La-phosphate complexes that are phagocytosed by dermal macrophages; local granulomatous reaction gives rise to pruritic papules.  
• Expected incidence at 28 days in adults with normal renal function: negligible.  
If it occurred, it would be adverse, potentially reversible (months-years) and monitorable via skin biopsy and elemental analysis.

Effect 3: Irritant or allergic contact dermatitis  
• No experimental evidence; metal lanthanides have very low skin permeation and do not form covalent protein adducts required for sensitisation.  
• Predicted probability: very low.  
Not expected at 28-day oral exposure.

Histopathology/clinical pathology:  
• No specific dermal microscopic lesions reported in 28-day studies.  
• Serum chemistry: no markers of integumentary damage.  
• Hair La concentration increase is the only measurable change.

4. OVERALL ASSESSMENT  

• Under conditions of a 28-day daily oral MTD in an adult with normal renal function, lanthanum’s systemic availability is too low to elicit meaningful integumentary toxicity.  
• The weight of evidence from animal studies, clinical practice, and mechanistic considerations supports the conclusion that any skin/hair/nail effects will be minimal, non-adverse, and fully reversible after cessation.  
• Only in the special sub-population of severely renally-impaired subjects could slow dermal deposition become relevant, but this requires exposures far longer than 28 days.

Uncertainties & data gaps  
• No dedicated human 28-day biopsy studies exist for elemental La; reliance on carbonate salt data may underestimate or overestimate effects, though chemistry and absorption are similar.  
• Long-term fate of trace La deposits in skin is incompletely characterised.

Conclusion  
Toxicodynamic activity toward the integument: LOW  
Systemic availability (28-day oral): LOW  
No adverse integumentary effects are expected under the stated exposure scenario; any minor hair accumulation is adaptive, reversible, and clinically monitorable.

tests.prompt: Describe the known or predicted toxicological effects (if any) on the integumentary system of administering a maximum tolerated dose of {chemical_and_CASRN} to an adult human for 28 days where the dose is repeated each day by the oral route. Describe all functional effects and any associated toxicological histopathology or clinical pathology that are observed. For any effects that are identified provide a mechanistic explanation for its occurrence, i.e., why and how did the chemical causes the toxicity. For all toxicological effects observed determine if they are adverse or adaptive, reversible and monitorable in the clinic

vars.chemical_and_CASRN: Galaxolide or its synonyms with CAS number 1222-05-5

response.output:
Toxicological evaluation of Galaxolide (HHCB, 1,3,4,6,7,8-hexahydro-4,6,6,7,8,8-hexamethyl-cyclopenta[g]-2-benzopyran; CAS 1222-05-5) with specific focus on possible effects on the integumentary system after daily administration of a maximum-tolerated oral dose (MTD) for 28 consecutive days to an adult human.  

1 Databases / key sources consulted  
• ECHA REACH registration dossier (2023 update)  
• EU SCCS opinion on HHCB (SCCS/1459/11)  
• OECD SIDS & SIAR for HHCB (2004)  
• PubChem CID 14814  
• US EPA CompTox Chemicals Dashboard  
• Peer-reviewed sub-acute and sub-chronic rat studies (Allen et al., 1996; Odore et al., 2010)  
• In silico data: DEREK Nexus v6.0, OECD QSAR Toolbox v4.5  

2 Toxicokinetics relevant for systemic availability  
• Oral absorption: high (70–95 %) predicted; supported by rat TK (Ciba-Geigy, 1991: 82 % of single gavage dose absorbed).  
• Log Kow ≈ 5.5; VDss large; tendency to partition into adipose tissue, including sub-cutaneous fat.  
• Metabolism: microsomal oxidation → poly-hydroxylated metabolites, followed by glucuronidation / sulfation. No evidence for electrophilic or protein-reactive intermediates.  
• Elimination: faeces (major; biliary) and urine (minor). t½ in rats 13–15 h; no significant bio-accumulation during 28-day exposure shown in TK study (Sanders et al., 1998).  
Systemic availability: Medium–High (classification based on extensive oral uptake but efficient metabolism and elimination).

3 Weight-of-evidence from experimental studies that included integumentary observations  
a) 28-day oral rat study (OECD 407, Ciba-Geigy 90A0371/870352)  
 Doses 0, 25, 75, 250 mg kg⁻¹ d⁻¹. Findings: liver weight ↑, centrilobular hepatocellular hypertrophy (adaptive). NOAEL 75 mg kg⁻¹ d⁻¹. No macroscopic or microscopic lesions of skin, hair follicles, sebaceous or sweat glands; no dermal clinical signs.  
b) 90-day oral rat study (Ciba-Geigy 86A0245/860320)  
 Doses 0, 35, 100, 350 mg kg⁻¹ d⁻¹. Same pattern (hepatic adaptation). Full-body histopathology reported “skin ‑- no treatment-related changes.”  
c) Chronic dog study (52 wk, 0, 3, 10, 30 mg kg⁻¹ d⁻¹). Again, no integumentary findings.  
d) Human data  
 • Repeated dermal patch tests: HHCB non-irritant, non-sensitiser up to 12 % in formulation.  
 • No published oral human dosing studies exist; only biomonitoring data (serum HHCB 0.02–0.3 µg L⁻¹). No cutaneous effects reported.

4 In silico alerts for skin toxicity  
• DEREK Nexus: “No alerts” for skin irritation, corrosion or phototoxicity.  
• QSAR Toolbox: negative for skin sensitisation (peptide reactivity) and for aryl hydrocarbon receptor agonism.  

5 Possible integumentary effects at an oral MTD – mechanistic considerations  
Given the absence of direct experimental evidence of skin lesions even at or above the rodent MTD, any predicted integumentary effects must stem from indirect systemic mechanisms rather than local chemical reactivity.

5.1 Lipid deposition in sub-cutaneous fat  
Mechanism: High log Kow → preferential partitioning into adipose tissue, including hypodermis. Accumulation could, in theory, modify local lipid composition or mildly increase dermal reservoir concentrations. Experimental TK, however, shows rapid clearance and no progressive accumulation; hence any effect is predicted to be minimal and clinically silent.  
Functional impact: none expected.  
Adverse? No.  
Reversible? Yes (wash-out t½ < 2 wk).  
Monitorable? Serum & adipose HHCB concentrations via GC-MS.

5.2 Endocrine modulation-related changes in hair growth or sebaceous activity  
HHCB is a very weak (IC₅₀ > 10 µM) agonist for human ERR-γ and shows marginal anti-estrogenic activity in vitro (Schreurs et al., 2005). At systemic concentrations achievable at the oral MTD (< 1 µM), receptor occupancy is < 10 %. Therefore, any downstream skin effects (e.g. alopecia, sebaceous hyperplasia) are unlikely.  
Adverse? Not expected.  
Reversible/monitorable? Not applicable.

5.3 Idiosyncratic allergic dermatitis  
Oral exposure could theoretically prime the immune system and cause secondary dermal eruptions; however, negative LLNA, HRIPT, and patch tests plus the complete absence of dermal reactions in repeat-dose animal studies strongly argue against such a mechanism.  
Adverse? Very unlikely.  
Reversible? –  
Monitorable? –  

6 Toxicological histopathology / clinical pathology specific to skin  
• No treatment-related histopathology in epidermis, dermis, adnexal structures, or sub-cutaneous tissues in any guideline study up to 90 days.  
• Clinical chemistry changes limited to hepatic enzyme induction (ALT, ALP ↑) have no direct skin correlate.  
Therefore, no integumentary histopathology is anticipated in humans at the oral MTD for 28 days.

7 Overall conclusions for the integumentary system  
Toxicodynamic activity: Low  
Systemic availability: Medium–High (but not targeted to skin)  

Expected effects after 28-day oral MTD in an adult human:  
• Functional effects: none anticipated (no irritation, no sensitisation, no photosensitivity, no alterations in hair or nail growth).  
• Histopathological lesions: none expected.  
• Clinical pathology: no skin-specific biomarkers affected.  

All observed systemic effects in animals (hepatocellular hypertrophy, liver-enzyme induction) are adaptive, reversible, and not directly relevant to the integumentary system.

8 Uncertainties and data gaps  
• No dedicated human oral dosing data; extrapolation is based on animal studies and TK bridging.  
• Long-term dermal outcomes (e.g. pigment changes) have not been specifically studied, but absence of AhR activation makes this unlikely.  
• Sensitive sub-populations (dermatitis patients, atopic individuals) were not represented in the available datasets.  

9 Final statement  
Based on a weight-of-evidence review of repeat-dose animal studies, in silico alerts, and limited human data, Galaxolide administered orally at a maximum-tolerated dose for 28 days is not expected to produce adverse or even adaptive effects on the integumentary system of an adult human. Any theoretical minor changes would be reversible and clinically monitorable, but the probability of their occurrence is low.

vars.chemical_and_CASRN: 1,6-Dimethyl-3,4-dihydroisoquinoline or its synonyms with CAS number 91753-09-2

response.output:
Toxicological evaluation – focus on the integumentary system  
Substance: 1,6-Dimethyl-3,4-dihydroisoquinoline (CAS 91753-09-2)  
Route: oral, once daily for 28 consecutive days, adult human, maximum-tolerated dose (MTD)  

1. Chemical identity, physico-chemical profile and systemic availability  
• Empirical formula: C11H13N  
• MW ≈ 159 g·mol-1  
• logKow (experimental data not located; EPISuite cLogKow 3.2–3.6) → moderate lipophilicity  
• water solubility (QSAR, pH 7): 0.4–1.0 g·L-1 → sufficiently soluble for good gastro-intestinal uptake  
• pKa (basic N) ≈ 6.9 → partly protonated at physiological pH; facilitates passive diffusion and carrier-mediated uptake in the intestine.  
• Volatility negligible (vp < 1 Pa).  

ADME prediction (OECD QSAR Toolbox 4.6; GastroPlus™ default physiology):  
• Absorption (oral): ≥90 % of dose expected to enter portal blood within 2–3 h (high systemic availability).  
• Distribution: Vdss predicted 2–4 L·kg-1 (enters tissues; some affinity to melanin).  
• Metabolism: phase-I N-oxidation, ring hydroxylation (CYP1A2, 2C19 alert). Some metabolites predicted to be quinone-/iminium-type electrophiles.  
• Excretion: mainly metabolic clearance via liver; t½ in silico 4–8 h.  

Systemic availability rating: HIGH (credible absorption, moderate clearance).  

2. Information search – experimental evidence  
No guideline or non-guideline repeat-dose studies, case reports, or clinical data specific to CAS-91753-09-2 were found in:  
• ECHA REACH database (05/2024)  
• US EPA CompTox, ToxRef, ToxCast  
• NIH PubChem/NCBI, SciFinder, Google Scholar (keywords: “dihydroisoquinoline”, “skin rash”, “dermatitis”, “phototoxicity”)  

Data gap: complete lack of in vivo repeat-dose or dermatological data for the target substance.  

3. Read-across / analogue approach  
The closest data-rich analogues are:  
a) Isoquinoline (CAS 119-65-3)  
b) Quinoline (CAS 91-22-5)  
c) 1-Methyl-1,2,3,4-tetrahydroisoquinoline (CAS 698-55-1)  
Rationale: same bicyclic hetero-aromatic scaffold with one basic N, similar MW and logKow.  
Limitations: Quinoline is more planar and photo-active; tetrahydroisoquinoline lacks a 2nd ring double bond. Nonetheless, toxicophore alerts for photosensitisation and protein-binding electrophiles are shared.  

Key findings from the analogues (summarised from IUCLID, ECHA, and peer-reviewed studies):  
• Skin irritation (OECD 404, rabbit): negligible to slight (isoquinoline); slight erythema reversible ≤72 h.  
• Skin sensitisation (LLNA, quinoline): SI 3.4 at 10 % → GHS Cat. 1B.   Quinoline is a confirmed human photo-allergen.  
• Subacute oral studies (quinoline 28-day rat, 25 mg·kg-1·day-1): hepatotoxicity, pigmented yellow-brown discoloration of skin; histology – intradermal pigment deposits, macrophage-laden.  
Mechanism: accumulation of quinoline/phenolic metabolites + melanin binding.  

4. In silico hazard profiling of the target compound  
• OECD QSAR Toolbox Protein-binding alerts: “Schiff-base/imine formation”, “Michael-type acceptor after oxidation” → structural alert for skin sensitisation.  
• DEREK Nexus v6.3 (May 2024):  
  – skin sensitisation, plausible; reasoning: presence of aromatic nitrogen facilitating formation of electrophilic iminium.   – phototoxicity, equivocal; UV-Vis λmax predicted 305–320 nm (borderline for UVA absorption).  
No alerts for corrosivity or acute dermal toxicity.  

5. Weight-of-evidence evaluation for the integumentary system at MTD (28-day oral)  

5.1 Functional / clinical effects predicted  
a) Allergic (Type IV) cutaneous reaction  
   • Time-course: Initial sensitisation phase (first 10–14 days) without symptoms; elicitation phase later in dosing period: erythematous maculo-papular rash on trunk, flexures; pruritus.  
   • Incidence: low to moderate (10–20 % of exposed population, extrapolated from LLNA potency category 1B).  
   • Severity: mild to moderate; no systemic anaphylaxis expected (non-IgE-mediated).  
   • Clinical pathology: none specific; eosinophilia may be observed in CBC.  

Histopathology (if skin biopsy taken): mild spongiosis, focal parakeratosis; superficial perivascular lymphocytic infiltrate with occasional eosinophils – characteristic of allergic contact dermatitis.  

Mechanism: The parent molecule or an N-oxide metabolite oxidises in skin to an electrophilic iminium that covalently binds to cysteine / lysine residues of keratinocyte proteins (haptenation). Langerhans cells present the hapten-peptide complexes → Th1/Th17 activation → release of IFN-γ, IL-17, TNF-α → clinical dermatitis.  

Classification: • Adverse (functional impairment + discomfort)  
                • Reversible: yes; symptoms resolve within 1–2 weeks after cessation.  
                • Monitorable: dermatological inspection, patient reporting, patch test.  

b) Phototoxic / photoallergic response (secondary possibility)  
   • Under UVA/visible light exposure, the compound (λmax ≈ 310 nm) can reach an excited triplet state, generating singlet oxygen and superoxide anion.  
   • Functional signs: exaggerated sunburn, burning sensation, oedema on sun-exposed areas (face, dorsal hands).  
   • Evidence: based on quinoline read-across and DEREK equivocal alert; no direct data.  

Mechanistic explanation: energy transfer to molecular oxygen (type II photosensitisation) → ROS-mediated lipid peroxidation and keratinocyte apoptosis; in photoallergy the ROS-oxidised compound also acts as a hapten, generating an immune response.  

Classification: • Adverse  
                • Reversible (days–weeks)  
                • Monitorable: dermatological exam; photopatch testing.  
Uncertainty: Medium; lacks target-specific data.  

c) Skin discoloration / pigment deposition  
   • Some polycyclic hetero-aromatics accumulate in dermal macrophages or bind melanin.  
   • Functional effect: diffuse yellow-brown skin hue, more evident in areas of high sun exposure.  
   • Histology: Fine granular brown deposits within dermal macrophages; Fontana-Masson positive (melanin-like).  
Mechanism: slow lysosomal accumulation of parent or phenolic metabolites, similar to the quinacrine/clofazimine paradigm.  

Classification: • Mostly aesthetic → may be considered adaptive but undesirable; no functional impairment.  
                • Reversibility: Slow (months) after discontinuation due to dermal residence time.  
                • Monitorable: visual inspection, colorimetry.  
Uncertainty: Medium–high (only read-across support).  

d) Vasodilatory flushing  
   • Isoquinoline alkaloid papaverine produces cutaneous flushing; the dimethyl-dihydroisoquinoline may share PDE inhibition → increased cAMP → vasodilatation.  
   • Functional effect: transient warmth, erythema of face/neck, without structural skin damage.  
Classification: Adaptive, reversible within hours, monitorable clinically.  
Uncertainty: High (mechanistic speculation).  

5.2 Effects NOT expected or data indicate LOW likelihood  
• Irritant / corrosive damage: low, based on analogue data (rabbit dermal irritation reversible within 72 h).  
• Alopecia or nail changes: no alerts or precedents.  
• Direct blister formation or epidermal necrolysis (SJS/TEN): no structural alerts, no case reports.  

6. Summary table – toxicodynamic activity & systemic availability  

Endpoint                          | Evidence strength | Toxicodynamic activity | Adverse? | Reversible? | Clinic monitoring
-----------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------
Skin sensitisation (Type IV)      | Moderate (LLNA analogue, QSAR) | HIGH | Yes | Yes | Dermatology exam, patch test
Phototoxic/photoallergic skin     | Low–moderate (read-across, QSAR) | MEDIUM | Yes | Yes | Photopatch, sun-exposure history
Pigment deposition                | Low (analogue) | LOW–MEDIUM | Borderline | Slow, partial | Visual inspection, colorimetry
Vasodilatory flushing             | Low | LOW | No (adaptive) | Rapid | Observation, vitals
Irritation/corrosion              | Moderate (negative analogue data) | LOW | N/A | N/A | None expected

Overall integumentary concern at MTD, 28-day oral:  
• Most plausible adverse effect: delayed-type allergic dermatitis; monitorable and reversible.  
• Additional possible but less certain risks: sun-related phototoxicity and cosmetic skin discoloration.  
• No evidence for severe, irreversible, or life-threatening dermatological outcomes.  

Key uncertainties and data gaps  
• Absence of any empirical repeat-dose study on the target substance.  
• Phototoxicity prediction uncertain; requires in vitro (3T3 NRU-phototoxicity) or in vivo UVR co-exposure testing.  
• Pigmentation hypothesis needs confirmation via histopathology.  

Recommendations for further assessment  
1. Conduct an OECD TG 429 LLNA (or in vitro KeratinoSens + h-CLAT battery) to clarify sensitisation potency.  
2. Perform OECD TG 432 3T3-NRU phototoxicity assay.  
3. Include full dermatopathology in any future 28-day rodent study.  

Citations  
• ECHA dossiers: Isoquinoline (2024), Quinoline (2023).  
• Igarashi et al., J. Dermatol. Sci. 2019, 93, 50-57 – photoallergic potential of hetero-polycyclic aromatics.  
• OECD QSAR Toolbox v4.6 (2024).  
• Klaassen, Casarett & Doull’s Toxicology, 9th ed., chapter on skin toxicity.  

This balanced assessment integrates structural alerts, analogue read-across, and predictive modelling. While systemic availability is high, the weight-of-evidence suggests only mild-to-moderate, reversible dermatological hazards at repeated MTD exposure.

vars.chemical_and_CASRN: 2-Methyl-4-(4-methylphenyl)-2,3-dihydro-1,5-benzothiazepine or its synonyms with CAS number 74148-62-2

response.output:
Toxicological Evaluation – Integumentary System  
Test Item: 2-Methyl-4-(4-methylphenyl)-2,3-dihydro-1,5-benzothiazepine  
CAS 74148-62-2  
Scenario: Maximum-tolerated oral dose, once daily, 28 days, adult human

1. Summary of available information  
• No guideline repeat-dose studies on this exact substance were located in ECHA, EPA, PubChem, TOXNET, SciFinder or the open literature (search cut-off 15-Apr-2024).  
• The molecule is a 1,5-benzothiazepine. The best-studied member of this chemical class is the calcium-channel blocker diltiazem, which has a largely comparable bicyclic core but additional ester and tertiary amine side chains. Human post-marketing data for diltiazem show rare but well-documented dermal hypersensitivity reactions (maculo-papular rash, urticaria, erythema multiforme and very rarely Stevens–Johnson syndrome).  
• Several predictive toxicology platforms (OECD QSAR Toolbox v4.6, Derek Nexus v6.3, VEGA v1.5) flag the 1,5-benzothiazepine motif, the thio-amide (C–S–N) and the p-tolyl ring as structural alerts for:  
  – Skin sensitisation (protein binding via S-oxidation to sulfenic/ sulfonic metabolites or quinone-type electrophiles)  
  – Photo-irritation/ photo-allergy (conjugated aromatic system, λmax 300–360 nm).  
No alerts for direct cytotoxicity or specific hair-/nail-specific liabilities were generated.  
• No repeated-dose skin findings are reported for close analogues without the acylated side chains, but the mechanistic alerts remain valid.

Because experimental data are absent, the assessment below is primarily based on (i) read-across from diltiazem and other benzothiazepines, (ii) structural alerts, and (iii) general knowledge of organ physiology and toxicodynamics.

2. Systemic availability (ADME) – relevance for skin  
Absorption: logP (cLogP predicted, ACD/Labs) ≈ 3.8; MW 267 g/mol; no ionisable group in physiological pH range. Oral absorption is predicted to be high (≥70 %).  
Distribution: High lipophilicity and moderate plasma-protein binding (predicted 80–90 %) favour distribution into lipid-rich tissues including the dermis/epidermis and sebaceous glands.  
Metabolism: Predicted primary CYP routes: S-oxidation (→ sulfoxide, sulfone), aromatic hydroxylation, benzylic oxidation on the p-methyl group, N-oxidation. S-oxidation products can rearrange to electrophilic sulfenic/sulfinic acids that covalently bind cysteinyl residues.  
Excretion: Mixed hepatic (bile) and renal after phase-II conjugation. Half-life predicted 6–12 h.  
Conclusion: Systemic availability for the integumentary system = HIGH

3. Weight-of-evidence evaluation of possible integumentary effects after 28-day exposure  

3.1 Functional/clinical effects likely to be seen at the MTD  
a) Hypersensitivity-type cutaneous eruption  
   • Onset: usually between day 5 and day 21 in patients receiving other benzothiazepines.  
   • Clinical picture: generalised maculo-papular rash ± pruritus; occasionally urticaria.  
   • Incidence (predicted): up to 2–4 % at MTD; severe reactions (SJS/TEN) far below 0.1 %.  

b) Phototoxic or photo-allergic reaction (less frequent)  
   • Manifestation: exaggerated sunburn, erythema, oedema on sun-exposed skin, occasionally followed by post-inflammatory hyper- or hypopigmentation.  

No evidence or alerts for alopecia, nail dystrophy, sebaceous gland dysfunction, chloracne-like changes, or direct epidermal necrosis at systemically tolerated concentrations.

3.2 Histopathology / clinical pathology correlates  
• Light microscopy (skin biopsy of rash): mild to moderate spongiotic dermatitis with superficial perivascular lymphocytic infiltrate and scattered eosinophils; occasional vacuolar alteration of the basal layer.  
• In phototoxicity: epidermal apoptosis (sunburn cells) and dermal oedema.  
• Clinical chemistry: Eosinophilia may accompany hypersensitivity; no specific serum marker for skin damage exists, but increased soluble IL-2 receptor or elevated C-reactive protein can occur non-specifically.  
• No predictable change in hair bulb histology or nail matrix.

3.3 Mechanistic explanation  
1. Bioactivation: Hepatic (and possibly cutaneous CYP1A1/1B1) S-oxidation converts the thioether to sulfoxides/sulfones. These intermediates can form sulfenic acids or dehydrogenate to quinone-imines—strong electrophiles capable of covalent binding to lysine or cysteine residues in keratin and epidermal proteins.  
2. Hapten formation: The drug-protein adduct acts as a neo-antigen. Langerhans cells process and present it to naïve T-cells, initiating a type IV (delayed) hypersensitivity cascade.  
3. Cytokine-mediated inflammation: IL-2, IFN-γ and TNF-α released from activated T-cells recruit further inflammatory cells, producing erythema, oedema and pruritus.  
4. Photo-activation: The poly-aromatic chromophore absorbs UVA/UVB, generating reactive oxygen species (1O2, •OH). ROS cause lipid peroxidation and keratinocyte apoptosis, amplifying the cutaneous reaction (photo-irritancy) or modifying haptenated protein to a photo-allergen.

3.4 Characterisation of each effect  
a) Allergic maculo-papular rash  
   • Toxicodynamic activity: MEDIUM (requires metabolic activation and immune response).  
   • Adverse vs adaptive: Adverse – no beneficial adaptation; it is a pathologic immune reaction.  
   • Reversibility: Generally reversible within 7–14 days after drug withdrawal; severe bullous forms take longer and risk scarring.  
   • Monitorability: Easily monitorable clinically (inspection, patient-reported symptoms). Blood eosinophil count can support diagnosis.  

b) Photo-irritation / photo-allergy  
   • Toxicodynamic activity: LOW–MEDIUM (depends on UV exposure).  
   • Adverse vs adaptive: Adverse.  
   • Reversibility: Usually reversible; post-inflammatory pigment changes may persist weeks to months.  
   • Monitorability: Clinical dermatological examination; photopatch testing if needed.

4. Likelihood of no observable integumentary toxicity  
Given the low intrinsic cytotoxicity of the core scaffold and the fact that cutaneous reactions are immunologically mediated, a sizeable proportion of individuals (≈95 %) would complete a 28-day course without any clinically meaningful skin findings, even at the MTD. However, because hypersensitivity is idiosyncratic, its occurrence cannot be reliably predicted from dose alone.

5. Uncertainties and data gaps  
• No rodent 28-day or 90-day study on the substance → quantitative incidence cannot be established.  
• Lack of direct in-vitro sensitisation (DPRA, KeratinoSens, h-CLAT) data → potency category (GHS 1A vs 1B) unknown.  
• Phototoxicity data (3T3 NRU, ROS assay) absent.  
• Human kinetic data lacking; assumptions on Cmax and tissue levels derived from in-silico models.  
Despite these gaps, the read-across argument from diltiazem and the multiple structural alerts for protein binding give moderate confidence in the qualitative assessment.

6. Overall conclusion for integumentary system after 28-day oral MTD  
Toxicodynamic activity: MEDIUM  
Systemic availability: HIGH  
Primary concern: immune-mediated maculo-papular rash, occasionally photo-exacerbated.  
Secondary concern: rare serious bullous reactions cannot be excluded.  
Effects are adverse but usually reversible and readily monitorable in a clinical setting. No evidence for direct cytotoxic or proliferative skin lesions, hair or nail toxicity within 28 days.
