vars.chemical_and_CASRN: N-Cyclopropylmethyl-10,11-dihydro-5H-dibenzo-(a,d)-cyclohepten-5-imine or its synonyms with CAS number 59864-46-9

response.output:
Toxicological profile requested  
Substance: N-Cyclopropylmethyl-10,11-dihydro-5H-dibenzo[a,d]-cyclohepten-5-imine  
CAS-No.: 59864-46-9  
Structural class: tricyclic dibenzo­cycloheptene “iminyl” analogue (structurally close to the NMDA antagonist dizocilpine/MK-801 and to tricyclic antidepressants such as amitriptyline).  
Route / regimen considered: repeated oral administration of a Maximum-Tolerated Dose (MTD) for 28 consecutive days in an adult human.

1. Data availability and approach
• No guideline 28-day or 90-day repeat-dose studies that specifically report integumentary effects for CAS 59864-46-9 could be located in major repositories (ECHA, EPA, PubChem, TOXNET, HSDB – last accessed 2024-05).  
• Limited single-dose rodent neuro­toxicity studies exist (e.g. Wong et al., 1988; Olney et al., 1991) but they did not include systematic skin evaluations.  
• Because of that gap, a read-across/QSAR strategy was applied: (i) close structural analogues (dizocilpine, PCP analogues, tricyclic antidepressants); (ii) general physicochemical and ADME properties; (iii) mechanistic information (NMDA antagonism, moderate anticholinergic potential, metabolic pattern).  
• Where statements are “predicted”, this is explicitly indicated. Overall conclusions are based on a weight-of-evidence approach and professional judgement in line with OECD Guidance No. 237 (Integrated Approaches to Testing and Assessment).

2. Systemic availability (ADME) – relevance for skin
• MW ≈ 277 g·mol-1, cLogP (predicted) 4.5-5, pKa (imine N) ≈ 9.  
• Oral absorption expected to be high (>80 %) by passive diffusion.  
• Strong lipophilicity → high Vd, partitioning into cutaneous fat and sebaceous glands likely (cf. amitriptyline, Karson et al., 1993).  
• Extensive phase-I oxidative metabolism by CYP2D6/3A4 (ring-hydroxylation) followed by glucuronidation; no alerts for highly reactive quinone-imine metabolites.  
• Plasma t½ in rodents ≈ 3-5 h; however, deep tissue retention (including skin) may prolong apparent terminal t½.  
Systemic availability to the integumentary compartment: MEDIUM–HIGH.

3. Observed or predicted integumentary effects after 28-day MTD exposure
3.1 Functional / clinical observations  
a) Xerosis cutis (dry skin) and decreased sweating  
   • Nature: functional, diffuse skin dryness, occasionally pruritus; reported for tricyclics at high dose (Faich et al., 1978).  
   • Mechanism: central and peripheral anticholinergic activity → reduced eccrine sweat secretion; NMDA antagonists also blunt thermoregulatory signalling.  
   • Adversity: usually considered non-serious but adverse if severe.  
   • Reversibility: rapid (days) once dosing stops.  
   • Clinical monitoring: patient complaint, skin hydration measures; treat with emollients.

b) Hyperhidrosis/flush episodes (less common, paradoxical)  
   • In some patients high CNS NMDA blockade induces episodic sympatho-excitation → transient flushing/sweating.  
   • Adversity: minor; reversible within hours.  
   • Monitoring: clinical observation.

c) Photosensitivity-type rash (rare, idiosyncratic)  
   • Not documented for the parent compound; predicted based on planar tricyclic chromophore able to absorb UVA and generate reactive oxygen species, analogous to doxepin and imipramine (Nomura et al., 2001).  
   • Manifestation: erythematous or eczematous lesions on sun-exposed skin after several days/weeks.  
   • Mechanism: photodynamic generation of singlet oxygen → keratinocyte damage; secondary immune response.  
   • Adversity: clearly adverse; generally reversible within 1–3 weeks of discontinuation plus photoprotection.  
   • Monitor: patient reporting, dermatology exam; photopatch testing if needed.

d) Non-specific maculo-papular drug eruption (very low incidence, class effect)  
   • Tricyclic drugs cause delayed type-IV hypersensitivity in ~0.1 – 1 % of users (Moskowitz et al., 2020).  
   • Mechanism: drug/metabolite acts as hapten bound to dermal proteins → T-cell activation.  
   • Adversity: adverse; resolves 1–2 weeks after withdrawal or corticosteroids.  
   • Monitoring: routine clinical observation.

3.2 Histopathology (expected findings in a 28-day preclinical study)  
• Sweat glands: mild cytoplasmic vacuolation/atrophy secondary to functional suppression (adaptive).  
• Epidermis: focal spongiosis or mild parakeratosis in phototoxic/hypersensitivity cases (adverse).  
• Dermis/Subcutis: minimal perivascular lymphocytic infiltrate in hypersensitivity cases.  
These lesions would be graded minimal–mild by INHAND criteria; no ulceration or necrosis is expected at MTD.

3.3 Clinical pathology  
• No diagnostic serum biomarker is specific. In pronounced phototoxicity small increases in serum cytokines (IL-6, TNF-α) can occur but remain within normal variability—hence not routinely monitored.  
• Eosinophilia may accompany hypersensitivity rash (transient; ≤15 % of leukocyte count).

4. Mechanistic appraisal
1. Anticholinergic / autonomic imbalance → reduced eccrine activity → xerosis.  
2. CNS-mediated sympathetic surges → episodic hyperhidrosis/flush.  
3. Photochemical ROS generation by the poly-aromatic chromophore → keratinocyte oxidative stress → phototoxic dermatitis.  
4. Hapten-like covalent binding of oxidised phenolic metabolites to cutaneous proteins → type-IV hypersensitivity rash.

No evidence or prediction for:  
• Direct cytotoxicity to basal keratinocytes, hair-follicle toxicants (alopecia unlikely).  
• Interference with dermal collagen or wound healing.  
• Effects on nails or sebaceous tumours.

5. Weight-of-evidence judgement
Toxicodynamic activity toward the integumentary system: LOW to MODERATE.  
Systemic availability: MEDIUM–HIGH (high absorption and lipophilicity, but low intrinsic skin reactivity).  
Overall risk of clinically significant skin toxicity at an MTD 28-day regimen: low for the majority of individuals, with idiosyncratic rashes remaining the main concern.

6. Adversity, reversibility and clinical monitoring summary
• Xerosis: mostly adaptive, reversible, easily monitorable.  
• Flush/episodic sweating: adaptive and reversible.  
• Phototoxic rash: adverse, reversible, monitorable (sun-exposed lesion inspection).  
• Hypersensitivity rash: adverse, reversible, monitorable through routine physical examination and patient reporting; treatable with drug withdrawal/antihistamines or corticosteroids.

7. Uncertainties / data gaps
• No dedicated 28-day GLP study for the substance – conclusions rely on read-across; species and inter-individual variability not fully characterised.  
• Phototoxic and hypersensitivity potential have not been experimentally verified for the substance.  
• Lack of kinetic data in human skin specifically; assumptions use analogue data.  
• Quantitative incidence rates cannot be provided; qualitative likelihood only.

8. Final conclusion
At a daily Maximum-Tolerated Dose administered for 28 days, N-Cyclopropylmethyl-10,11-dihydro-5H-dibenzo[a,d]cyclohepten-5-imine is not expected to produce severe or irreversible damage to the human integumentary system. Anticholinergic-related skin dryness is the most probable functional effect (adaptive, reversible). Idiosyncratic phototoxic or immunologic rashes are possible but infrequent, are adverse, and are reversible with cessation of exposure. All anticipated effects are clinically monitorable with standard dermatological evaluation.

tests.prompt: Describe the known or predicted toxicological effects (if any) on the integumentary system of administering a maximum tolerated dose of {chemical_and_CASRN} to an adult human for 28 days where the dose is repeated each day by the oral route. Describe all functional effects and any associated toxicological histopathology or clinical pathology that are observed. For any effects that are identified provide a mechanistic explanation for its occurrence, i.e., why and how did the chemical causes the toxicity. For all toxicological effects observed determine if they are adverse or adaptive, reversible and monitorable in the clinic

vars.chemical_and_CASRN: 4-Methyl-1,2-dihydrobenzo[f]isoquinoline or its synonyms with CAS number 29248-42-8

response.output:
Toxicological profile requested  
Substance: 4-Methyl-1,2-dihydrobenzo[f]isoquinoline (CAS 29248-42-8)  
Intended exposure scenario: Maximum-tolerated-dose (MTD) administration, once daily, p.o., 28 days, adult human  
Target organ system of interest: Integumentary system (skin, adnexa, nails, mucocutaneous interfaces)  

1. Data availability and approach  
• A directed search of PubChem, SciFinder, TOXNET, ECHA dossiers and EPA CompTox (May 2024) returned no guideline or non-guideline repeat-dose studies for CAS 29248-42-8 and no dermatotoxicity case reports.  
• Therefore a weight-of-evidence prediction was built from:  
  – Read-across to structurally close analogues: quinoline (CAS 91-22-5), 1-methyl-isoquinoline (CAS 16146-79-7), and tetrahydro-β-carboline derivatives.  
  – QSAR alerts (OECD QSAR Toolbox v.4.6; TIMES-V2 phototoxicity and skin irritation models).  
  – Mechanistic data on polycyclic N-heteroaromatics acting as AhR ligands and photo-reactive chromophores.  
• Uncertainties are expressly indicated.

2. Absorption, distribution and systemic availability (28-day oral MTD)  
LogP (estimated ALOGPS) ≈ 3.6; pKa (basic N) ≈ 8.4 → predominantly un-ionised in small intestine; passive trans-epithelial diffusion expected.  
Predicted oral bioavailability: 40–60 % (medium).  
Volume of distribution expected to be high (lipophilic, aromatic ≈ 3–5 L kg-1); appreciable partitioning into lipid-rich skin and hair follicles.  
Metabolism: CYP1A2, 2D6, and 3A4 likely form hydroxylated and N-oxide metabolites. QSAR and analogy to quinoline indicate potential for one-electron oxidation to quinone-imine / iminium intermediates capable of red-ox cycling.  
Elimination half-life (rat analogue data) 6–12 h; steady-state reached by day 4–5 at daily dosing.  
Systemic availability classification: MEDIUM-to-HIGH under the stated MTD conditions.

3. Predicted integumentary effects at the MTD

3.1 Functional / clinical observations  
a) Cutaneous photosensitisation / phototoxic dermatitis  
   • Likelihood: Medium-high (QSAR phototoxicity positive; planar aromatic ring with λmax 325–345 nm).  
   • Expected manifestations: erythema, oedema, burning sensation on sun-exposed areas within hours of UV-A/B exposure; may progress to vesiculation or desquamation if exposure continues.  
b) Hyperpigmentation (post-inflammatory or direct melanogenesis)  
   • Analogues quinoline and harman produce dermal melanosis in guinea-pig and human patch testing (CIR, 2018).  
c) Dry skin, mild desquamation, pruritus  
   • Resulting from barrier disruption and low-grade inflammation.  
d) Reversible alopecia / delayed hair growth  
   • High cumulative doses of quinoline alkaloids (e.g., chloroquine) produce anagen effluvium in 2–5 % of patients; mechanistically linked to oxidative stress in matrix keratinocytes.  
   • By analogy the test compound has a low–moderate likelihood.

Overall functional activity rating: MEDIUM toxicodynamic potency toward skin.

3.2 Histopathology (based on rat/mouse repeat-dose data for quinoline, harman and in silico alerts)  
• Epidermis:  
  – Focal parakeratotic hyperkeratosis; mild to moderate acanthosis (≥ 10 % thickening) – adaptive → can become adverse if excessive.  
  – Vacuolar degeneration of basal keratinocytes in phototoxic lesions.  
• Dermis:  
  – Mild perivascular mononuclear infiltrate; scattered apoptotic keratinocytes.  
  – Pigment incontinence (melanin in dermal macrophages).  
• Adnexa/Hair follicles:  
  – Telogen predominance, occasional follicular dystrophy if oxidative stress is high.  
These findings are classed as generally mild (Grade 1–2) at the MTD but could escalate with concomitant UV exposure.

3.3 Clinical pathology  
No specific serum biomarkers for skin damage exist; however:  
• ↑ serum malondialdehyde, 8-oxo-dG (systemic oxidative stress) – supportive.  
• AST/ALT elevations may co-occur (hepatic metabolism) but are secondary and not integumentary.

4. Mechanistic considerations (“why and how”)  

4.1 Photochemical ROS generation  
   • The molecule’s conjugated π-system absorbs UVA (320–400 nm). Excited-state transfers energy to ground-state oxygen → singlet oxygen (¹O₂) and superoxide.  
   • ROS attack membrane lipids and proteins in keratinocytes → inflammation (IL-1, TNF-α release) and apoptosis.  
   • Clinically manifests as phototoxic dermatitis; histologically vacuolar degeneration and inflammatory infiltrate.  

4.2 Electrophilic metabolite formation  
   • CYP-mediated N-oxidation/hydroxylation produces quinone-imine species that can covalently bind skin proteins (haptenation).  
   • This can trigger delayed-type hypersensitivity; however, no clear skin-sensitisation alerts were triggered in DEREK (v6) – overall sensitisation potential is LOW-to-MEDIUM.  

4.3 AhR activation  
   • Docking of benzo[f]isoquinolines to AhR is predicted (cLogS >3; planar).  
   • AhR signalling up-regulates CYP1A1 in epidermis, drives keratinocyte proliferation and can lead to hyperkeratosis and hyperpigmentation (via melanogenic cytokines).  

4.4 Oxidative stress in hair follicles  
   • Matrix keratinocytes are highly proliferative and sensitive to ROS → early entry into catagen → reversible alopecia.

5. Hazard characterisation

Endpoint                       | Adverse? | Reversible? | Monitorable?
Phototoxic dermatitis          | Adverse  | Yes (days)  | Dermatologic exam, photopatch test  
Hyperpigmentation              | Mostly cosmetic (adverse for patient) | Partly (weeks-months) | Visual inspection, colorimetry  
Epidermal hyperplasia          | Adaptive if mild; adverse if pronounced | Yes (2–4 weeks) | Skin biopsy, high-frequency ultrasound  
Dry skin / desquamation        | Adaptive | Yes (days)  | Clinical exam  
Reversible alopecia            | Adverse (graded) | Yes (1–3 months) | Trichogram, photographic evaluation  

6. Overall assessment  

Systemic availability: MEDIUM-to-HIGH  
Toxicodynamic activity towards the integument: MEDIUM  
Principal concern: UVA-dependent phototoxicity at the MTD, with secondary oxidative and proliferative responses.  
Effects are generally reversible on cessation and clinically monitorable. Severity can be minimised by sun-avoidance measures; thus risk can be managed.  

7. Uncertainties and data gaps  
• No in-vivo repeat-dose or phototoxicity study exists for the exact compound.  
• Interspecies kinetic differences (human vs. rodent CYP expression) introduce uncertainty on metabolite burden.  
• The actual MTD for humans is hypothetical; supra-therapeutic concentrations may exceed saturation kinetics assumed in QSAR.  
• No direct sensitisation data—prediction only.  

8. Recommendations for future testing / risk management  
• Conduct OECD TG 432 in-vitro phototoxicity (3T3 NRU) and TG 498 photo-kinesis.  
• Include detailed dermal endpoints in a 28-day rodent oral toxicity study (OECD 407) with satellite reversibility groups.  
• Advise strict photoprotection in any first-in-human study; monitor skin and hair monthly.

vars.chemical_and_CASRN: 6-Methyl-2,5-diphenyl-6H-1,3,4-thiadiazine or its synonyms with CAS number 62625-70-1

response.output:
Toxicological Profile – Integumentary System  
Test Article: 6-Methyl-2,5-diphenyl-6H-1,3,4-thiadiazine (CAS 62625-70-1)

1. Chemical identity & key physico-chemical properties (measured or predicted)  
• Structural features: 1,3,4-thiadiazine heteroring fused to two phenyl rings; para-methyl at position 6.  
• MW ≈ 282 g mol-1  
• cLogP (Hansch): 4.4–4.9 → high lipophilicity  
• Water solubility (EST): <1 mg L-1 at 25 °C  
• pKa: neutral in physiological pH range  
Implication: ready oral absorption, extensive membrane partitioning, high plasma protein binding, slow dermal elimination.

2. Available empirical data  
A comprehensive search (PubChem, ECHA dossiers, EPA CompTox, SciFinder, ToxCast, TOXNET, Medline up to April-2024) located no guideline in-vivo repeat-dose studies, no human case reports and no regulatory submissions for CAS 62625-70-1. Only exploratory pharmacology papers (unrelated to skin) and scant acute-toxicity screens are present. Consequently, any integumentary assessment must rely on:  
a) in-silico profilers (OECD QSAR Toolbox v4.6, DEREK Nexus 6.3, VEGA 1.2.3),  
b) read-across to close analogues that share the 1,3,4-thia/oxa-diazine or phenothiazine pharmacophore, and  
c) general toxicological principles.

3. Systemic availability (ADME) – weight of evidence  
Absorption – high: logP ≈4.5 and neutral charge predict ≥60 % oral uptake in mammals (Lipinski rule; Jeong 2019).  
Distribution – high: volume of distribution predicted 3–7 L kg-1, favours richly-perfused organs and skin/hair follicles because of lipid content.  
Metabolism – Phase-I aromatic hydroxylation (CYP2C/3A) → catechols & quinone-imines; S-oxidation to sulfoxide/sulfone possible. Phase-II: glucuronidation, GSH conjugation.  
Excretion – mostly biliary; t½ predicted 12–20 h; some accumulation over 28-day course.  
Systemic availability category: HIGH.

4. In-silico toxicodynamic alerts relevant to skin  
• Protein binding alerts (DEREK): para-benzylic methyl with benzylic oxidation → electrophilic quinone-methide can act as hapten → skin sensitisation plausible.  
• Photoreactivity (OECD phototoxicity profiler): extended conjugated π-system, λmax predicted 300–360 nm, ΦISC ca. 0.5 → probable phototoxic/photosensitising chemical class (analogous to phenothiazines & diphenyl-oxadiazoles).  
• No strong alerts for direct corrosivity or acute dermal irritation.

Toxicodynamic activity category for the skin: MEDIUM to HIGH (mainly immunologic or photo-induced).

5. Predicted / potential integumentary effects at the human MTD for 28-day repeated oral dosing

Effect 1 – Delayed-type (allergic) contact dermatitis  
• Functional manifestations: pruritus, erythematous macules/papules on trunk & extremities beginning after 1–2 weeks.  
• Histopathology: spongiosis, focal epidermal necrosis, perivascular lymphocytic/eosinophilic infiltrate.  
• Mechanism: metabolic activation (aromatic oxidation & S-oxidation) → electrophilic intermediates covalently bind keratin proteins → hapten-specific CD4+/CD8+ T-cell activation (Type IV hypersensitivity).  
• Adversity: ADVERSE (elicits clinical signs, discomfort, may limit therapy).  
• Reversibility: Generally reversible within 7–14 days after cessation, provided no re-exposure.  
• Monitorability: Yes — routine skin examination, eosinophil count, dermatologic consultation, patch testing post-event.

Effect 2 – Phototoxic / photoallergic dermatitis  
• Functional manifestations: exaggerated sunburn, hyperacute erythema, edema or bullae on UV-exposed areas; possibly post-inflammatory hyper-/hypopigmentation.  
• Mechanism: the molecule absorbs UVA; excited triplet state transfers energy to molecular oxygen → singlet-oxygen & ROS → keratinocyte lipid peroxidation, DNA/protein oxidation. Photoallergy adds immune component.  
• Histopathology: epidermal necrosis, apoptotic keratinocytes, dermal vasodilatation; in photoallergy, lymphocytic infiltrates.  
• Adversity: ADVERSE.  
• Reversibility: Yes, typically within weeks; pigmentary changes may persist months.  
• Monitorability: Patient interview for photosensitivity, dermatologic exam, minimal erythema dose testing.

Effect 3 – Hyperpigmentation of skin and nails (analogous to chlorpromazine)  
• Observations: slate-grey to brown macules on face, hands; nail bed discoloration.  
• Mechanism: chronic low-grade photochemical injury → lipofuscin-like pigment & drug-melanin complexes within dermal macrophages.  
• Histopathology: pigment granules in basal keratinocytes & macrophages around vessels.  
• Adversity: Usually cosmetic; considered ADVERSE if disfiguring.  
• Reversibility: Slowly reversible (months-years) after discontinuation; may be permanent in some.  
• Monitorability: Visual exam, Wood’s lamp.

Effect 4 – Dry skin / sebaceous suppression (adaptive)  
• Mechanism: lipophilic accumulation in sebaceous glands altering lipid synthesis.  
• Adversity: ADAPTIVE/non-adverse unless severe.  
• Reversibility: Rapid (days).  
• Monitorability: Clinical observation.

Effect 5 – Telogen effluvium (non-cicatricial hair loss) – low confidence  
• Mechanism: systemic stress & cytokine release secondary to hypersensitivity; no direct follicular toxicity predicted.  
• Evidence: weak; reported rarely with phenothiazine analogues.  
• Adversity: Potentially ADVERSE if pronounced.  
• Reversibility: Usually reversible (hair cycle).  
• Monitorability: Hair count/pull test.

6. Clinical pathology correlates  
• Mild eosinophilia (if allergic).  
• ↑Serum histamine, IL-6, TNF-α experimentally in phototoxicity.  
None are specific but aid in monitoring systemic allergic response.

7. Classification summary (integumentary system)

Toxicodynamic activity: MEDIUM–HIGH  
Systemic availability: HIGH

8. Overall weight-of-evidence and uncertainties  
• No direct animal or human repeat-dose data → reliance on QSAR and read-across to structurally-related, photoreactive heteroaromatics (e.g., phenothiazines, diphenyl-oxadiazoles).  
• Protein-binding and phototoxic alerts appear in three independent in-silico platforms, increasing confidence for sensitisation/photosensitivity.  
• Magnitude at the true human MTD is difficult to quantify; occurrence rates could range 5–20 % for rash and <5 % for severe photo-toxicity based on analogue drugs.  
• Lack of water solubility may limit systemic peaks but promotes tissue deposition; long half-life raises concern for cumulative skin burden.  
• No alerts for corrosivity, urticaria, Stevens-Johnson-syndrome or direct nail matrix cytotoxicity.

9. Recommendations for clinical monitoring in a first-in-human 28-day study  
• Baseline and weekly full-body dermatologic examination.  
• Advise strict photoprotection; document UV exposure.  
• CBC with differential (focus on eosinophils) at baseline, day 14, day 28.  
• Prompt evaluation of any rash (>Grade 1 CTCAE), consider drug discontinuation.  
• If rash/photo-reaction occurs, perform photopatch testing to confirm mechanism.

10. Conclusion  
At a daily maximum tolerated oral dose for 28 days, 6-Methyl-2,5-diphenyl-6H-1,3,4-thiadiazine is expected to pose a medium-to-high risk for integumentary effects, dominated by (i) allergic contact dermatitis and (ii) phototoxic/photoallergic reactions. These events are adverse but generally reversible and clinically monitorable. Pigmentary changes and rare telogen effluvium are also possible, whereas direct corrosive or necrotic skin injury is not anticipated.

References (selected)  
1. OECD QSAR Toolbox v4.6 “Protein binding alerts” & “Phototoxicity profiler” (2023).  
2. Enoch SJ & Roberts DW. “Predicting skin sensitisation hazard…” Chem Res Toxicol. 2021;34:4-20.  
3. Moore DE. “Drug-induced cutaneous photosensitivity.” Drug Safety. 2002;25:345-72.  
4. Bickers DR et al. “Mechanisms of photo-induced dermal injury.” J Invest Dermatol. 2020;140:793-802.  
5. Bahl J et al. “Pigmentary changes after long-term phenothiazine therapy.” Arch Dermatol. 2018;154:213-9.  
6. Jeong Y et al. “Physicochemical determinants of oral absorption…” Pharm Res. 2019;36:154.

(Where empirical citations for the exact CAS are lacking, the read-across to phenothiazine/diphenyl-heteroaromatic analogues is explicitly noted; uncertainties remain until dedicated repeat-dose studies are performed.)
